title: wrapper-mems architecture: git-based canonical JSONL store
content:
What: wrapper-mems will use a neutral canonical memory format stored in-repo at .wrapper-mems/memory.jsonl + index.json + schema.json. Append-only JSONL, ULID canonical_ids, soft-delete via tombstones, provider adapters map native IDs to canonical IDs via index.json.

Why: Team has two sub-teams using different memory tools (engram and claude-mem) on the same project. SoT must live in git (no remote backend in v1, scope = project only). Native-as-canonical was rejected because it locks the repo to one provider and contradicts the user's stated "easily replaceable" goal. File-per-observation was rejected because of repo clutter. JSONL solves both.

Where: prds/PRD-0-canonical-schema.md

Learned: Real semantic gap between engram (discrete typed observations) and claude-mem (session narrative summaries). Bridged by `kind` field (observation | session_summary | relation). PRD-0 leaves the ingest strategy for claude-mem narratives as a blocking open question (a/b/c options) — must be resolved before PRD-1 (adapter design).

title: Decision: claude-mem narratives stored as disjoint session_summary stream in v1
content:
What: claude-mem session narratives will be stored in the canonical store as kind=session_summary records — a parallel stream that engram-side users can browse but that engram does not treat as native observations. Option (c) — deriving discrete observations from narratives via extraction — is explicitly deferred to v2.

Why: User picked option (a) over (b/c). Extraction pipelines (LLM or heuristic) are a project in themselves and would dilute v1 scope. v1 goal is a working broker, not unified semantic memory. Convergence between the two streams can come later once the wrapper proves valuable.

Where: prds/PRD-0-canonical-schema.md §10.1 and §11. PRD-1 (adapter contract / engram adapter) is now unblocked.

Learned: The semantic gap between engram (atomic typed observations) and claude-mem (compressed session narratives) is real and irreducible without LLM extraction. Honesty about that gap in v1 is better than fake convergence.

title: Decision: wrapper-mems coexists with engram sync (option 3 mirror)
content:
What: wrapper-mems will NOT replace `engram sync`. Both `.engram/` (engram-native chunks) and `.wrapper-mems/memory.jsonl` (neutral canonical) coexist in the repo. The engram adapter mirrors engram-origin content into the canonical JSONL. engram-only users may continue using `engram sync` standalone (they will miss claude-mem content but their existing workflow stays intact). claude-mem users go through wrapper-mems. Power users use wrapper-mems which orchestrates both.

Why: Discovered mid-design that engram already ships `engram sync` and an HTTP API (engram serve on :7437). Forcing the engram sub-team to abandon `engram sync` for a v1 tool would add fricción and provide no immediate value to them. Option 3 (mirror) preserves the replaceability property (JSONL outlives engram) without breaking existing workflow.

Where: prds/PRD-0-canonical-schema.md §4 and §10.6.

Learned: Engram exposes HTTP API (`engram serve`), CLI (`engram save/search/export/import/sync`), MCP, and TUI. For a Go binary, CLI subprocess or HTTP API are both viable transports. index.json must NOT be committed because engram-native IDs are per-user-installation.

title: PRD-1 written: adapter contract + engram adapter (Go, CLI subprocess)
content:
What: Drafted PRD-1 defining the Go `Adapter` interface (Name/Health/ListNative/ReadNative/ToCanonical/FromCanonical/WriteNative + IDMap) and the engram reference implementation that shells out to the engram CLI. Pure-function conversion methods; side effects only in List/Read/Write. Personal-scope observations filtered at adapter boundary. Mirror with `engram sync` is opt-in via --mirror-engram flag.

Why: Go was the chosen language; engram is the most structured v1 provider so it validates the contract first. CLI subprocess preferred over HTTP API for v1 (no daemon to manage; `engram serve` upgrade tracked for v2 via flag).

Where: prds/PRD-1-adapter-contract-engram.md

Learned: engram CLI v1.15 doesn't expose `engram get <sync_id>` for single-record read — likely need HTTP API fallback for that one op. CLI also doesn't accept tags on save, so tags are lost on canonical→engram in v1. Engram relations (supersedes/conflicts_with/etc) are NOT round-tripped in v1 — deferred to v1.1. PRD-0 §10.4 resolved: index.json is gitignored because engram-native sync_ids are per-user.

title: PRD-2 written: claude-mem adapter (HTTP, read-only v1)
content:
What: Drafted PRD-2. claude-mem adapter implements the PRD-1 Adapter interface in read-only mode for v1. Transport is HTTP against the running worker (default :37701, port discoverable via ~/.claude-mem/supervisor.json). All claude-mem records enter canonical as kind=session_summary (never observation), preserving disjoint streams per PRD-0 §10.1. WriteNative returns ErrUnsupported in v1; FromCanonical is implemented as pure function for round-trip validation and future v1.1 use.

Why: claude-mem v13.2.0 CLI has no save/add command (capture is passive via transcript watch). CLI search rejects empty queries so cannot enumerate — HTTP API on :37701 is the only practical read surface. server export/import are marked "not yet implemented" upstream. Write surface (POST /api/observations or equivalent) not investigated yet — deferred to v1.1.

Where: prds/PRD-2-claude-mem-adapter.md

Learned: claude-mem stores data in ~/.claude-mem/ (SQLite + Chroma vector DB + corpora + logs). HTTP endpoints confirmed: GET /api/observations (paginated items/hasMore/offset/limit), GET /api/projects, GET /api/search (needs params), GET /health. CRITICAL: actual JSON shape of an observation item is TO-CONFIRM (test install was empty); adapter must dump first real sample before shipping. claude-mem identifies projects by filesystem path while wrapper-mems uses its own project names — need explicit mapping config. One claude-mem worker holds ALL of a developer's projects, so adapter must filter strictly to avoid cross-project leakage.

title: PRD-3 written: sync engine with append-only writes, deterministic collision tiebreaker
content:
What: Drafted PRD-3. CLI surface: `wrapper-mems init|sync [pull|push]|status|show|sync resolve`. Default `sync` does pull-then-push, no git actions. Per-provider watermarks in index.json (last_pushed_at, last_pulled_at). Append-only writes; revision collisions resolved by greater updated_at then greater canonical_id lex; losers kept in jsonl but recorded in index.json.conflicts for TUI. Mirror with engram sync is opt-in via --mirror-engram. Failure of one provider warns and continues; doesn't abort.

Why: Append-only avoids most git merge pain on memory.jsonl. Deterministic tiebreaker means two machines independently rebuilding index.json from the same jsonl always agree on the winner. Not auto-committing keeps wrapper-mems out of the git surprise-action territory. Read-only claude-mem (PRD-2 decision) means pull skips it; only engram receives canonical→provider writes in v1.

Where: prds/PRD-3-sync-engine.md

Learned: Concurrency on a single machine (manual run + cron) needs a lockfile at .wrapper-mems/.lock. Deletes are NOT propagated into providers in v1 (rare case, --prune flag deferred to v1.1). Mirror needs `engram sync --import` BEFORE the canonical→engram pull stage to avoid writing the same logical record twice via two paths.

title: PRD-4 written: TUI dashboard (bubbletea, read-mostly, alma A)
content:
What: Drafted PRD-4. TUI built with bubbletea + bubbles + lipgloss + glamour (for markdown). Four tabs: Observations (filterable list + detail pane), Conflicts (winner/loser comparison + resolution keys), Status (provider health, watermarks, sync shortcuts), Help. The TUI writes NOTHING on its own; all mutations go through wrapper-mems CLI subprocess so the two paths cannot drift. Closed-by-default soul A (dashboard, no polling, no daemon).

Why: Soul A confirmed by user. Reusing the CLI as the only write path (TUI shells out) guarantees consistency and shrinks the TUI to a read-mostly visualization layer. Subprocess approach keeps the TUI codebase small and the sync semantics single-sourced in the engine.

Where: prds/PRD-4-tui.md

Learned: TUI must lazy-parse memory.jsonl (only fully parse content body when row is selected) to hit the 100k-line in <100ms target. Conflict tab is hidden when empty. Default `wrapper-mems` with no args probably opens the TUI when initialized — friendlier than --help; if not initialized, print init instructions. Markdown rendering via glamour adds binary size but worth it for readable details.

title: PRD-5 written + v1 scope frozen (config, identity, install, privacy)
content:
What: Drafted PRD-5 and froze v1 scope. Config in two layers: .wrapper-mems/config.yaml (project, committed) + ~/.config/wrapper-mems/config.yaml (user, never committed); user overrides project field-by-field. Env vars override both. `wrapper-mems init` creates dir, schema, config, and adds .wrapper-mems/index.json + .lock to .gitignore. claude-mem session opt-out via privacy.excluded_session_ids (no native scope mechanism in claude-mem). `wrapper-mems doctor` is the single ground-truth health command with --fix for safe repairs. Distribution: GitHub Releases binaries + `go install` in v1.0; Homebrew tap deferred to v1.1. No telemetry, ever.

Why: Two-layer config maps to real team dynamics (shared defaults + personal overrides). claude-mem privacy gap forced an excluded_session_ids list because claude-mem has no scope mechanism. Doctor command consolidates all health checks so users have one place to verify setup. Telemetry policy stated explicitly to remove ambiguity.

Where: prds/PRD-5-config-identity-install.md, §12 contains the v1 implementation order.

Learned: claude-mem session opt-out is reactive (must know the session ID). v1.1 should add `wrapper-mems exclude <last>` for ergonomics. Single binary in v1 (all adapters compiled in); plugin model deferred until a third adapter is requested. Implementation order: skeleton I/O → engram adapter → claude-mem adapter (with mapping reconfirmation as first action) → sync engine → CLI → TUI → distribution.

title: Session summary: wrapper-mems
content:
## Goal
Diseñar la arquitectura completa de `wrapper-mems`, un broker de memoria entre proveedores de IA (engram, claude-mem) para un equipo donde sub-equipos usan herramientas distintas sobre el mismo proyecto. Output esperado: set de PRDs cerrados que congelen el scope de v1.

## Instructions
- Usuario prefiere voseo rioplatense, tono mentor-directo, decisiones honestas con tradeoffs.
- Una pregunta por turno, parar después.
- No inventar superficies de tools sin verificar — investigar antes de tipear contratos.
- Markdown para PRDs en `prds/`.

## Discoveries
- Engram CLI v1.15.10 expone `serve` (HTTP :7437), `save`, `search`, `export`, `import`, `sync` (chunks comprimidos a `.engram/` en repo), `obsidian-export`. CLI subprocess es viable transport.
- Engram CLI v1.15 NO expone `get <sync_id>` directo — single-record read necesita HTTP API fallback.
- Engram CLI no acepta tags array en `save` — tags se pierden en canonical→engram (v1).
- claude-mem v13.2.0: storage en `~/.claude-mem/` (SQLite + Chroma + corpora + logs). Worker corre en :37701 (default).
- claude-mem CLI NO tiene `save`/`add` — captura es pasiva vía `transcript watch`. `server export`/`import` marcados "not yet implemented" upstream.
- claude-mem CLI `search ""` rechaza queries vacíos → NO enumera. Adapter debe ir por HTTP API.
- claude-mem HTTP endpoints confirmados: `GET /api/observations?limit=N&offset=N` (paginated), `GET /api/projects`, `GET /api/search` (needs params), `GET /health`. Shape exacta de un item NO confirmada (test install vacío).
- Engram-native sync_ids son per-user-installation → `index.json` (mappings provider→canonical) NO puede ir en git.

## Accomplished
- **PRD-0 — Canonical Memory Schema**: `.wrapper-mems/{memory.jsonl, index.json, schema.json}`. JSONL append-only, ULIDs, soft-delete via tombstones, schema versioning. Coexiste con `.engram/`. `kind` field: observation | session_summary | relation.
- **PRD-1 — Adapter contract + engram adapter**: interfaz Go con 7 métodos (`Name`, `Health`, `ListNative`, `ReadNative`, `WriteNative`, `ToCanonical`, `FromCanonical`); pure-function conversions, side effects solo en List/Read/Write. Engram adapter usa CLI subprocess. Personal scope filtrado en `ListNative`. Mirror con `engram sync` opt-in (`--mirror-engram`). Engram relations NO se round-trippean en v1.
- **PRD-2 — claude-mem adapter (read-only v1)**: HTTP transport (:37701), todos los records como `kind: session_summary`. `WriteNative` devuelve `ErrUnsupported`. Port discovery vía `~/.claude-mem/supervisor.json`. Bidirectional deferred a v1.1.
- **PRD-3 — Sync engine**: CLI `init|sync [pull|push]|status|show|sync resolve`. Watermarks per-provider en `index.json.sync_state`. Tiebreaker determinístico para revision collisions (mayor `updated_at`, empate→mayor `canonical_id` lex). Loser queda en JSONL, registrado en `index.json.conflicts` para TUI. Failure de provider = warn+skip. NO auto-commit a git (opt-in `--commit`). Lockfile `.wrapper-mems/.lock` para concurrencia local.
- **PRD-4 — TUI (alma A, dashboard read-mostly)**: bubbletea + bubbles + lipgloss + glamour. Cuatro tabs (Observations, Conflicts, Status, Help). TUI escribe NADA — todas las mutaciones shelean `wrapper-mems sync`. Default `wrapper-mems` sin args abre TUI si está inicializado.
- **PRD-5 — Config / identity / install / privacy**: dos layers de config (project committed + user override), env vars on top. `wrapper-mems init` crea dir + actualiza `.gitignore`. claude-mem privacy gap mitigada con `privacy.excluded_session_ids`. `wrapper-mems doctor` health command + `--fix`. Distribución GitHub Releases en v1.0; brew tap diferido a v1.1. **Cero telemetría, ever.**
- **v1 scope congelado** en PRD-5 §12 con orden de implementación.

## Next Steps
- Usuario relee los 5 PRDs tranquilo, anota correcciones.
- Próxima sesión: aplicar correcciones (si hay) y arrancar bootstrap del repo Go.
- **Primera acción técnica al codear**: dumpear el JSON real de `/api/observations` de claude-mem con una sesión real cargada, y reconfirmar el mapping de PRD-2 §5-§6 (marcado como TO-CONFIRM).
- Orden de implementación (PRD-5 §12): skeleton I/O → engram adapter → claude-mem adapter → sync engine → CLI → TUI → distribución.

## Relevant Files
- `prds/PRD-0-canonical-schema.md` — schema canónico, layout en disco, IDs, append-only
- `prds/PRD-1-adapter-contract-engram.md` — interfaz Go + engram adapter
- `prds/PRD-2-claude-mem-adapter.md` — claude-mem adapter (HTTP, read-only)
- `prds/PRD-3-sync-engine.md` — orquestación, collisions, watermarks
- `prds/PRD-4-tui.md` — bubbletea dashboard
- `prds/PRD-5-config-identity-install.md` — config, identity, install, privacy, doctor + v1 scope freeze

title: PRD-0 written: canonical memory schema (JSONL + ULID + append-only)
content:
**What**: PRD-0 defines the canonical memory schema for wrapper-mems. Storage: `.wrapper-mems/memory.jsonl` (append-only, one JSON object per line, committed) + `index.json` (local cache, gitignored) + `schema.json` (version + provider registry). Each observation has: canonical_id (ULID), schema_version, kind (observation|session_summary|relation), revision, supersedes, deleted (tombstone), title, type, topic_key, tags, content, content_format, origin {provider, provider_id, author, session_id}, created_at, updated_at. Coexists with `.engram/` (engram sync remains valid standalone). Mutation: create=revision 1; update=append new line same canonical_id revision N+1 supersedes=prior; delete=tombstone line with deleted:true. index.json rebuildable from JSONL. schema_version bumps are breaking.

**Why**: Need a provider-agnostic, lossless, diff-friendly, compact, versioned format that lets engram + claude-mem round-trip without losing user-visible info. JSONL chosen over markdown-per-observation to avoid file explosion. ULID for stable, sortable, provider-independent IDs.

**Where**: prds/PRD-0-canonical-schema.md

**Learned**: Open questions resolved in PRD-0: (1) claude-mem narratives enter as kind=session_summary parallel stream (no LLM extraction in v1); (4) index.json NOT committed (per-user engram IDs); (6) coexistence with engram sync. Still open: topic_key cross-provider mapping, merge conflict tiebreak (proposed updated_at + ULID), `local_only` privacy flag, personal scope filtering at adapter boundary.

title: SDD init: wrapper-mems project context
content:
## Project Context
- **Name**: wrapper-mems
- **Stack**: Go (CLI + TUI with bubbletea)
- **Status**: Greenfield — no code yet, only PRDs (0-5) defining v1 scope

## Testing Capabilities

- **coverage_command**: `go test -cover ./...`

## SDD Configuration (this session)

## Conventions
- Conventional commits, no AI attribution
- Use rg/fd/bat/sd/eza instead of grep/find/cat/sed/ls
- Code/identifiers/comments in English; user-facing replies in Rioplatense Spanish

## PRDs (frozen scope)

## Why

title: sdd/canonical-memory-schema/explore
content:
## Exploration: PRD-0 — Canonical Memory Schema (Storage Layer)

### Current State

Greenfield Go project. No code exists. PRD-0 freezes the on-disk contract:
- `.wrapper-mems/memory.jsonl` — append-only JSONL, one canonical observation per line
- `.wrapper-mems/index.json` — gitignored local cache, canonical_id ↔ provider-native-id maps + latest_line offsets
- `.wrapper-mems/schema.json` — schema_version registry, committed

PRD-1 already specifies the `CanonicalRecord` Go struct and the `Adapter` interface. The storage layer's job is to own the JSONL file, the index, and all mutation primitives that adapters call through.

---

### 1. ULID Library Choice

**Candidates**

| Library | Thread-safe Make? | Monotonic? | Allocation | Module path |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `oklog/ulid/v2` `Make()` | YES (sync.Pool) | YES (DefaultEntropy, process-global) | ~0 alloc/call | `github.com/oklog/ulid/v2` |
| `oklog/ulid/v2` `LockedMonotonicReader` | YES (sync.Mutex) | YES, strict within-ms | 1 mutex alloc at init | same |
| `oklog/ulid/v2` `MonotonicEntropy` | NO | YES | cheap | same — single-goroutine only |
| `oklog/ulid` (v1, no module) | Depends on entropy | Manual | — | legacy, no Go modules |
| `go.rtnl.ai/ulid` (fork) | — | — | — | thin fork, negligible adoption |

**Recommendation**: `github.com/oklog/ulid/v2` with `ulid.Make()` for all canonical_id generation at ingest time.

Rationale:
- `Make()` is the zero-ceremony path: thread-safe, uses a process-global DefaultEntropy backed by crypto/rand with sync.Pool, monotonically increasing within a millisecond. No setup code, no manual entropy source.
- wrapper-mems will not be a high-throughput ID factory (one ULID per observation ingest, not per request). `Make()` allocation cost is irrelevant.
- Strict monotonic (LockedMonotonicReader) is only useful for databases that need total ordering within the same millisecond. We do not need that; JSONL append order IS the ordering.
- v1 (`oklog/ulid` no module path) is legacy; do not use.

---

### 2. Append-Only JSONL Writer

**Concurrency model decision (critical)**

PRD-0 §3 explicitly states "no real-time multi-writer concurrency — git is the merge boundary." In practice:
- In v1 there is ONE active writer process per repo at a time (the `wrapper-mems sync` CLI invocation).
- The CLI + a background sync daemon can coexist in v2, but that's out of scope.

This means: **single-writer per process, no concurrent file writes within a single sync run**.

**fsync strategy options**

| Strategy | Durability | Performance | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| fsync per line | Highest | Slow (1 disk flush per observation) | Low |
| fsync per batch (end of sync) | Adequate | Fast | Low |
| fsync never (OS flush) | Crash risk | Fastest | Trivial |
| WAL-style two-phase | Overkill | — | High |

**Recommendation**: **fsync per batch** — call `f.Sync()` once after flushing all lines in a sync run, before closing the file.

Rationale:
- If the process crashes mid-write, the worst case is a partial last line. The partial line is not valid JSON. On next startup, the reader detects a truncated last line (non-terminated JSON) and truncates the file to the last valid newline before rebuilding the index. This is the canonical "crash recovery via truncation" pattern for append-only logs.
- fsync per line is needlessly slow for batch import scenarios (e.g. importing 500 engram observations).
- WAL is overkill: we have `git reset --hard` as our recovery story if data corruption happens.

**File opening pattern**

```go
f, err := os.OpenFile(path, os.O_APPEND|os.O_CREATE|os.O_WRONLY, 0644)
```

Use a `bufio.Writer` to buffer individual JSON encodings; call `Flush()` + `f.Sync()` at end of batch.

**Cross-process locking**

v1 assumption is single writer; no cross-process lock needed. For defensive coding, use `gofrs/flock` advisory lock (`.wrapper-mems/memory.jsonl.lock`) as a non-blocking TryLock at the start of a sync run — if it fails, print a "another wrapper-mems process is running" error and exit. This costs nothing in the happy path and prevents confusion if a user accidentally runs two syncs.

**Partial write / crash recovery** (startup reader)

```
On open:
1. Seek to end of file.
2. Read backward until last '\n'.
3. If last byte != '\n', truncate file to last '\n' position.
4. Rebuild index from full scan.
```

This is a single-pass O(file size) repair. Acceptable because partial writes are rare.

---

### 3. Index.json Rebuild Strategy

**Approaches**

| Strategy | Startup cost | Memory | Incremental complexity | Correctness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Full rebuild every startup | O(N lines) | Low (streaming) | None | Perfect |
| Checksum-validated cache | O(1) if cache valid | Low | Low-Medium | High |
| Append-incremental (track file offset) | O(new lines only) | Low | Medium | High if offset tracked correctly |
| In-memory only (no file) | O(N lines) every run | Low | None | Perfect but no persistence |

**Scale context**: PRD-0 mentions 10k–100k observations. At ~300 bytes/line average, that's 3MB–30MB of JSONL. A full sequential scan at 500MB/s disk speed takes 6ms–60ms. That is perfectly acceptable startup latency for a CLI tool.

**Recommendation**: **Checksum-validated full rebuild**.

Approach:
1. `index.json` stores a `source_checksum` field = `sha256(first 16 bytes of memory.jsonl + file size)` (a fast fingerprint, not a full hash).
2. On startup: compute the fingerprint of the current `memory.jsonl`. If it matches `index.json.source_checksum`, trust the cache. If not, do a full streaming rebuild.
3. Rebuild is a single O(N) scan with a `bufio.Scanner` — keep only the last seen entry per `canonical_id` in a `map[string]IndexEntry`.
4. Write the new `index.json` atomically: write to `.index.json.tmp`, then `os.Rename()`.

Why checksum over pure incremental:
- Incremental requires tracking last processed byte offset. If the file is truncated (crash recovery), the offset is stale and the index will be wrong. A checksum detects this.
- Atomic write via rename prevents a corrupted index.json if the process dies mid-write.
- Full hash of the file is too expensive at 30MB. The fingerprint (size + first 16 bytes of header) is not cryptographic but is sufficient for cache invalidation — not security.

---

### 4. Schema Versioning

**Per-record schema_ver**: every JSONL line carries `"schema_version": 1`. The reader checks this field before deserializing.

**Read-path approach**: define a `RawRecord` minimal struct for version detection:
```go
type RawRecord struct {
    SchemaVersion int             `json:"schema_version"`
    Kind          string          `json:"kind"`
    Raw           json.RawMessage // retain unparsed bytes
}
```

On read: unmarshal into `RawRecord`, switch on `SchemaVersion`, then unmarshal `Raw` into the versioned struct. This pattern (version-gated two-pass decode) is idiomatic Go and keeps migration logic localized.

**Forward compatibility rule**: unknown `schema_version` values — the reader MUST skip the record and log a warning (not error), so that a team member with a newer wrapper-mems doesn't corrupt another's data by refusing to read the file.

**schema.json check**: on startup, if `schema.json.schema_version > StoreSupportedVersion`, refuse to operate with a clear error message. This is the hard gate for breaking schema changes. Defined in PRD-0 §9.

**Migration story**: schema migrations are applied by writing a new-format version of the observation as an append (new revision). There is NO in-place rewrite of historical records — that would break the append-only invariant and produce a messy git diff. The compaction tool (PRD-0 §8, future) is the right moment for full migration rewrites.

---

### 5. Tombstone Semantics

**PRD-0 is unambiguous** (§8): delete = append a tombstone line. No in-place modification.

Tombstone record shape:
```json
{
  "canonical_id": "01HXY...",
  "schema_version": 1,
  "kind": "observation",
  "revision": 3,
  "supersedes": "01HXY...",
  "deleted": true,
  "title": "",
  "content": "",
  ...
}
```

**Read-path filtering**: the index tracks `deleted: true`. The canonical reader exposes two modes:
- `ReadLive(id)` — returns error if `deleted: true` in index
- `ReadAll(id)` — returns all revisions including tombstones (needed for sync audit)

The index already stores `deleted bool` per `canonical_id` (PRD-0 §6 schema). The live read is therefore O(1) — check index, skip if deleted, seek to `latest_line` offset.

**Open question confirmed by exploration**: PRD-0 says `supersedes` stores "canonical_id of prior revision". For tombstones this means `supersedes = canonical_id` of the last live revision, NOT the observation's own `canonical_id`. The propose phase should clarify this in the data model to avoid confusion (self-referential vs. prior-revision-id).

---

### 6. Revision Conflicts from Concurrent Writers

**Scenario**: wrapper-mems CLI running on machine A and a future background sync daemon (v2) on machine B both append a revision for `canonical_id = X` at the same time. After a git merge, both lines exist. `index.json.latest_revision` will conflict in git (PRD-0 §10.3 open question).

**v1 mitigation** (single writer per machine, git as merge boundary):
- No two processes write to the SAME `memory.jsonl` concurrently. Concurrent writes from different machines happen in different working trees and are merged via git.
- After a merge, `memory.jsonl` is append-only so it never git-conflicts (new lines from both sides are preserved).
- `index.json` IS gitignored and is NOT subject to git conflicts.
- The index is rebuilt locally from the merged `memory.jsonl` after `git pull`.

**Conflict on revision numbering**: after a merge, two lines may have `revision: 2` for the same `canonical_id`. The reader must handle this by taking the LATEST by `updated_at` and breaking ties by ULID lexicographic order (higher ULID = later). This is the tiebreak rule from PRD-0 §10.3 — not yet decided there, but our exploration can propose it.

**Recommendation for propose phase**: formalize the "last-writer-wins by `updated_at` + ULID tiebreak" rule as the conflict resolution strategy for revision numbering post-merge. The index rebuild pass applies this rule to determine `latest_revision`.

---

### Affected Areas (Files to Create)

```
internal/store/
  store.go          — Store struct, public API (Append, ReadLive, ReadAll, Rebuild)
  index.go          — IndexEntry, index read/write, checksum validation
  record.go         — CanonicalRecord, RawRecord, version-gated decode
  ulid.go           — thin wrapper around ulid.Make() for testability
  tombstone.go      — tombstone construction helpers
  lock.go           — advisory flock wrapper
```

These are internal-only. No exported API surface until PRD-1's adapter layer calls through.

---

### Open Questions for Propose Phase

1. **Tombstone `supersedes` field**: should it point to the canonical_id of the last live revision, or to the observation's own canonical_id? Clarify to avoid ambiguity in the index builder.
2. **Conflict tiebreak rule**: confirm "last-writer-wins by `updated_at` + ULID lex order" for post-merge index rebuild.
3. **Index fingerprint algorithm**: SHA-256 first 16 bytes + size is simple but theoretically wrong for files of identical size and identical first 16 bytes. Should we use full SHA-256 of the file (adds 6–60ms) or a better partial hash? Consider XXH64 via `github.com/cespare/xxhash` for speed.
4. **advisory lock on index.json vs. memory.jsonl**: lock on which file? Locking the append target (memory.jsonl) is more semantically correct; locking a separate .lock file avoids any concern about locking an append-target fd.
5. **bufio.Writer buffer size**: default 4096 bytes. For bulk import (500 records × ~300 bytes = 150KB), a larger buffer (e.g. 64KB) would reduce syscall count. Benchmark before deciding.

---

### Risks

- **Partial write detection**: if a JSONL line is split across a 4KB disk sector boundary and the process crashes, we may have partially written JSON. The truncation-at-last-newline strategy handles this but must be tested explicitly.
- **index.json fingerprint collision**: edge case where different file states produce the same fingerprint (size + first 16 bytes). This causes a stale index to be served silently. Mitigation: add a `last_line_count` field to the index and cross-check. Low probability but worth tracking.
- **flock advisory nature**: `gofrs/flock` advisory lock is not enforced by the kernel if another process ignores it. In practice, both processes must use the same lock convention. Document this in the store package.
- **revision collision post-merge**: if the tiebreak rule is not applied consistently by all clients, two machines may disagree on which revision is "latest" after a merge. Must be deterministic and documented.
- **`encoding/json` performance**: for 100k observations, the streaming decode with `bufio.Scanner` + `json.Unmarshal` is adequate. If profiling shows it's a bottleneck, consider `github.com/json-iterator/go` or Go 1.25's json/v2 package. Not a v1 concern.

---

### Recommendation

Build the storage layer as `internal/store` with these concrete choices:
- ULID: `github.com/oklog/ulid/v2`, `ulid.Make()`
- Writer: `os.O_APPEND`, `bufio.Writer`, fsync-per-batch, `gofrs/flock` advisory lock
- Index: checksum-validated full rebuild (size + first 16 bytes fingerprint), atomic rename write
- Schema versioning: two-pass decode (`RawRecord` → versioned struct), skip unknown versions
- Tombstones: pure append, tracked in index as `deleted: true`, two read modes (Live/All)
- Conflict resolution: `updated_at` tiebreak + ULID lex order, applied in index rebuild

### Ready for Proposal

YES — all PRD-0 constraints are clear and the open questions above are bounded scope items, not blockers. The propose phase can lock the package API surface and the open questions into spec decisions.

title: sdd/canonical-memory-schema/proposal
content:
# Proposal: canonical-memory-schema — PRD-0 Storage Layer

## 1. Intent

**Problem**: wrapper-mems has a frozen on-disk contract (PRD-0) but zero code. Adapters (PRD-1), sync (PRD-3), TUI (PRD-4), config (PRD-5), and CLI all depend on a working storage primitive that owns `memory.jsonl`, `index.json`, and `schema.json`. Without it, nothing else can be built or tested.

**Why now**: Storage is the bottom of the dependency stack. Every other PRD calls into it. Implementing it first unblocks parallel work on adapters and CLI.

**Success looks like**: a `internal/store` package whose public API lets a caller append a `CanonicalRecord`, read the latest live revision of any `canonical_id`, read full revision history, rebuild the index from disk, and detect+recover from a crash-truncated last line — all with unit tests, no adapter dependencies, and zero external services.

## 2. Scope

### IN scope
- `internal/store/` Go package (no exported API beyond the package boundary in v1).
- ULID generation via `github.com/oklog/ulid/v2` (`ulid.Make()`).
- Append-only JSONL writer: `os.O_APPEND|O_CREATE|O_WRONLY`, `bufio.Writer`, fsync-per-batch.
- Advisory file lock via `github.com/gofrs/flock` (TryLock, non-blocking, sidecar `.lock` file).
- `index.json` bootstrap, atomic write (tmp + rename), checksum-validated cache, full rebuild.
- `schema.json` bootstrap on first run; reject newer-than-supported `schema_version`.
- Tombstones (append-only soft-delete, `deleted: true`).
- Revisions (`revision N+1` + `supersedes` pointer).
- Crash recovery: truncate trailing partial line at startup.
- Version-gated two-pass JSON decode (`RawRecord` → versioned struct); skip unknown `schema_version` with warning.
- Post-merge conflict resolution rule in the index rebuild path.
- A minimal internal `debug-store` CLI subcommand (write/read/rebuild) for manual smoke testing — NOT a user-facing feature.
- Unit tests covering: append, read live/all, tombstone hiding, rebuild parity, partial-line recovery, lock contention, fingerprint invalidation, schema-version skip.

### OUT of scope
- Adapter interface and implementations (PRD-1: engram, claude-mem).
- Sync orchestration, `engram serve` HTTP calls, claude-mem narrative ingest (PRD-3).
- TUI / bubbletea (PRD-4).
- Config loading, `~/.config/wrapper-mems` (PRD-5).
- User-facing CLI surface (`wrapper-mems sync`, `wrapper-mems list`, etc.).
- Compaction tool (PRD-0 §8 future work).
- Personal-scope filtering, `local_only` privacy flag.
- Multi-writer concurrency / WAL.
- Performance optimization (json-iterator, json/v2). Default `encoding/json` is sufficient for v1.

## 3. Decisions on Open Questions

### Q1. Tombstone `supersedes` semantics
**Decision**: `supersedes` on a tombstone points to the **canonical_id of the prior revision**, which is identical to the observation's own `canonical_id` (since the same canonical_id is shared across all revisions of an observation). The semantic is "this line supersedes the chain identified by canonical_id X up through revision N-1". For tombstones specifically, `supersedes` MUST equal `canonical_id`. This will be enforced by a validator in `record.go`.

Rationale: keeps `supersedes` unambiguous — it always names the canonical_id whose chain is being mutated. The `revision` field already disambiguates within the chain.

### Q2. Conflict tiebreak rule (formal)
**Decision**: For two JSONL lines with the same `(canonical_id, revision)` (possible after a git merge across machines):

```
winner = argmax over candidates of (updated_at, canonical_id_as_appended_ulid)
```

i.e. compare `updated_at` (RFC3339 nanosecond precision); on tie, compare a per-line ULID stored in a new field `line_ulid` (lex-ordered, higher wins). `line_ulid` is generated at append time and is distinct from `canonical_id` — it identifies the LINE, not the observation. This guarantees a total order even when two machines write at the exact same wall-clock nanosecond.

Applied deterministically in the index rebuild pass. All clients reach the same winner.

### Q3. Fingerprint algorithm
**Decision**: `index.json.source_fingerprint` = hex(`xxh64(file_size_u64_le || first_4KB_of_file || last_4KB_of_file)`) using `github.com/cespare/xxhash/v2`.

Rationale:
- Pure size + first-16-bytes (exploration's original suggestion) is too weak — collision-prone for files that grow by appends with identical headers.
- Full SHA-256 of 30MB is fine (~60ms) but wasted work.
- xxh64 of size + head + tail is collision-resistant in practice for the failure modes we care about (file truncation, file appended-to, file replaced). xxhash is non-cryptographic but we are NOT defending against adversaries — only detecting cache staleness.
- Also store `last_line_count` as a secondary validator. Mismatch → rebuild.

### Q4. Lock target
**Decision**: Sidecar lock file: `.wrapper-mems/.lock`.

Rationale:
- Single lock guards the entire store directory, not just one file (the index and schema may also be written).
- Avoids any semantic concern about locking an append-target fd.
- Standard `gofrs/flock` pattern; no surprises across macOS/Linux.

### Q5. bufio.Writer buffer size
**Decision**: 64 KB (`bufio.NewWriterSize(f, 64<<10)`).

Rationale: bulk imports (e.g. mirroring 500 engram observations at ~300B each = 150KB) issue 3 syscalls instead of 38. No measurable cost on small writes. 64KB is the conventional sweet spot for log writers.

## 4. Libraries and Versions

| Library | Version | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `github.com/oklog/ulid/v2` | latest v2.x | canonical_id and line_ulid generation |
| `github.com/gofrs/flock` | latest v0.x | advisory cross-process lock |
| `github.com/cespare/xxhash/v2` | latest v2.x | fast index fingerprint |
| stdlib `encoding/json` | go1.22+ | JSON encode/decode |
| stdlib `bufio`, `os`, `io` | — | file I/O |

Test-only:
| `github.com/stretchr/testify` | latest | assertions, require.NoError ergonomics |

No other deps. The package is intentionally lean.

## 5. Approach Outline

### File layout

```
internal/store/
  store.go        — Store struct, constructor Open(rootDir), public API
  record.go       — CanonicalRecord, RawRecord, version-gated decode, validators
  index.go        — IndexEntry, index read/write, fingerprint, atomic rename
  rebuild.go      — full streaming rebuild from JSONL, conflict tiebreak
  ulid.go         — wrapper around ulid.Make() for test injection
  tombstone.go    — tombstone constructor + validation
  lock.go         — flock wrapper, TryLock + clear "another process is running" error
  schema.go       — schema.json bootstrap + supported-version gate
  recover.go      — startup truncation of partial trailing line
```

### Public API surface (package `store`)

```go
type Store struct { /* unexported */ }

// Open initializes the store at rootDir (creates files/dirs on first run),
// acquires the advisory lock, runs crash recovery, validates the index.
func Open(rootDir string) (*Store, error)
func (s *Store) Close() error  // releases lock, flushes buffers, fsyncs

// Append appends a single record. Generates canonical_id if zero. Generates
// line_ulid. Validates schema. Returns the appended record (with IDs filled).
func (s *Store) Append(r CanonicalRecord) (CanonicalRecord, error)

// AppendBatch appends N records atomically-ish (single fsync at end).
func (s *Store) AppendBatch(rs []CanonicalRecord) ([]CanonicalRecord, error)

// ReadLive returns the latest non-deleted revision of canonical_id.
// Returns ErrNotFound if absent. Returns ErrDeleted if tombstoned.
func (s *Store) ReadLive(canonicalID string) (CanonicalRecord, error)

// ReadAll returns every revision (including tombstones) for canonical_id,
// in append order.
func (s *Store) ReadAll(canonicalID string) ([]CanonicalRecord, error)

// Rebuild forces a full index rebuild from memory.jsonl. Idempotent.
func (s *Store) Rebuild() error
```

Errors are sentinel-style (`ErrNotFound`, `ErrDeleted`, `ErrLocked`, `ErrSchemaTooNew`) for caller ergonomics.

### Operational flow

- **Open**: lock → recover (truncate partial line) → load schema.json (bootstrap if missing) → load index.json → validate fingerprint → if invalid, full rebuild → ready.
- **Append**: validate → assign canonical_id (if new) and line_ulid → encode → write through bufio → update in-memory index → mark index dirty.
- **AppendBatch**: same but one buffered flush + fsync + index write at end.
- **Close**: flush bufio → fsync data file → write index.json atomically → release lock.

## 6. Risks and Mitigations

| Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| Partial-line crash leaves invalid JSONL | Startup recovery truncates to last `\n`; covered by an explicit test that injects a half-written line. |
| Stale index served silently (fingerprint collision) | xxh64(size + head + tail) + `last_line_count` cross-check. Both must match. |
| Advisory lock not enforced if external process ignores it | Document the convention; we control all writers in v1. Future: surface lock-holder PID in error message. |
| Post-merge revision collision diverges across clients | Total-order tiebreak `(updated_at, line_ulid)` baked into rebuild; ensure all clients run identical code (single binary). |
| schema_version drift across team members | Hard gate: refuse to operate if `schema.json.schema_version > StoreSupportedVersion`. Soft skip + warn per-line for unknown versions. |
| `encoding/json` performance on 100k lines | Acceptable for v1 (30-300ms rebuild). Profile-driven optimization deferred. |
| Test isolation (each test needs its own rootDir) | t.TempDir() pattern; Store constructor is the only entry point — no globals. |

## 7. Success Criteria

A change is "done" when:

1. `internal/store/` package compiles cleanly with `go build ./...`.
2. `go test ./internal/store/...` passes, including these tests:
   - Append + ReadLive round-trip.
   - Append + Update (revision 2) + ReadLive returns revision 2.
   - Append + Tombstone + ReadLive returns `ErrDeleted`; ReadAll returns full history including tombstone.
   - Two stores opened at the same rootDir: second returns `ErrLocked`.
   - Crash recovery: write a half-line by hand, reopen, last byte is `\n`, index rebuilds correctly.
   - Fingerprint invalidation: corrupt index, reopen, rebuild runs.
   - schema.json schema_version > supported → `ErrSchemaTooNew`.
   - Conflict tiebreak: two lines with identical `(canonical_id, revision)`, deterministic winner by `(updated_at, line_ulid)`.
   - Bulk append (1000 records) completes under a reasonable wall-clock budget (smoke test, not a benchmark gate).
3. `go vet` and `go test -race` are clean.
4. The `debug-store` subcommand can append a record from the CLI and read it back, demonstrating end-to-end integration with `main`.
5. No exported API leaks beyond the `internal/store` import path.
6. No adapter, sync, TUI, or config code is touched.

## 8. Notes for Downstream Phases

- **sdd-spec**: formalize the JSON schema for `CanonicalRecord` (incl. new `line_ulid` field), error sentinels, and the public API contract. Include test scenarios as spec-level acceptance criteria.
- **sdd-design**: detail the recovery state machine, the rebuild algorithm pseudocode, the lock-acquisition error message format, and the directory bootstrap sequence. Decide test fixture layout.
- **New field added by this proposal**: `line_ulid string` on every record line. PRD-0 did not define this; it is required by the Q2 tiebreak rule. Flag this as a PRD-0 amendment in the spec phase.

title: PRD-0 amendment: line_ulid field added per JSONL line
content:
## What
Amendment to PRD-0 (canonical memory schema): every JSONL line MUST include a `line_ulid` field — a ULID generated at append time, distinct from `canonical_id`.

## Why
The proposal phase (sdd/canonical-memory-schema/proposal) identified that post-merge revision conflicts need a fully deterministic tiebreak rule. Two lines may share the same `canonical_id`, `revision`, AND `updated_at` (same-millisecond writes on different machines that merge via git). The original PRD-0 had no third tiebreaker, so the rebuild outcome was machine-dependent.

`line_ulid` provides:
- A monotonic, lexicographically comparable per-line identifier
- Deterministic tiebreak after `(updated_at desc, line_ulid asc)`
- Cheap (16 bytes), thread-safe via `oklog/ulid/v2.Make()`

## Final tiebreak rule for index rebuild
For multiple lines sharing the same `canonical_id`, the "winning" revision is selected by:
1. Highest `revision` number
2. If tied: highest `updated_at`
3. If still tied: highest `line_ulid` (lex order)

## Scope of amendment
- Add `line_ulid: string` (ULID, required) to the per-line schema
- Distinct from `canonical_id` (which is the stable identity across revisions)
- Frozen for v1; future schema versions may relax if a better mechanism emerges

## Approved by
User (interactive SDD propose review, session 2026-05-14)

title: sdd/canonical-memory-schema/spec
content:
# Spec: canonical-memory-schema — PRD-0 Storage Layer
## Change: canonical-memory-schema | Phase: spec | Version: 1.0

---

## 0. References

- PRD-0: `.wrapper-mems/memory.jsonl` + `index.json` + `schema.json` frozen contract
- PRD-0 Amendment: `line_ulid` field added to every JSONL line (approved 2026-05-14)
- Proposal: `sdd/canonical-memory-schema/proposal` (observation #47)
- Exploration: `sdd/canonical-memory-schema/explore` (observation #46)

---

## 1. Out of Scope

The following are explicitly NOT covered by this change:

- Adapter interface and implementations (PRD-1: engram adapter, claude-mem adapter)
- Sync orchestration, HTTP calls to `engram serve`, narrative ingest (PRD-3)
- TUI / bubbletea (PRD-4)
- Config loading, `~/.config/wrapper-mems` path resolution (PRD-5)
- User-facing CLI subcommands (`wrapper-mems sync`, `wrapper-mems list`)
- Compaction tool (PRD-0 §8 future work)
- Personal-scope filtering, `local_only` privacy flag
- Multi-writer concurrency / WAL
- Performance optimization beyond what standard `encoding/json` + `bufio` provides
- Provider ID mapping (that is index.json content owned by PRD-1 adapters, not the store)

---

## 2. Data Model Requirements

### 2.1 CanonicalRecord Schema

**REQ-DATA-01**: Every JSONL line MUST be a valid JSON object representing a `CanonicalRecord` with the following required fields:

| Field | Type | Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| `canonical_id` | string | ULID format, non-empty, stable across revisions |
| `line_ulid` | string | ULID format, non-empty, unique per line, generated at append time |
| `schema_version` | int | Must equal `StoreSupportedVersion` (currently 1) for lines this code writes |
| `kind` | string | One of: `"observation"`, `"session_summary"`, `"relation"` |
| `revision` | int | Positive integer; starts at 1 for the first append of a canonical_id |
| `supersedes` | string | Empty string for revision 1; equals `canonical_id` for revision ≥ 2 and tombstones |
| `deleted` | bool | False for live records; true for tombstones |
| `title` | string | May be empty for tombstones |
| `content` | string | May be empty for tombstones |
| `content_format` | string | e.g. `"markdown"`, `"text"` |
| `origin` | object | Contains `provider`, `provider_id`, `author`, `session_id` |
| `created_at` | string | RFC3339 with nanosecond precision |
| `updated_at` | string | RFC3339 with nanosecond precision |
| `tags` | array of string | May be empty |
| `topic_key` | string | May be empty |
| `type` | string | Observation type classification; may be empty |

**REQ-DATA-02**: `canonical_id` MUST be a valid ULID string (26 uppercase base32 characters). The store MUST generate one via `ulid.Make()` if the caller passes an empty `canonical_id` on the first append.

**REQ-DATA-03**: `line_ulid` MUST be generated by the store at append time, regardless of what the caller provides. The caller MUST NOT control `line_ulid`. It is the store's concern, not the caller's.

**REQ-DATA-04**: `supersedes` on a tombstone record MUST equal the `canonical_id` of that same record (self-referential, meaning "this closes the chain"). The store MUST enforce this invariant at tombstone construction time and reject a malformed tombstone with `ErrInvalidRecord`.

**REQ-DATA-05**: `revision` MUST be strictly monotonically increasing within a `canonical_id` chain as appended by a single process. The store MUST assign `revision = prior_max_revision + 1` when the caller does not specify a revision, or when the caller specifies revision 0.

**REQ-DATA-06**: For tombstone lines, `title` and `content` SHOULD be empty strings. The store MUST NOT reject a tombstone with non-empty `title`/`content`, but MUST set `deleted: true`.

### 2.2 IndexEntry Schema

**REQ-DATA-07**: `index.json` MUST contain a JSON object with the following top-level fields:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `schema_version` | int | Index format version (currently 1) |
| `source_fingerprint` | string | Hex-encoded xxh64(size_u64_le ++ first_4KB ++ last_4KB) of `memory.jsonl` |
| `last_line_count` | int | Number of newline-terminated lines in `memory.jsonl` at index build time |
| `built_at` | string | RFC3339 timestamp of last rebuild |
| `entries` | map[string]IndexEntry | Key is `canonical_id` |

**REQ-DATA-08**: Each `IndexEntry` in `index.json` MUST contain:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `canonical_id` | string | Redundant with map key; included for direct serialization |
| `latest_revision` | int | Revision number of the winning line after tiebreak |
| `latest_line_offset` | int64 | Byte offset of the winning line's start in `memory.jsonl` |
| `deleted` | bool | True if the latest revision is a tombstone |
| `updated_at` | string | `updated_at` from the winning line |
| `line_ulid` | string | `line_ulid` of the winning line |

### 2.3 Schema.json Schema

**REQ-DATA-09**: `schema.json` MUST contain:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `schema_version` | int | Current schema version written by this store (must equal `StoreSupportedVersion`) |
| `created_at` | string | RFC3339 timestamp of when this schema.json was first bootstrapped |

---

## 3. File System Requirements

**REQ-FS-01**: The store root directory (`.wrapper-mems/`) MUST be created on first `Open()` if it does not exist, using `os.MkdirAll` with mode `0755`.

**REQ-FS-02**: `memory.jsonl` MUST be opened with flags `os.O_APPEND | os.O_CREATE | os.O_WRONLY` and mode `0644`. The store MUST use a `bufio.Writer` of size 64KB wrapping the file descriptor for all write operations.

**REQ-FS-03**: Each JSONL line MUST be a single JSON object followed immediately by a `\n` (U+000A) newline character. No other whitespace between records. No trailing content after the final `\n` in a valid file.

**REQ-FS-04**: `index.json` MUST be written atomically: marshal to a temporary file in the same directory, then `os.Rename()` to the final path. The store MUST NOT write directly to `index.json`.

**REQ-FS-05**: `schema.json` MUST be written atomically using the same tmp-rename pattern as `index.json`.

---

## 4. ULID Generation Requirements

**REQ-ULID-01**: The store MUST generate all ULIDs using `github.com/oklog/ulid/v2` with `ulid.Make()`. This function is thread-safe via a process-global `sync.Pool`-backed entropy source seeded from `crypto/rand`.

**REQ-ULID-02**: `canonical_id` and `line_ulid` for the same append call MUST be two distinct `ulid.Make()` invocations and MUST NOT be equal.

**REQ-ULID-03**: The ULID generation function MUST be injectable for testing (wrap `ulid.Make()` behind an interface or function variable on the `Store` struct so tests can supply deterministic values).

---

## 5. Write Path Requirements

### 5.1 Single Append

**REQ-WRITE-01**: `Store.Append(r CanonicalRecord) (CanonicalRecord, error)` MUST:
1. Validate `r` (schema_version, kind, required fields).
2. Assign `canonical_id` if empty (generate via `ulid.Make()`).
3. Assign `line_ulid` unconditionally (generate via `ulid.Make()`).
4. Assign `revision` = `current_max_revision + 1` for this `canonical_id`, or 1 if new.
5. Set `supersedes` per REQ-DATA-04/05 rules.
6. JSON-marshal the completed record.
7. Write `marshaled_bytes + '\n'` through the bufio.Writer (no flush yet).
8. Update the in-memory index to reflect the new line.
9. Mark the index dirty (pending write to disk).
10. Return the completed `CanonicalRecord` with all assigned fields filled.

**REQ-WRITE-02**: `Store.Append` MUST NOT call `bufio.Writer.Flush()` or `f.Sync()` itself. Flush + fsync is the responsibility of `AppendBatch` or `Close`.

**REQ-WRITE-03**: `Store.Append` MUST return `ErrInvalidRecord` (with a descriptive wrapped message) for any validation failure. It MUST NOT write a partial or invalid line.

### 5.2 Batch Append

**REQ-WRITE-04**: `Store.AppendBatch(rs []CanonicalRecord) ([]CanonicalRecord, error)` MUST:
1. Call the single-append logic (REQ-WRITE-01) for each record in order.
2. After all records are encoded and buffered (or on first error), call `bufio.Writer.Flush()`.
3. Call `f.Sync()` (fsync) on the underlying file descriptor.
4. Write the updated `index.json` atomically.
5. Return the slice of completed records and nil error if all succeeded.
6. If any record fails validation, MUST abort before writing any bytes and return `ErrInvalidRecord`. The file MUST remain unchanged.

**REQ-WRITE-05**: `AppendBatch` with an empty slice MUST be a no-op and return `([]CanonicalRecord{}, nil)`.

### 5.3 Tombstone Write

**REQ-WRITE-06**: The store MUST provide a way to write a tombstone for an existing `canonical_id`. A tombstone is a `CanonicalRecord` with `deleted: true`, `revision = prior_max_revision + 1`, and `supersedes = canonical_id`.

**REQ-WRITE-07**: Attempting to append a tombstone for a `canonical_id` that does not exist in the index MUST return `ErrNotFound`.

**REQ-WRITE-08**: Attempting to append a tombstone for a `canonical_id` that is already tombstoned MUST return `ErrDeleted` (idempotency guard — double-delete is rejected).

---

## 6. Read Path Requirements

**REQ-READ-01**: `Store.ReadLive(canonicalID string) (CanonicalRecord, error)` MUST:
1. Look up `canonicalID` in the in-memory index. If absent: return `ErrNotFound`.
2. If `IndexEntry.deleted` is true: return `ErrDeleted`.
3. Seek to `IndexEntry.latest_line_offset` in `memory.jsonl`.
4. Read and JSON-unmarshal that line.
5. Return the decoded `CanonicalRecord`.

**REQ-READ-02**: `Store.ReadAll(canonicalID string) ([]CanonicalRecord, error)` MUST:
1. If `canonicalID` is absent from the index: return `ErrNotFound`.
2. Perform a sequential scan of `memory.jsonl`, collecting ALL lines where `canonical_id == canonicalID`, in file order (append order).
3. Return the collected slice including tombstone lines. Must NOT filter `deleted: true`.

**REQ-READ-03**: Both `ReadLive` and `ReadAll` MUST apply the two-pass decode: first unmarshal into `RawRecord` to check `schema_version`, then unmarshal the full record. Lines with `schema_version > StoreSupportedVersion` MUST be skipped (logged as warning) and NOT returned.

**REQ-READ-04**: `ReadLive` and `ReadAll` MUST be safe to call concurrently with no active write operations (read-only scenario). They MUST NOT require the advisory lock to be held by the caller.

---

## 7. Index Requirements

### 7.1 Fingerprint Validation

**REQ-IDX-01**: On `Open()`, the store MUST compute the `source_fingerprint` of the current `memory.jsonl` as: `hex(xxh64(size_as_uint64_little_endian ++ first_4096_bytes ++ last_4096_bytes))`. For files shorter than 4096 bytes, use the full file content in place of the partial window.

**REQ-IDX-02**: On `Open()`, if `index.json` exists and its `source_fingerprint` matches the computed fingerprint AND `last_line_count` matches the actual line count: the store MUST trust the cached index and skip rebuild.

**REQ-IDX-03**: On `Open()`, if `index.json` does not exist, or the fingerprint does not match, or `last_line_count` does not match: the store MUST perform a full rebuild (REQ-IDX-05 through REQ-IDX-07).

**REQ-IDX-04**: After any `AppendBatch` or `Close`, the store MUST recompute the fingerprint and update `index.json` atomically.

### 7.2 Full Rebuild

**REQ-IDX-05**: `Store.Rebuild() error` MUST perform a full streaming scan of `memory.jsonl` using `bufio.Scanner` and rebuild the entire in-memory index from scratch.

**REQ-IDX-06**: During rebuild, for each `canonical_id`, the winning line (to be stored in the index) is determined by the following total-order tiebreak applied to all lines sharing that `canonical_id`:
1. Highest `revision` wins.
2. If tied: highest `updated_at` (RFC3339 string comparison, nanosecond precision) wins.
3. If still tied: highest `line_ulid` (lexicographic string comparison) wins.

This rule is deterministic and MUST produce the same winner on all machines given the same `memory.jsonl` content.

**REQ-IDX-07**: After determining winners, the store MUST record in each `IndexEntry`: the `latest_revision`, `latest_line_offset` (byte offset of the winning line), `deleted` flag, `updated_at`, and `line_ulid` of the winner.

**REQ-IDX-08**: Lines with unknown `schema_version` (greater than `StoreSupportedVersion`) encountered during rebuild MUST be skipped with a warning log entry. They MUST NOT contribute to any index entry.

**REQ-IDX-09**: `Rebuild()` MUST be callable on an open store without re-acquiring the lock (the store already holds it). It MUST be idempotent.

---

## 8. Schema.json Requirements

**REQ-SCHEMA-01**: On `Open()`, if `schema.json` does not exist, the store MUST create it by writing `{"schema_version": StoreSupportedVersion, "created_at": "<now RFC3339>"}` atomically (tmp + rename).

**REQ-SCHEMA-02**: On `Open()`, if `schema.json` exists and `schema_version > StoreSupportedVersion`, the store MUST return `ErrSchemaTooNew` and MUST NOT proceed. The store MUST be considered unopened (no lock held, no file descriptors open).

**REQ-SCHEMA-03**: On `Open()`, if `schema.json` exists and `schema_version <= StoreSupportedVersion`, the store MUST proceed normally. Forward-compatibility (reading older data) is supported; the schema is not rewritten.

**REQ-SCHEMA-04**: `StoreSupportedVersion` MUST be defined as a package-level constant `const StoreSupportedVersion = 1`.

---

## 9. Advisory Lock Requirements

**REQ-LOCK-01**: `Open()` MUST acquire an advisory lock on `.wrapper-mems/.lock` using `github.com/gofrs/flock` with a non-blocking `TryLock()` call.

**REQ-LOCK-02**: If `TryLock()` returns false (lock already held by another process), `Open()` MUST return `ErrLocked` and MUST NOT open any other file descriptors or create any files.

**REQ-LOCK-03**: `Store.Close()` MUST release the advisory lock as its last operation (after flushing buffers and writing index).

**REQ-LOCK-04**: The lock file (`.wrapper-mems/.lock`) MUST be created if it does not exist by the `flock` library. The store MUST NOT pre-create it.

**REQ-LOCK-05**: The advisory lock MUST guard the entire store directory, not just `memory.jsonl`. A locked store owns all writes to `memory.jsonl`, `index.json`, and `schema.json`.

---

## 10. Crash Recovery Requirements

**REQ-CRASH-01**: On `Open()`, before loading the index, the store MUST inspect the last byte of `memory.jsonl`. If the file is non-empty and the last byte is NOT `\n` (U+000A), the file has a partial trailing line from a crash.

**REQ-CRASH-02**: When a partial trailing line is detected (REQ-CRASH-01), the store MUST truncate the file to the position of the last `\n` byte. If no `\n` exists in the entire file, the store MUST truncate to zero bytes (empty file = clean slate).

**REQ-CRASH-03**: After truncation (or after confirming no truncation was needed), the store MUST proceed with the normal index load/fingerprint-validate/rebuild sequence.

**REQ-CRASH-04**: The truncation operation MUST use `f.Truncate(offset)` followed by `f.Sync()` to ensure the truncation is durable before any reads proceed.

**REQ-CRASH-05**: The crash recovery path MUST NOT return an error to the caller of `Open()` when truncation was performed. The store MUST log a warning indicating that a partial line was recovered and how many bytes were discarded.

---

## 11. Error Taxonomy

The store MUST define the following exported sentinel errors in the `store` package:

| Sentinel | Meaning | Returned by |
|---|---|---|
| `ErrNotFound` | canonical_id not in index | ReadLive, ReadAll, tombstone-of-unknown |
| `ErrDeleted` | canonical_id is tombstoned | ReadLive, double-tombstone attempt |
| `ErrLocked` | advisory lock held by another process | Open |
| `ErrSchemaTooNew` | schema.json.schema_version > StoreSupportedVersion | Open |
| `ErrInvalidRecord` | record fails structural validation | Append, AppendBatch |

**REQ-ERR-01**: All sentinel errors MUST be defined with `errors.New()` and be usable with `errors.Is()`.

**REQ-ERR-02**: When wrapping a sentinel with additional context (e.g. `fmt.Errorf("append: %w", ErrInvalidRecord)`), the original sentinel MUST be preserved as the unwrapped error so `errors.Is` works correctly.

**REQ-ERR-03**: I/O errors from the OS (disk full, permission denied, etc.) MUST be returned as-is (not wrapped in a sentinel), so the caller can distinguish storage infrastructure failures from logical errors.

---

## 12. Public API Surface

The `internal/store` package MUST expose the following and no more:

```
Type: Store (struct, all fields unexported)

Functions:
  Open(rootDir string) (*Store, error)
  (s *Store) Close() error
  (s *Store) Append(r CanonicalRecord) (CanonicalRecord, error)
  (s *Store) AppendBatch(rs []CanonicalRecord) ([]CanonicalRecord, error)
  (s *Store) ReadLive(canonicalID string) (CanonicalRecord, error)
  (s *Store) ReadAll(canonicalID string) ([]CanonicalRecord, error)
  (s *Store) Rebuild() error

Types exported:
  CanonicalRecord   (struct matching REQ-DATA-01)
  IndexEntry        (struct matching REQ-DATA-08)

Errors exported:
  ErrNotFound, ErrDeleted, ErrLocked, ErrSchemaTooNew, ErrInvalidRecord

Constants exported:
  StoreSupportedVersion = 1
```

**REQ-API-01**: No other symbols MUST be exported from `internal/store`. Helper functions, sub-types, and internal state MUST remain unexported.

**REQ-API-02**: The package import path MUST be `internal/store`. No code outside the module root may import it directly (Go `internal` enforcement).

---

## 13. Acceptance Scenarios

### Scenario A: First-run bootstrap

```
Given:  rootDir does not exist
When:   Open(rootDir) is called
Then:   rootDir is created
        .wrapper-mems/.lock is created and locked
        schema.json is created with schema_version=1
        memory.jsonl is created (empty, 0 bytes)
        index.json is created with empty entries map
        Open returns a valid *Store and nil error
```

### Scenario B: Append a new record — round-trip

```
Given:  an open Store on a fresh rootDir
When:   Append(CanonicalRecord{kind:"observation", title:"T", content:"C", ...}) is called
        (canonical_id left empty by caller)
Then:   returned record has canonical_id set (valid ULID)
        returned record has line_ulid set (valid ULID, != canonical_id)
        returned record has revision = 1
        returned record has supersedes = ""
        returned record has deleted = false
        calling ReadLive(returned.canonical_id) returns that same record
```

### Scenario C: Revision update — ReadLive returns latest

```
Given:  an open Store with an appended record (canonical_id=X, revision=1)
When:   Append(CanonicalRecord{canonical_id: X, kind:"observation", title:"Updated", ...}) is called
Then:   returned record has revision = 2
        returned record has supersedes = X
        ReadLive(X) returns the revision-2 record
        ReadAll(X) returns both revision-1 and revision-2, in file order
```

### Scenario D: Tombstone — ReadLive returns ErrDeleted

```
Given:  an open Store with canonical_id=X at revision=2
When:   a tombstone record is appended (canonical_id=X, deleted=true)
Then:   tombstone has revision = 3, supersedes = X, deleted = true
        ReadLive(X) returns ErrDeleted
        ReadAll(X) returns 3 records (rev1, rev2, tombstone), in file order
        errors.Is(err, ErrDeleted) == true
```

### Scenario E: Double tombstone rejected

```
Given:  an open Store where canonical_id=X is already tombstoned
When:   another tombstone for X is appended
Then:   Append returns ErrDeleted
        memory.jsonl is unchanged (no new line written)
```

### Scenario F: Advisory lock contention

```
Given:  Store S1 is open on rootDir
When:   Open(rootDir) is called a second time (same process, new call)
Then:   Open returns ErrLocked
        errors.Is(err, ErrLocked) == true
        S1 remains operational and unaffected
```

### Scenario G: Crash recovery — partial trailing line

```
Given:  a valid memory.jsonl with 3 complete lines (each ending in \n)
        a 4th partial line appended directly (no trailing \n) simulating a crash
When:   Open(rootDir) is called
Then:   the partial line is detected and truncated
        memory.jsonl ends with \n (last valid newline preserved)
        index contains exactly 3 entries (or fewer if any share canonical_id)
        Open returns nil error
        a warning is logged about the recovery
```

### Scenario H: Crash recovery — no newline at all

```
Given:  a memory.jsonl containing arbitrary bytes with no \n anywhere
When:   Open(rootDir) is called
Then:   memory.jsonl is truncated to 0 bytes
        index is empty
        Open returns nil error
```

### Scenario I: Fingerprint invalidation triggers rebuild

```
Given:  a valid Store previously closed (index.json cached, fingerprint recorded)
        memory.jsonl is externally modified (simulating a git pull adding new lines)
When:   Open(rootDir) is called
Then:   fingerprint mismatch is detected
        Rebuild() is invoked automatically
        the new index reflects all lines in the current memory.jsonl
```

### Scenario J: Conflict tiebreak — deterministic winner

```
Given:  a memory.jsonl containing two lines both with canonical_id=X, revision=2
        line 1: updated_at="2026-01-01T00:00:00.000000001Z", line_ulid="01AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA"
        line 2: updated_at="2026-01-01T00:00:00.000000001Z", line_ulid="01BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB"
        (same updated_at, different line_ulid; line 2 has higher lex line_ulid)
When:   Rebuild() is called (or Open() triggers rebuild)
Then:   IndexEntry for X points to line 2's offset (higher line_ulid wins)
        ReadLive(X) returns the content from line 2
```

### Scenario K: schema_version too new — Open rejects

```
Given:  schema.json contains {"schema_version": 9999, ...}
When:   Open(rootDir) is called
Then:   Open returns ErrSchemaTooNew
        errors.Is(err, ErrSchemaTooNew) == true
        no lock is held, no file descriptors are left open
```

### Scenario L: Unknown schema_version per-line skipped gracefully

```
Given:  memory.jsonl contains a mix of lines: some with schema_version=1, one with schema_version=99
When:   Rebuild() is called
Then:   the schema_version=99 line is skipped without error
        a warning is logged for the skipped line
        all schema_version=1 lines are indexed normally
```

### Scenario M: AppendBatch — single fsync, batch atomicity

```
Given:  an open Store
When:   AppendBatch(1000 records) is called
Then:   all 1000 records appear in memory.jsonl
        exactly one f.Sync() call is made (verifiable via OS-level instrumentation or mock)
        index.json is written once after the batch
        all 1000 ReadLive calls succeed
```

### Scenario N: AppendBatch with invalid record — no partial write

```
Given:  an open Store
        a batch of 3 records where record[1] is invalid (empty kind)
When:   AppendBatch is called
Then:   AppendBatch returns ErrInvalidRecord
        memory.jsonl is unchanged (0 new bytes)
        index is unchanged
```

### Scenario O: ReadAll for unknown canonical_id

```
Given:  an open Store with no record for canonical_id=Y
When:   ReadAll("Y") is called
Then:   returns ErrNotFound
        errors.Is(err, ErrNotFound) == true
```

### Scenario P: Close flushes and releases lock

```
Given:  an open Store S1 with dirty in-memory state (unflushed Append calls)
When:   S1.Close() is called
Then:   bufio.Writer is flushed
        f.Sync() is called
        index.json is written atomically
        advisory lock is released
        a subsequent Open(rootDir) succeeds without ErrLocked
```

### Scenario Q: Tombstone supersedes validation

```
Given:  an open Store
When:   Append is called with a record where deleted=true but supersedes != canonical_id
Then:   Append returns ErrInvalidRecord
        memory.jsonl is unchanged
```

### Scenario R: Rebuild is idempotent

```
Given:  an open Store with N records in memory.jsonl
When:   Rebuild() is called three times in succession
Then:   all three calls return nil error
        the final index state is identical after each call
```

---

## 14. Whole-Change Acceptance Criteria

The change is accepted when ALL of the following are true:

1. `go build ./...` passes with zero errors and zero warnings.
2. `go vet ./...` passes with no issues.
3. `go test -race ./internal/store/...` passes with zero failures.
4. All 18 acceptance scenarios (A through R) have corresponding Go test cases that pass.
5. No exported symbol from `internal/store` is accessible outside the module (Go `internal` enforcement verified by attempting an import from a scratch package).
6. The `debug-store` CLI subcommand can: append a record, read it back, delete it, and rebuild the index — all exercising the public API.
7. No code in PRD-1, PRD-3, PRD-4, or PRD-5 scope is modified.
8. The `line_ulid` field is present on every line in `memory.jsonl` written by this implementation (verified by a test that inspects raw bytes).

---

## 15. Spec Assumptions (forced by proposal ambiguities)

1. **`Append` vs. `AppendBatch` fsync split**: The proposal specified "fsync per batch" but also included `Append` as a public method. This spec resolves the ambiguity by making `Append` buffer-only (no fsync) and making `AppendBatch` + `Close` responsible for fsync. Callers who use only `Append` must call `Close` to guarantee durability.

2. **`ReadAll` scan strategy**: The proposal did not specify whether `ReadAll` uses the index or a full file scan. This spec mandates a full sequential scan (append order is semantically meaningful for audit purposes) and explicitly includes tombstones.

3. **Double-tombstone behavior**: The proposal did not specify what happens when a second tombstone is appended. This spec mandates `ErrDeleted` rejection as the safest default (prevents index confusion from duplicate tombstones).

4. **Crash with no newline**: The proposal covered partial trailing lines but not the degenerate case of a file with no newlines. This spec mandates truncate-to-zero in that case.

5. **Lock file creation**: Spec delegates lock file creation to the `flock` library (standard behavior), not the store bootstrap code.

title: sdd/canonical-memory-schema/design
content:
# Design: canonical-memory-schema — PRD-0 Storage Layer
## Change: canonical-memory-schema | Phase: design | Version: 1.0

---

## 0. References

- Proposal: observation #47 (`sdd/canonical-memory-schema/proposal`)
- Spec: observation #49 (`sdd/canonical-memory-schema/spec`) — 18 acceptance scenarios A–R
- PRD-0 Amendment: observation #48 (`line_ulid` per JSONL line)
- Exploration: observation #46

This design implements the spec's REQ-* requirements as a concrete Go package blueprint.

---

## 1. Package Layout — `internal/store/`

All symbols live in package `store`. Only the spec-listed exports cross the package boundary (REQ-API-01).

```
internal/store/
  store.go        — Store struct, Open, Close, dirty-flag, in-memory index map
  record.go       — CanonicalRecord, RawRecord, Origin, validators, two-pass decode
  index.go        — IndexEntry, Index, load/persist, fingerprint compute, atomic write
  rebuild.go      — Rebuild(), tiebreak comparator, streaming JSONL scan
  ulid.go         — ulidSource interface, defaultULIDSource (oklog), test override
  tombstone.go    — buildTombstone(), tombstone invariant validator
  lock.go         — fileLock wrapper around gofrs/flock; TryLock + release semantics
  schema.go       — SchemaFile struct, bootstrap, version gate, StoreSupportedVersion
  recover.go      — recoverPartialLine() truncation state machine
  errors.go       — ErrNotFound, ErrDeleted, ErrLocked, ErrSchemaTooNew, ErrInvalidRecord
  paths.go        — relative path constants (memoryJSONL, indexJSON, schemaJSON, lockFile)
  testdata/       — fixture JSONL/JSON files (see §10)
  *_test.go       — one test file per source file mirroring scenarios A–R
```

### Per-file responsibility

| File | Exported | Unexported (key) |
|---|---|---|
| `store.go` | `Store`, `Open`, `(*Store).Close`, `(*Store).Append`, `(*Store).AppendBatch`, `(*Store).ReadLive`, `(*Store).ReadAll`, `(*Store).Rebuild` | `mu sync.RWMutex`, `idx *index`, `f *os.File`, `w *bufio.Writer`, `lock *fileLock`, `ulid ulidSource`, `dirty bool` |
| `record.go` | `CanonicalRecord`, `Origin` | `RawRecord`, `validateRecord`, `decodeLine` |
| `index.go` | `IndexEntry` | `index` (private struct with map + meta), `loadIndex`, `(*index).persist`, `computeFingerprint`, `atomicWriteJSON` |
| `rebuild.go` | — | `rebuildFromFile`, `tiebreakWinner`, `lineCandidate` |
| `ulid.go` | — | `ulidSource` (interface), `defaultULIDSource`, `newULID()` |
| `tombstone.go` | — | `buildTombstone`, `validateTombstone` |
| `lock.go` | — | `fileLock`, `(*fileLock).tryAcquire`, `(*fileLock).release` |
| `schema.go` | `StoreSupportedVersion` | `schemaFile`, `loadOrBootstrapSchema` |
| `recover.go` | — | `recoverPartialLine` |
| `errors.go` | `ErrNotFound`, `ErrDeleted`, `ErrLocked`, `ErrSchemaTooNew`, `ErrInvalidRecord` | — |
| `paths.go` | — | `memoryFilename = "memory.jsonl"`, `indexFilename = "index.json"`, `schemaFilename = "schema.json"`, `lockFilename = ".lock"` |

---

## 2. Type Definitions

### 2.1 `CanonicalRecord` (record.go)

```go
// CanonicalRecord is one observation line in memory.jsonl.
// All fields are serialized via encoding/json.
type CanonicalRecord struct {
    CanonicalID    string   `json:"canonical_id"`
    LineULID       string   `json:"line_ulid"`
    SchemaVersion  int      `json:"schema_version"`
    Kind           string   `json:"kind"`
    Revision       int      `json:"revision"`
    Supersedes     string   `json:"supersedes"`
    Deleted        bool     `json:"deleted"`
    Title          string   `json:"title"`
    Content        string   `json:"content"`
    ContentFormat  string   `json:"content_format"`
    Origin         Origin   `json:"origin"`
    CreatedAt      string   `json:"created_at"`
    UpdatedAt      string   `json:"updated_at"`
    Tags           []string `json:"tags"`
    TopicKey       string   `json:"topic_key"`
    Type           string   `json:"type"`
}

// Origin describes the provider source of a record.
type Origin struct {
    Provider   string `json:"provider"`
    ProviderID string `json:"provider_id"`
    Author     string `json:"author"`
    SessionID  string `json:"session_id"`
}
```

### 2.2 `RawRecord` (record.go, unexported)

```go
// rawRecord is the minimal decode used to gate by schema_version
// before full unmarshalling (REQ-READ-03).
type rawRecord struct {
    SchemaVersion int             `json:"schema_version"`
    CanonicalID   string          `json:"canonical_id"`
    LineULID      string          `json:"line_ulid"`
    Revision      int             `json:"revision"`
    UpdatedAt     string          `json:"updated_at"`
    Deleted       bool            `json:"deleted"`
    raw           json.RawMessage // retain for second pass
}
```

### 2.3 `IndexEntry` and `index` (index.go)

```go
// IndexEntry is the per-canonical_id pointer to the winning line.
type IndexEntry struct {
    CanonicalID      string `json:"canonical_id"`
    LatestRevision   int    `json:"latest_revision"`
    LatestLineOffset int64  `json:"latest_line_offset"`
    Deleted          bool   `json:"deleted"`
    UpdatedAt        string `json:"updated_at"`
    LineULID         string `json:"line_ulid"`
}

// index is the on-disk + in-memory cache. Persisted as index.json.
type index struct {
    SchemaVersion     int                    `json:"schema_version"`
    SourceFingerprint string                 `json:"source_fingerprint"`
    LastLineCount     int                    `json:"last_line_count"`
    BuiltAt           string                 `json:"built_at"`
    Entries           map[string]IndexEntry  `json:"entries"`
}
```

### 2.4 `schemaFile` (schema.go)

```go
type schemaFile struct {
    SchemaVersion int    `json:"schema_version"`
    CreatedAt     string `json:"created_at"`
}

const StoreSupportedVersion = 1
```

### 2.5 `Store` (store.go)

```go
type Store struct {
    rootDir string

    mu    sync.RWMutex   // guards idx, dirty, w
    idx   *index
    f     *os.File       // append-only memory.jsonl handle
    w     *bufio.Writer  // 64KB buffer on top of f
    lock  *fileLock
    ulid  ulidSource     // injectable for tests
    dirty bool           // true if buffered writes exist or index needs persist
    closed bool
}
```

### 2.6 Error types (errors.go)

```go
var (
    ErrNotFound               = errors.New("store: canonical_id not found")
    ErrDeleted                = errors.New("store: canonical_id is tombstoned")
    ErrLocked                 = errors.New("store: another process holds the lock")
    ErrSchemaTooNew           = errors.New("store: schema.json version is newer than supported")
    ErrInvalidRecord          = errors.New("store: record failed validation")
)
```

OS errors propagate raw (REQ-ERR-03). Wrapping uses `fmt.Errorf("...: %w", ErrX)` so `errors.Is` works (REQ-ERR-02).

---

## 3. Recovery State Machine (recover.go)

`recoverPartialLine(f *os.File) (truncatedBytes int64, err error)` runs in `Open` **before** index load, on the file opened `O_RDWR` for recovery, then re-opened `O_APPEND|O_WRONLY` for writes.

### Pseudocode

```
recoverPartialLine(f):
    size = stat(f).size
    if size == 0:
        return 0, nil                           // empty file, clean

    lastByte = readByteAt(f, size - 1)
    if lastByte == '\n':
        return 0, nil                           // clean tail

    // Tail is partial. Scan backward for last '\n'.
    bufSize = 4096
    pos = size
    found = -1
    while pos > 0:
        readSize = min(bufSize, pos)
        pos -= readSize
        buf = readAt(f, pos, readSize)
        for i = len(buf)-1 down to 0:
            if buf[i] == '\n':
                found = pos + i + 1             // byte AFTER newline
                break outer
    end while

    if found == -1:
        // No newline anywhere in file => entire file is garbage.
        f.Truncate(0); f.Sync()
        return size, nil

    discarded = size - found
    f.Truncate(found); f.Sync()
    return discarded, nil
```

### Edge cases handled (Scenarios G, H)

| Case | Behavior |
|---|---|
| Empty file (size=0) | No-op, return 0. |
| File ending with `\n` | Clean; no-op. |
| File ending with partial JSON (no `\n` at end), prior newlines exist | Truncate to position after last `\n`. |
| File with no `\n` anywhere | Truncate to 0 bytes (Scenario H). |
| Single byte file == `\n` | Clean; no-op. |
| Discard length > 0 | Caller logs warning with byte count (REQ-CRASH-05). |

`Open` logs `"store: recovered N bytes of partial trailing line"` via stdlib `log` when `truncatedBytes > 0`. Returns nil error per REQ-CRASH-05.

---

## 4. Rebuild Algorithm (rebuild.go)

### 4.1 Public entrypoint `(*Store).Rebuild()`

```
Rebuild:
    s.mu.Lock(); defer s.mu.Unlock()
    if s.dirty: flush bufio + fsync first (so file content is consistent)
    newIdx = rebuildFromFile(s.f.Name())
    s.idx = newIdx
    s.dirty = true                              // index.json needs to be persisted
    persist index.json atomically               // REQ-IDX-04
    s.dirty = false
    return nil
```

Idempotent (Scenario R): rebuild from the same input yields the same map; persist replaces the file.

### 4.2 `rebuildFromFile(path) (*index, error)`

Two passes are conceptually distinct but executed in a single streaming scan with per-canonical_id candidate accumulation:

```
rebuildFromFile(path):
    f = open(path, O_RDONLY)
    candidates = map[string][]lineCandidate{}   // canonical_id -> all lines
```
