  - Error message contains "upgrade wrapper-mems" (user-facing copy)

**Test Traceability:** Every test name either directly references a requirement ID (REQ-CFG-04, REQ-IDN-01, REQ-PRV-02) or describes the scenario being tested. Comments in test bodies cite spec scenarios verbatim.

**Engine Config Alignment:** Verified that config.SyncConfig fields (MirrorEngram, MirrorTimeoutSeconds, Providers) are structurally compatible with enginesync.Config shape, confirming toEngineConfig() shim is sufficient for backward compatibility without requiring engine changes.

Test execution confirms 100% pass rate across all config, identity, and wiring tests. The test suite is comprehensive, traceable to specification, and covers both happy paths and error cases.

title: Comprehensive code review confirms PR-A implementation matches specification and design in all critical areas
content:
Session performed detailed code inspection of all PR-A implementation files. The read-through confirms that:

**Config System (load.go)** implements the exact six-step merge process from design: defaults → project file → user file → env overlay → path expansion → validate. Two-pass yaml.v3 decode (ADR-D7) correctly handles "key absent" vs "key present with zero value" distinction. Slice rule (ADR-D2) properly REPLACES excluded_session_ids on deepest layer (prevents ambiguity). Map merge handles project_path_mapping correctly by merging individual entries.

**Identity System (identity.go)** provides Default() with fallbacks (hostname:user, then USER env, then "unknown"), and Resolve() with correct precedence (env > config > default). Stdlib-only, zero internal deps, ready to be imported by both config and adapters without cycles.

**Schema Version Gating (schema.go)** adds WrapperMemsMinVersion field with omitempty tag (defaults to empty string = permissive). ReadSchema() exposes it to command layer for version-refusal guard (config.Refuse() call in cmd).

**Adapter Refactors** (engram.go, claudemem.go) both define Config structs with all necessary fields. Both implement author resolution via identity.Resolve() when cfg.Author is empty. Claudemem correctly builds excludedSet map at New() time for O(1) filtering. ListNative() filters excluded sessions per REQ-PRV-01 with zero error, zero logging.

**Wiring Helper (wiring.go)** correctly selects transport (cli/http for engram, http-only for claudemem), only includes enabled providers, errors on unknown transport values. Passes privacy.ExcludedSessionIDs into claudemem adapter config (D5).

**Command Integration** (status.go, sync.go) both use buildAdapters to construct real provider instances. Status command now populates provider_health with actual Health() results (D3 fix). Sync command uses toEngineConfig() shim to translate config.SyncConfig to engine's Config shape (backward compat, D5).

**Layering Invariants Verified**: Grepped internal/adapter/ for "internal/config" imports — found zero (comments only, no actual imports). Grepped internal/config/ and internal/identity/ for "internal/adapter" — zero matches. Invariant D4 (no adapter→config edge, no back-edge) is enforced.

**Test Coverage**: claudemem_filter_test.go covers both REQ-PRV-01 scenarios explicitly (excluded → zero records, non-excluded → pass through). load_test.go includes comprehensive table covering every REQ-CFG requirement, every config layer, every merge rule, env var binding, and error cases. Test names and comments directly reference spec IDs, enabling traceability.

The implementation is complete, architecturally sound per the design, and specification-compliant across all tested domains. No gaps, no deviations, no deferred work for PR-A scope.

title: PR-A (Foundation) successfully verified—all implementation artifacts present and tests passing
content:
PR-A (foundation slice) implementation for config-identity-install feature has been completed and verified. The session switched to branch feat/config-identity-install-pr-a and confirmed that all foundation tasks are in place:

**Config system (T-1.1 through T-1.5):** The new internal/config package implements deterministic runtime configuration via a four-layer merge (defaults → project file → user file → env vars). The implementation uses a two-pass yaml.v3 decode pattern (ADR-D7) to distinguish "key absent" from "key present with zero value," enabling precise field-level override behavior. Slice rule (ADR-D2) enforces REPLACE semantics for excluded_session_ids and providers lists to prevent append ambiguity. Map rule merges project_path_mapping key-by-key. All validation checks are in place (schema version, project required, transport values, default_action enum, timeout ≥0). Env overlay binds WRAPPER_MEMS_PROJECT, WRAPPER_MEMS_ENGRAM_BIN, WRAPPER_MEMS_CM_BASE_URL. Path expansion handles ~/ prefixes via os.UserHomeDir(). All unit tests in config_test.go, load_test.go, env_test.go, path_test.go, version_test.go pass.

**Identity system (T-1.6):** The internal/identity package provides author resolution with zero internal dependencies (stdlib only). Implements Default() returning hostname:user, and Resolve() with precedence: env (WRAPPER_MEMS_AUTHOR) > user config > Default(). Decoupled from config package per ADR-D3 to avoid adapter→config import cycles. Both adapters call identity.Resolve() when constructing, eliminating duplicate authorAttribution() functions.

**Schema version field (T-1.7):** schema.go now defines WrapperMemsMinVersion as an optional JSON field with omitempty tag. Empty string is permissive (no version error). Non-empty values trigger config.Refuse() check in command layer (refusal flow confirmed in design, integration testing deferred to PR-C).

**Adapter refactors (T-1.8, T-1.9):** Both engram and claudemem constructors now accept a Config struct by value (instead of bare parameters or nil). Engram's NewExecCommander(cliPath) now accepts the binary path. Claudemem's ListNative() filters ExcludedSessionIDs with O(1) map lookup, implementing REQ-PRV-01 inside the adapter (privacy enforced even if engine is bypassed). All existing adapter tests updated to new signature; new claudemem_filter_test.go covers both privacy filter scenarios.

**Wiring helper (T-1.10):** buildAdapters(cfg *config.Config) constructs only enabled providers, selects engram transport (cli vs http), returns map keyed by provider name. Unknown transport values error. Placed in cmd package per ADR-D4.

**Command fixes (T-1.11, T-1.12):** status.go and sync.go now use buildAdapters. Status command wired to pass real adapters to engine, enabling provider_health population in JSON output. Sync command uses toEngineConfig shim for backward compatibility between config.SyncConfig and enginesync.Config.

**Build & test verification:** Branch is 8 commits ahead of master. go build, go vet, and full test suite (all packages) pass with zero failures. No type errors, no vet warnings, 100% test success rate (tests in all packages that have test files cached or passing).

title: PR-A (PRD-5 foundation slice) implementation complete and verified
content: PR-A (foundation/apply slice) of PRD-5 implementation is complete and verified. Eight commits were merged implementing the full foundation layer: config package (T-1.1-1.5), identity package (T-1.6), schema versioning (T-1.7), engram adapter refactor (T-1.8), claudemem refactor with privacy filtering (T-1.9), buildAdapters wiring helper (T-1.10), status.go fixes (T-1.11), and sync.go refactoring with toEngineConfig shim (T-1.12). Build and test verification passed cleanly. A session limit issue was discovered during T-1.8: the original apply agent reached its context limit while working on the engram refactor, causing the actual code changes to remain uncommitted. The resume agent's checkpoint only captured test file updates (a61213f), leaving the substantive engram refactor uncommitted. An orchestrator manually committed the complete refactor as 76a84fe. This reveals a continuity hazard when agent sessions hit limits mid-task: partial state can be persisted while actual implementation remains lost. Next phases PR-B (init redesign) and PR-C (doctor + version + integration tests) are queued for implementation.

title: Sync refactor builds successfully and passes all unit tests
content: After refactoring buildSyncEngine to use configuration-driven adapter loading via config.Load() and buildAdapters(), the codebase successfully builds with no errors and all unit tests pass. The cmd package tests executed fresh (1.171s), validating the sync command refactoring, while other packages used cached test results. This confirms that the removal of hard-coded adapter instantiation and replacement with the toEngineConfig shim function maintains correct behavior and backward compatibility with the sync engine. The changes were committed with a descriptive message explaining the refactoring purpose and architectural rationale.

title: Implement configuration-driven adapter loading in buildSyncEngine, fulfill T-1.12 TODO
content: The buildSyncEngine function was refactored to implement configuration-driven adapter loading. Previously, adapters were hard-coded inline with fixed host/port values and a TODO comment indicating this should be replaced with a buildAdapters helper once it existed. The refactoring replaces the legacy enginesync.Load() with config.Load(), which supports the full PRD-5 configuration hierarchy (project settings → user settings → environment overrides). A new toEngineConfig() shim function translates the typed PRD-5 config.Config into the legacy enginesync.Config shape, maintaining backward compatibility with the internal/sync package while isolating it from CLI concerns. Adapter instantiation is now delegated to buildAdapters(loadedConfig), which presumably reads adapter configuration from the loaded config object. This fulfills the referenced TODO(T-1.12) and implements part of the D3 (shared wiring helper) design mentioned in comments.

title: Remove direct adapter imports from sync command, introduce config-based adapter loading
content: The sync command's import statements were refactored to remove direct dependencies on specific adapter implementations (claudemem, engram) and the generic adapter interface. In their place, a config package import was added. This refactoring decouples the CLI command layer from concrete adapter implementations, suggesting a shift toward configuration-driven adapter instantiation. The buildSyncEngine function still exists to wire adapters into the Engine, but the direct imports have been removed, likely indicating that adapter factory logic will be moved into the config or a separate wiring module. This improves modularity by eliminating hard-coded adapter references from the CLI layer.

title: claudemem.ToCanonical() updated to use cfg.Author instead of function call
content: The claudemem adapter's ToCanonical() method now retrieves the author from the adapter's configuration (a.cfg.Author) rather than calling authorAttribution(). This change completes the refactoring to eliminate environment-variable reading from within the adapter methods. The author is now resolved once during construction (in New()), ensuring all records from a given adapter instance share the same consistent author. This pattern matches the earlier engram adapter and aligns with the design constraint that adapters receive their configuration by value at construction time rather than making runtime configuration decisions. The ToCanonical() method remains pure (no I/O), simply using pre-resolved configuration. The claudemem package still contains the old authorAttribution() function definition (no longer used), and approximately 20 test constructor calls in claudemem_test.go need updating to pass Config structs rather than just Transport.

title: REQ-PRV-01 privacy filtering implemented in claudemem.ListNative()
content: The privacy filtering requirement REQ-PRV-01 has been implemented in the claudemem adapter's ListNative() method. Records whose session ID appears in the exclusion set are silently skipped from the result, with no error returned and no log entry generated. This implements the design's constraint that privacy filtering must happen inside the adapter (not in the engine), ensuring that excluded observations never reach any downstream consumer even if they bypass the engine. The filtering uses the O(1) excludedSet map built during construction, maintaining efficient performance. The filter is applied after the project filter but before timestamp validation, following the logical order of the requirements.

title: claudemem adapter refactored to accept Config struct with privacy filtering infrastructure
content: The claudemem adapter has been refactored to accept a Config struct at construction time, matching the design specification for task 1.9. The Config struct mirrors the fields needed by the adapter: HTTPBaseURL for transport configuration, ExcludedSessionIDs for privacy filtering, Author for attribution, and supporting fields like WorkerPIDPath and ProjectPathMapping for future use. The New() constructor now builds an excludedSet map from the ExcludedSessionIDs list once during construction, enabling O(1) membership testing. Author resolution is delegated to identity.Resolve() (via engramidentity alias), removing the dependency on os.Getenv calls and aligning with the design constraint that adapters import identity but not config. The excludedSet and isExcluded() method provide the infrastructure for REQ-PRV-01 session filtering, but the actual filtering in ListNative() and other methods still needs to be integrated. This refactoring mirrors the earlier engram adapter changes and sets up both adapters for the upcoming wiring helper function.

title: claudemem adapter constructor signature still unchanged from design specification
content: The claudemem adapter has not been refactored to accept a Config struct, despite the design specification in task 1.9 (Phase 1 Foundation). The engram adapter was refactored to accept engram.Config{CLIPath, HTTPBaseURL, Author, ...} and the integration tests were updated to pass this new signature. However, claudemem.New() still maintains its original single-Transport signature. The design specifies that claudemem should also be refactored to accept a Config struct containing HTTPBaseURL, WorkerPIDPath, ProjectPathMapping, ExcludedSessionIDs (for privacy filtering per REQ-PRV-01), and Author. All 19 test New() calls in claudemem_test.go use the old signature and would need to be updated if the constructor changes. This is part of task 1.9 in the tasks artifact, which is a Phase 1 Foundation task that must complete before the wiring helper can be created.

title: engram integration tests updated to use new Config-based constructor
content: The integration test helper realAdapter() has been updated to use the refactored constructor signatures. Previously it called engram.New(engram.NewExecCommander(), engram.NewHTTPTransport()) with no arguments. Now it constructs a Config struct specifying the binary path and HTTP base URL, and passes it along with the transport to engram.New(). This matches the design pattern where adapters receive their configuration by value at construction time. The engram package builds successfully and tests pass, showing this part of the refactoring is complete. The cmd package still needs the same pattern applied via a wiring helper function.

title: Two Config types in conflict: internal/sync.Config vs new internal/config.Config
content: The refactoring creates a conflict between the old internal/sync.Config (used by enginesync for mirror/timeout settings) and the new internal/config.Config (command-level configuration with multiple subsystems). The design specifies that cmd/wrapper-mems/cmd code should load the new config.Config, pass its Sync field to enginesync.New(), and build adapters via a wiring.go helper that translates from new config to adapter.Config types. Currently sync.go and status.go still use the old approach: enginesync.Load() reads the legacy config.yaml shape. Task 1.10 (create wiring.go buildAdapters function) is blocked on tasks 1.1-1.9 (config, identity, adapter refactors) being complete. Tasks 1.11 and 1.12 (status.go and sync.go rewiring) depend on 1.10. The build cannot proceed until the wiring layer is in place.

title: Constructor refactoring incomplete: adapter signatures changed but call sites not updated
content: The design for config-identity-install refactored the engram and claudemem adapter constructors to accept typed Config structs at construction time rather than being hard-wired. The refactored code in internal/adapter/{engram,claudemem} is complete and shows NewExecCommander(cliPath string) and NewHTTPTransport(baseURL string) signatures now require parameters. However, the call sites in sync.go and integration tests still invoke these with no arguments. The design specifies a wiring helper function (cmd/wrapper-mems/cmd/wiring.go) that should translate from *config.Config to adapter.Config and construct adapters, but this hasn't been created yet. The build is blocked because sync.go line 177 and integration_test.go line 60 have incompatible calls. This is task 1.10 (wiring.go) + 1.11 (status.go fix) + 1.12 (sync.go refactor) in the tasks artifact, which are blocked on prior tasks in Phase 1 (config, identity, schema, adapter constructors).

title: Engram adapter API breaking change: NewExecCommander and NewHTTPTransport now require string argument
content: The refactoring of the engram adapter introduced breaking API changes to the NewExecCommander and NewHTTPTransport constructors. Both now require a string parameter (likely a configuration identifier or path), but existing call sites in the sync command and integration tests were not updated to pass this required argument. Additionally, the return type semantics may have changed—NewExecCommander() returning an engram.Commander interface cannot be used directly as an engram.Config parameter to engram.New(), suggesting the constructor wrapping or composition logic was restructured. This is a complete build failure blocking the feature branch.

title: Engram adapter modified with 87 net insertions across 4 files
content: The engram adapter package (internal/adapter/engram/) underwent refactoring with modifications spanning the main module, test suite, execution logic, and transport layer. The changes total 148 lines affected (87 added, 61 removed), indicating a significant restructuring or behavioral update. These changes are part of a feature branch for config/identity/install PR work. The recent git log shows related work on WrapperMemsMinVersion field support, new config and identity packages, and TUI enhancements, suggesting the engram changes are part of a coordinated feature rollout.

title: Fix internal/config/load.go - Remove store Package Dependency (DAG Constraint)
content: The initial implementation of load.go attempted to import internal/store to access StoreDirName and ConfigFilename constants, but this would create a back-edge in the dependency graph. The design explicitly prohibits config from importing store to maintain a clean DAG. Instead, the constants are duplicated locally in config/load.go with explicit documentation of why. This is a cost of the design but preserves the architectural constraint that adapters and commands depend on config, not the reverse.

title: Implement internal/config/load.go - Four-Layer Configuration Merge
content: The load.go file implements the heart of the config system: a deterministic four-layer merge that respects the design patterns documented in ADR-D2 (slice REPLACE semantics), ADR-D7 (two-pass decode), and design §2.1. The Load function reads the project config file (.wrapper-mems/config.yaml), then the user config file (XDG-compliant, missing is OK), overlays env vars, expands paths, and validates. The two-pass decode strategy allows the merge to distinguish "key absent" from "key present with default/zero value", which is critical for both the slice REPLACE rule (only replace slices when the key is explicitly set) and for the map MERGE rule (only merge into maps when the key is present). The layer-specific rule that Identity is user-config-only is enforced in mergeInto(). Path expansion is XDG-aware. Validation is comprehensive and produces user-friendly error messages that guide action (e.g., "run `wrapper-mems init` or set WRAPPER_MEMS_PROJECT").

title: Implement internal/config/version.go - Version Comparison and Refusal Guard
content: The version.go file implements the version comparison logic needed for REQ-CFG-04: preventing wrapper-mems from operating on stores that require a newer version. CompareVersion is a semver-lite function that handles the canonical version formats (with or without leading "v") and treats the dev build as infinitely high so dev builds always satisfy minimum requirements. Refuse is the guard function that returns an error if the store's wrapper_mems_min_version field is greater than the binary's Version. Empty storeMin is permissive (returns nil), which is the v1.0 default behavior since init.go does not set the field in v1.

title: Implement internal/config/env.go - Environment Variable Overlay
content: The env.go file implements the topmost layer of the four-layer merge: environment variables. The envOverlay function applies each binding when the corresponding env var is non-empty, overriding the project and user config values. The design intentionally excludes WRAPPER_MEMS_AUTHOR from the Config field bindings because identity.Resolve() reads it directly, breaking the adapter→config import edge (ADR-D3). The file documents the architectural decisions around env var scope and responsibility separation.

title: Implement internal/config/config.go - Typed Configuration Structure
content: The Config struct is the central typed representation of the runtime configuration, matching PRD-5 §4.2 exactly. It is the foundation of PR-A and will be instantiated by Load() in load.go after merging project/user/env layers. The struct includes all fields needed to drive adapter construction (Providers.*.{Enabled,Transport,CLIPath,HTTPBaseURL} for engram; Providers.ClaudeMem.{HTTPBaseURL,WorkerPIDPath,ProjectPathMapping} for claude-mem) and privacy filtering (Privacy.ExcludedSessionIDs). The SyncConfig carries over existing fields for engine back-compat. IdentityConfig is documented as user-config-only. Default() returns the v1 baseline with sensible defaults (engram on CLI, claude-mem on HTTP, no mirroring, no exclusions).

title: Transport Interface Contracts and Error Mapping Patterns
content: Both adapters use HTTP transports for I/O, but differ in configuration and error semantics. engram.httpTransport is hardcoded to localhost:7437 and has no configuration path. claudemem.httpTransport resolves its base URL dynamically from supervisor.json with a fallback, making it environment-aware. Both correctly propagate context cancellation and deadline exceeded errors raw (not wrapped), while wrapping network/server errors as ErrUnavailable. claudemem adds a defensive 10s timeout on the http.Client independent of context deadline. claudemem's GetByID must distinguish between "record not found" (clean 404 or empty results) and "endpoint unavailable" (5xx or malformed response) to signal ReadNative whether to degrade to scan. These patterns will inform how PR-A's Config structs drive transport construction and how adapter methods handle context and errors.

title: Existing Store Schema and Config Structure
content: The current schema.json is minimal and only tracks schema version, not provider versions. The existing sync.Config is narrowly scoped to sync engine settings (mirror behavior and provider list). Neither structure has the fields needed for PR-A: schema.json will gain WrapperMemsMinVersion, and config will expand to include providers.engram.{enabled,transport,cli_path,http_base_url}, providers.claude-mem.{enabled,http_base_url,worker_pid_path,project_path_mapping}, privacy.excluded_session_ids, and identity.author. PR-A will introduce internal/config as the new central config system that reads the richer config.yaml shape, merges defaults/project/user/env, and provides config slices to adapter constructors.

title: PR-A Feature Branch Created: feat/config-identity-install-pr-a
content: The primary session has created a feature branch to begin work on PR-A (foundation) of the config-identity-install split. The branch is ready for implementation. The directory structure for the new internal/config and internal/identity packages has been set up. This is the start of the practical implementation work on the design documented in artifacts #118-121.

title: Existing Adapter Constructor Signatures and Wiring Patterns
content: The current codebase wires adapters at the command level without a centralized configuration system. engram.New takes Commander+Transport directly; claudemem.New takes only Transport. Neither constructor reads from config or resolves the author field. The sync command hard-codes both adapter creations, making it impossible to override via environment or config files. The status command passes nil adapters to the engine, causing the provider_health field in JSON output to be unpopulated (documented bug). Both adapters duplicate the authorAttribution() function. All existing tests use the old signatures and will require updates when PR-A refactors constructors to accept Config structs. This discovery confirms the scope of PR-A's refactoring: adapter signatures must change, a wiring helper must be created, and both command files must switch to config-driven initialization.

title: Project Module and Dependency Structure
content: The project is a Go CLI tool with a TUI built on charmbracelet. The module path is github.com/agustincastanol/wrapper-mems and targets Go 1.26.1. Core dependencies are charming and relatively minimal. The project uses yaml.v3 for configuration (to be enhanced in PR-A), cobra for CLI commands, and a rich TUI framework. Current internal packages handle store operations, sync orchestration, and TUI rendering, but lack the config and identity packages that PR-A will introduce. The adapter packages are well-structured under adapter/{engram,claudemem} with test files alongside implementation.

title: PRD-5 Split into 3 Stacked PRs
content: PRD-5 feature work for config-identity-install component was analyzed and found to exceed the project's single-PR review limit of 400 lines (estimated 1800-2800 LOC total). Rather than submit as monolithic change, the feature was decomposed into three sequenced PRs that stack onto main. PR-A establishes the foundation layer, PR-B refactors initialization, and PR-C adds diagnostic/version/integration features. This preserves review quality by keeping each PR under the budget while maintaining logical dependency order through stacking.

title: sdd-tasks Agent Completed: 22 Tasks Across 3 Phases for config-identity-install Implementation
content:
The sdd-tasks sub-agent executed successfully, consuming the spec, design, and proposal artifacts for config-identity-install (PRD-5) and producing a 22-task breakdown across 3 phases organized into 3 stacked PRs.

The task list respects the architectural layering defined in the design: PR-A lays foundation (config/identity packages, schema, adapter refactors) with no new CLI commands; PR-B builds init command using that foundation; PR-C completes doctor/version commands and integration tests. Each task specifies concrete file paths, test locations, and upstream dependencies, enabling parallel work within each phase and clear sequential ordering between phases.

The workload forecast (1800–2800 lines, High risk) triggered the `ask-on-risk` guard: the orchestrator will not proceed to sdd-apply until the user confirms the 3-PR stacked strategy. This prevents undersized or oversized slices and keeps reviewer cognitive load within bounds.

The artifact is persisted in engram (obs #121) and ready for consumption by the sdd-apply phase, which will execute the first slice (PR-A tasks) to deliver the foundation upon which all downstream work depends.

title: SDD Task Breakdown Created: config-identity-install Split into 3 Chained PRs
content:
The sdd-tasks skill executed and produced a detailed task breakdown for the config-identity-install change (PRD-5). The 1800–2800 line estimate exceeds the 400-line review budget, so the work is split into three chained PRs with clear start/finish boundaries and autonomous scope.

PR-A lays the foundation: the new config package with six-step load flow, identity package for author resolution, schema version field, refactored adapter constructors (engram and claudemem with privacy filtering), a wiring helper, and fixes to status/sync commands. This is ~12 tasks covering backend infrastructure with zero new CLI commands.

PR-B adds the init command redesign: provider detection probes, project-name detection chain, flag handling, config/schema/gitignore writing, and non-interactive CI mode. This consumes the config package from PR-A and is cleanly isolated.

PR-C completes the feature: the doctor command with seven health checks and safe-only --fix repairs, the version command, telemetry guarantee in help text, and comprehensive integration tests validating doctor/status parity and version refusal flow.

Each task specifies concrete files, test locations, and dependencies. The plan is ready for implementation once the user confirms the chained PR strategy.

title: SDD Design Reviewed: Config-Identity-Install Architecture for wrapper-mems
content:
The SDD design document for config-identity-install (PRD-5) was retrieved and reviewed. It specifies a complete rewrite of the config system with three-layer merging (defaults, project, user, env), a new identity package for author resolution without import cycles, and refactored adapters that receive config by value rather than importing config globally.

The design introduces six new/refactored command components: a config loader with yaml-based layer merging and env overlay, an identity resolver with precedence rules, a `doctor` command with eight health checks and safe-only fixes, an `init` command with provider auto-detection and project-name detection chain, a `version` command, and adapter constructors updated to accept a config struct.

Key architectural decisions (ADRs D1–D9) lock in slice-replace merge semantics (not append), filtering in adapters (not engine), version refusal in config (not store), and two-pass yaml decode for detecting which config keys were explicitly set (supporting proper field-by-field override semantics without pointer pollution).

The design includes migration guidance: removing `.wrapper-mems/memory.jsonl` from `.gitignore` (doctor detects and fixes stale entries), and stamping v1.0 stores with empty `wrapper_mems_min_version` for forward-compat without blocking users.

This completes the design phase (SDD artifact #120); next is task breakdown via sdd-tasks skill.

title: SDD Specification Retrieved: Config Layering, Init, Doctor, Privacy Filtering Requirements
content:
The specification artifact (SDD #119) for config-identity-install (PRD-5) was retrieved. It defines seven requirement domains covering the complete configuration, initialization, and health-check infrastructure for wrapper-mems v1.

Key requirements span config file paths and precedence (three-layer merge with env overlay), init command behavior (provider probing, project detection, file creation, gitignore setup), doctor command checks and fixes, author identity resolution (three-layer precedence), session privacy filtering (excluded_session_ids list merged from project + user config), and a telemetry-free guarantee.

Specific technical locks include: schema_version must be 1, version refusal on min-version mismatch, memory.jsonl must remain committed (doctor warns and fixes if gitignored), init refuses on existing store without --force, doctor exit code protocol (0/1/2), and strict test scenarios for each requirement.

The spec serves as the source of truth for the implementation; the design (SDD #120) translates requirements into concrete Go package architecture.

title: SDD technical design completed and persisted for PRD-5 (Config-Identity-Install)
content: The sdd-design agent (opus) completed and persisted the comprehensive technical architecture for the Config-Identity-Install change. The design formalizes 11 components with Go code patterns, signatures, and implementation strategies that directly implement the 19 specification requirements. Key architectural decisions: (1) single central config.Config struct orchestrates three-layer merge (project→user→env) with per-field override and RFC 3986 path expansion; (2) tiny identity package (stdlib-only) unblocks adapter construction by keeping it out of config, eliminating the duplicate authorAttribution() function; (3) shared wiring helper centralizes adapter construction, fixing the nil-adapter bug in status.go line 83 (D3) while factoring common code between sync and doctor; (4) doctor command uses real adapters (not nil) to populate provider health, performs 8 ordered checks, and --fix applies only safe idempotent repairs (gitignore entry management, index rebuild, stale lock removal, partial-line truncation) with diff display but no git commands; (5) init command detects providers via subprocess probing, runs project name detection chain, and stops gitignoring memory.jsonl (D2); (6) version refusal guard in separate flow using semver-lite comparison, guarding against forward-incompatible stores; (7) claude-mem privacy filtering happens inside adapter's ListNative() (not engine) to preserve privacy boundary; (8) layering DAG explicitly prevents adapter→config imports while allowing both to import identity. Test design covers unit tests per component with table-driven fixtures and temp-dir isolation, integration tests for init→doctor end-to-end and status-doctor parity. Nine ADRs document major decisions and tradeoffs. Design is implementation-ready and ready for task generation (sdd-tasks phase).

title: SDD specification completed and persisted for PRD-5 (Config-Identity-Install)
content: The sdd-spec agent (sonnet) completed and persisted the comprehensive specification for the Config-Identity-Install change. The specification formalizes all 19 requirement groups (REQ-CFG, REQ-INIT, REQ-DOC, REQ-VER, REQ-IDN, REQ-PRV, REQ-TEL) with explicit behavioral contracts and 30+ testable scenarios. Key specification highlights: (1) config layer is three-tiered (project → user → env) with per-field override semantics and six env var hooks; (2) init command autodetects project name and providers via subprocess probing, creates canonical files, and updates .gitignore to exclude only index.json and .lock (not memory.jsonl) per D2; (3) doctor command performs 8 health checks using real adapters (fixing the nil-adapter bug from status.go line 83 per D3) and --fix applies only safe idempotent repairs; (4) identity author resolution lives in internal/identity package with no import from adapters to config per D4, maintaining clean DAG; (5) claude-mem privacy filter operates inside ListNative() with zero overhead for excluded sessions; (6) zero telemetry with explicit documentation. The spec artifact is now ready for technical design phase (sdd-design agent running in parallel).

title: SDD technical design initiated for PRD-5 (Config, Identity, Install, Privacy)
content: The technical design phase for PRD-5 has been formally initiated to translate the 9 locked decisions and specification requirements into concrete Go package structures and APIs. The design formalizes the architecture decision to centralize configuration in a single `internal/config` package that orchestrates three-layer precedence (project config → user config → environment variables) with per-field merge semantics. A separate `internal/identity` package (per D4) breaks dependency cycles by keeping identity resolution independent, allowing both the config layer and adapters to import it without forming import edges between config and adapters. The adapter constructor API is formalized to accept config-driven values (base URLs, CLI paths, worker PID paths, session ID filters) rather than hard-wired defaults, enabling the shared wiring helper that fixes the long-standing nil-adapter bug in the status command. The design specifies new commands (doctor with --fix capability, version with ldflags injection) and explicitly addresses the gitignore migration challenge (D2) where existing repos incorrectly have memory.jsonl ignored, with doctor --fix providing idempotent removal. Test strategy is scoped at unit/integration boundaries with fixture patterns for filesystem and subprocess operations. The design output will be persisted to engram as the definitive technical specification for implementers.

title: SDD specification initiated for PRD-5 (Config, Identity, Install, Privacy)
content: The wrapper-mems project has initiated formal SDD specification for PRD-5 (Config, Identity, Install, Privacy), formalizing the 9 locked architectural decisions made in the proposal. The specification covers a multi-layered configuration system (project + user + environment), the init command with provider detection and .gitignore management, a doctor command with health checks and repair capabilities (including specific rules around memory.jsonl and .lock file handling), identity management with hostname:user default authorship, and privacy filtering for session exclusion. The spec requires testable scenarios for each requirement group and will be persisted in engram as the canonical specification artifact. A key constraint: --fix in doctor command must never remove lines from memory.jsonl except truncating partial final lines, never modify provider data, and never run git commands.

title: SDD propose phase completed for PRD-5; agent generated proposal artifact
content: The SDD propose phase for PRD-5 completed via subagent, locking all 9 architectural decisions with detailed rationale. The proposal was persisted to engram (obs #118) as the authoritative design artifact. Four major implementation risks were identified and each carries a documented mitigation: constructor refactor must be mechanical (no interleaved logic changes), gitignore edits require exact-match filtering and preview before application, early adopters need clear documentation that memory.jsonl won't auto-commit, and the constructor API breakage is a deliberate v1 freeze gate decision that sdd-design may revisit if a cheaper backward-compat path emerges. The next phase (sdd-spec and sdd-design in parallel) has clear inputs and decision constraints from this proposal.

title: SDD proposal for PRD-5 completed with 9 locked architectural decisions
content: The SDD proposal phase for PRD-5 is complete, locking 9 major architectural decisions that define the v1 operational surface. The design centers on a single-source-of-truth `Config` struct that merges project, user, and environment settings with deterministic precedence. Adapters are refactored to receive config as constructor parameters (breaking change accepted as v1 freeze), eliminating global state and enabling shared testing. The `init` command is updated to emit config.yaml with auto-detected providers and to stop gitignoring memory.jsonl. A new `doctor` command provides ground-truth health diagnosis with safe auto-fixes (gitignore cleanup, lock removal, partial-write recovery). Schema.json gains a min_version field for forward compatibility. The design explicitly defers encryption, multi-project support, cloud sync, and plugin architecture to post-v1. PR slicing is tentatively 2 PRs: foundation (config, identity, constructors, schema guard) and commands (init, doctor, version, UX). All discovered risks carry identified mitigations (test coverage tables for precedence, wiring helper to keep status/doctor in sync, diff preview before gitignore edits, zero value permissiveness for min_version).

title: SDD archive phase added as final PRD-5 workflow step
content: The SDD workflow for PRD-5 was completed with addition of an archive phase as the final step to close the SDD cycle and prepare for master merge. Work has commenced on the propose phase (task 1), which locks architectural decisions for config package shape, gitignore migration, status nil-adapters scope, identity dedup home, and adapter constructor API.

title: Structured SDD workflow established for PRD-5 in wrapper-mems
content: A formal Software Design Document (SDD) workflow was established for PRD-5 as a structured six-phase approach to managing the feature development lifecycle. Starting with architectural decision locking (propose phase already created), the workflow progresses through behavioral specification, technical design, implementation task breakdown with review forecasting, PR-based implementation using stacked commits, and final validation. This systematic approach ensures decisions are captured before design, design before implementation, and implementation is validated against both spec and design before completion. The use of stacked-to-main PRs indicates a preference for atomic, reviewable commits over large monolithic changes.

title: Architectural decisions locked for PRD-5 in wrapper-mems
content: A structured task was created to lock down critical architectural decisions for the wrapper-mems project under PRD-5. The decision areas span package configuration (config package shape), source control patterns (gitignore migration), adapter lifecycle (nil-adapters scope, constructor API), and identity management (identity dedup home). This is part of a formal SDD (Software Design Document) proposal process to ensure these foundational decisions are explicitly captured and agreed upon before implementation.

title: SDD exploration phase completed for PRD-5; Approach A (single config package) recommended
content: SDD exploration sub-agent completed comprehensive analysis of wrapper-mems codebase against PRD-5 specification. Existing codebase has only a minimal 3-field internal/sync.Config using yaml.v3 (already in dependencies). PRD-5 requires a complete config rewrite with three-layer merge (project .wrapper-mems/config.yaml → user ~/.config/wrapper-mems/config.yaml → environment variables). yaml.v3 is already present in go.mod so no new dependency needed. Recommended architectural approach is Approach A: single internal/config package with unified Load() function consolidating merge logic, env var overlay, and identity handling (currently duplicated as authorAttribution() in both adapters). Exploration identified six risks, of which the gitignoreEntries conflict is highest priority — init.go currently suppresses memory.jsonl in .gitignore contrary to PRD-5 which designates it as the committed canonical store. Findings saved to engram memory for proposal phase. Sub-agent ready to transition to proposal phase (sdd-propose).

title: PRD-5 SDD exploration reveals codebase state, config conflicts, and implementation approach
content: User conducted comprehensive exploration of wrapper-mems codebase against PRD-5 spec. Discovered that PRD-5 config shape is a superset of existing sync.Config; yaml.v3 dependency already in place. Current provider construction is hard-wired with no config-driven base URLs or paths. authorAttribution() is duplicated across both adapters (noted in ADR-8). CRITICAL CONFLICT: repo .gitignore currently suppresses memory.jsonl (via init.go gitignoreEntries), but PRD-5 specifies canonical store should be committed with only index.json and .lock gitignored — this is backward-incompatible. status command has nil-adapter gap that prevents real health checks. No version command or doctor command exists yet, though reusable primitives are available. Recommends Approach A: single internal/config package with unified Load(projectDir, userConfigPath) function that merges project config → user config → env vars. This consolidates env var overlay, deduplicates authorAttribution, and provides single entry point for doctor and init validation. Risks identified: gitignoreEntries conflict requires data semantics decision on whether doctor --fix removes erroneous memory.jsonl entries; status command gap must be addressed; adapter constructor API change is breaking.

Store subsystem extended with lock-free Stats() for concurrent TUI backend access. Master branch now contains production-ready interactive terminal interface alongside existing command-line tools.

title: TUI feature stack ready for merge: 15-commit history from feat/tui-pr-3
content: Pre-merge verification confirmed feat/tui-pr-3 branch contains the complete 3-PR TUI feature stack (15 commits) with no uncommitted changes, ready for either fast-forward merge to master or GitHub PR workflow. Untracked build/doc artifacts (.antigravitycli/, docs/) present but do not block merge operations. The commit sequence shows progression from dependency additions (PR-1: Charm deps, status --json), through backend/loader work (PR-2: stats, lazy-index), to complete TUI implementation (PR-3: tabs, routing, observations, conflicts, benchmarks).

title: SDD verify phase completed by sub-agent: final verdict PASS
content: The SDD verification phase for PRD-4 TUI has completed via dedicated sub-agent. All compliance gates pass without blocker issues. The two accepted deviations (D10: 500ms budget, D11: title-only filter) are correctly implemented and documented. The 256ms benchmark result directly justifies the D10 500ms tradeoff. All 26 implementation tasks are accounted for in the filesystem. Build, vet, and test suites are clean. The two suggestions are quality-of-life improvements for future cycles, not blockers. Verification report is persisted to Engram and ready for review by human stakeholders or downstream phases.

title: SDD verification phase complete: PRD-4 TUI passes all compliance gates
content: The verification phase for PRD-4 Terminal UI (three-PR stacked delivery) is complete. All 26 implementation tasks have been validated against the specification, design, and actual code. Build gates (vet, test, benchmark) all pass cleanly. Twelve specification requirements and eight design decisions are confirmed satisfied. The two accepted deviations (D10 and D11) are transparently documented in code comments and design rationale. The verification report captures the full compliance matrix and archives it to Engram for future reference. Ready for next SDD phase (archive).

title: Binding decision D11 verified: filter searches title/kind/type/provider only, NOT content
content: Spec deviation D11 (filter restricted to title/kind/type/provider, NOT content body) is verified in the implementation. The filterRecords function explicitly avoids content deserialization during filter operations, maintaining the lazy-load strategy that keeps startup within budget. The accepted limitation reflects a realistic performance tradeoff: Go's json.Unmarshal is slow enough that decoding every record on every keystroke would violate REQ-TUI-04 (100ms budget). The deferral to v1.1 is properly documented in code comments.

title: Comprehensive test suite passes: 50+ tests across all PR-3 components
content: All test suites for the three-PR stacked delivery pass without error. The 50+ individual tests comprehensively cover the PR-3 surface: tab models (observations, conflicts, status, help), filter/search logic, subprocess mutation delegation, conflict resolution workflows, data integrity (collapse by revision, skip deleted), UI navigation, and golden layout snapshots. The 251.29ms benchmark directly confirms D10 500ms startup budget is realistic. store.Stats() lock-free backend change is tested for correctness and error robustness. No test failures, no code coverage gaps detected.

title: Binding decision D4 verified: no-args exits 0 with help, does not launch TUI
content: Spec requirement REQ-TUI-01 (Scenario: no-args does not open TUI) and binding decision D4 are satisfied: the binary correctly prints help to stdout and exits code 0 when invoked with no subcommand, rather than launching the TUI. This prevents accidental interactive mode activation and improves discoverability of the `tui` subcommand through help text. The behavior is consistent with `--help` flag.

title: Design decisions D1, D8, D9 verified in implementation
content: Spot checks confirm three critical design decisions are correctly implemented in the codebase. D1 (no store.Open) eliminates advisory lock contention, D8 (subprocess mutation) guarantees CLI/TUI consistency, and D9 (snake_case JSON) maintains the canonical snake_case convention established in observer output. These checks validate architectural boundaries and API consistency without requiring full integration test.

title: TUI implementation (PR-3) complete and verified on feat/tui-pr-3
content: The TUI implementation stacked across three PRs (PR-1 backend, PR-2 core, PR-3 tabs+tests) is complete and verified. All build, linting, and test gates pass with no errors. The 256ms measured loadRecords benchmark for 100k lines directly confirms the engineering rationale in decision D10: Go's standard json.Unmarshal cannot beat ~250ms on this workload without external dependencies (sonic, jsonparser) unsuitable for v1 scope. The 500ms acceptance in the specification represents a realistic, measured boundary, not a guess. This closes the spec deviation gap documented in observation #102.

title: Accept D10/D11 PRD-4 deviations as v1 tradeoffs
content: Two spec deviations for PRD-4 were formally accepted as v1 tradeoffs to meet launch deadline while maintaining usability. The 500ms loadRecords budget reflects hardware reality on M4 Macs; bytecode optimization would require external dependencies unsuitable for v1. The title-only search filter preserves interactivity by deferring full-body content indexing to v1.1, while keeping content accessible through detail pane selection. Benchmark assertions and regression tests document these known limitations as intentional design decisions rather than bugs, enabling v1 release without blocking full-content search.

title: PR-3 TUI Tab Implementation Complete: Observations, Conflicts, Status, Help
content: PR-3 implements the three main information tabs plus help for the wrapper-mems TUI (REQ-TUI-04 through REQ-TUI-11). The observations tab provides a filterable, two-pane browser with glamour-rendered markdown content. Filters support query strings (case-insensitive substring on title), kind/type cycling ([f]/[t] keys), and structured prefixes (type:, kind:, provider:). Content is decoded lazily on selection to keep startup under 500ms for 100k-line stores — a trade-off from the original 100ms budget due to stdlib JSON decoder floor. The conflicts tab surfaces unresolved conflicts with a winner/loser detail pane; [w] and [l] keys invoke subprocess calls to `wrapper-mems sync resolve --keep 1/2` respectively. The status tab displays provider health (✓/✗ glyphs), sync watermarks, and store statistics via store.Stats() without opening the store (maintaining D1 isolation). All three tabs use the same subModel interface with pointer receivers and support window sizing propagation. Subprocess execution via syncCmd() uses an injectable runner, ensuring tests never spawn real processes. OSC52 clipboard support (for canonical IDs) is cross-platform safe. Test coverage includes unit tests per-tab, filter correctness, golden layout snapshots, a smoke test exercising the full lifecycle, and a 100k-line benchmark validating the lazy-load performance budget. The implementation adheres to all binding constraints except the startup time budget, which is documented with a clear migration path if faster JSON decoding becomes necessary.

title: Startup performance benchmark and test implemented for 100k-record load budget
content: Phase 3 T-26 implements startup performance validation for the TUI data loader. loadRecords() must parse and collapse a 100,000-line memory.jsonl within 100ms to keep the TUI interactive on first load (REQ-TUI-04). The test suite includes both a Go benchmark (for long-term throughput tracking) and a regular test with explicit time assertion (for CI regression detection without requiring -bench). The generateFixture helper synthesizes realistic records with all fields populated, exercising the real parse and collapse logic. The regular test logs elapsed time and budget in CI logs, making performance regressions visible immediately. Both tests skip in -short mode to avoid slowing down fast CI cycles. This validates that the single-pass load + in-memory collapse strategy stays within the budget and that future optimizations (if needed) are measurable.

title: Smoke integration test implemented using teatest for full TUI lifecycle validation
content: Phase 3 T-23 implements a smoke integration test for the full TUI lifecycle using Bubble Tea's teatest harness. The test launches the root Model with a temporary directory, waits for the header bar to render (confirming the Bubble Tea event loop is running), navigates through all four tabs via keypresses, and quits cleanly. This validates end-to-end wiring: Model.Init() → Update() message loop → View() rendering at each step. The test uses teatest.WaitFor with a timeout and polling interval to robustly wait for the expected header text without blocking indefinitely on slow systems or hanging on crashes. The final output check confirms the program exits without panicking or rendering error messages. The test is skipped in -short mode because running the full Bubble Tea event loop is slower than unit tests, appropriate for CI fast-failure paths. This satisfies REQ-TUI-01 scenario 1 (explicit tui command launches TUI) and provides a regression gate against breaking changes in the root model or subModel interface contract. The smoke test complements golden file tests by validating runtime behavior rather than visual snapshots.

title: Golden file regression tests implemented for layout snapshots at 80×24 terminal
content: Phase 3 T-22 implements golden file regression tests for TUI layout consistency. Golden files serve as visual baselines for full-terminal renders at a fixed, minimal terminal size (80×24, standard for lowest-common-denominator terminal support per REQ-TUI-09). Two test cases capture: the full root layout (header + tab bar + Observations content + status bar) and the Help tab render (keybinding cheatsheet). The tests auto-create golden files on first run, eliminating manual setup. On subsequent runs, current renders are compared against baselines; mismatches report side-by-side diffs. The -update flag allows controlled regeneration after intentional styling or layout changes (e.g., new keybindings in Help tab). This approach catches unintended layout regressions (header bar sizing, tab bar label width, content overflow) without requiring manual visual inspection. Golden files are version-controlled artifacts, making layout changes auditable and reviewable in PRs. Tests satisfy REQ-TUI-05 layout validation and REQ-TUI-09 80-column fit requirement without brittle pixel-level assertions.

title: Golden file test infrastructure set up for layout tests
content: Infrastructure for Phase 3 T-22 golden file tests established. Golden files will serve as regression tests for TUI layout and styling by storing baseline full-terminal renders (e.g., 80×24 terminal with fixture data) and comparing future renders against them. The testdata/golden directory structure is now in place to store these baseline snapshots. Golden tests are updated via `go test -run ... -update` flag, allowing controlled updates when changes are intentional. This setup completes infrastructure for rendering regression detection, addressing REQ-TUI-05 header/status bar layout validation without manual visual inspection.

title: Phase 3 TUI unit tests all passing: observations, conflicts, status tab implementations verified
content: Phase 3 unit tests pass completely. The comprehensive test suite for the three non-stub tabs (observations, conflicts, status) plus utility functions validates filter behavior, user key dispatch, state transitions, subprocess argument construction, and rendering logic. Tests use isolated fixtures (t.TempDir), injected dependencies (fake runners), and direct message injection to avoid side effects and achieve deterministic execution. The fast execution time (381ms) confirms that tests are well-scoped and don't involve expensive I/O or subprocess operations. This milestone completes Phase 3 test coverage requirements (T-19, T-20, T-21) and signals readiness for integration testing and Phase 3 PR submission.

title: Observations test adjusted to accept nil or empty slice as valid "no records" state
content: Test correction in observations_test.go: TestObsModel_ReloadCmd was overly strict, asserting that reload.records must be non-nil. However, loadRecords legitimately returns nil when memory.jsonl doesn't exist in the directory. Both nil and an empty slice represent "no records" semantically. The test was updated to check the important invariant: len(reload.records)==0, which is true whether the return value is nil or []. This makes the test more robust to implementation changes in loadRecords and focuses on behavior over internal representation. This is a minor test hygiene improvement.

title: Status tab unit tests implemented covering health glyphs, watermarks, and sync key dispatch
content: Phase 3 T-21 implements unit tests for the Status tab. The test suite covers health monitoring, sync watermark display, and key dispatch. Tests verify that provider health is visualized via Unicode glyphs (✓ for ok, ✗ for any non-ok status), sync watermarks (last pulled/pushed timestamps) are displayed per provider, and store statistics (line count, file size, schema version) are visible. Key dispatch tests confirm that s (sync), p (pull), and P (push) all return Cmd objects to trigger subprocess execution. The formatBytes utility is tested separately with a table-driven suite covering byte-to-human-readable conversions (B, KB, MB). Tests use direct message injection (statusDataMsg) to avoid async subprocess calls and disk I/O, providing fast, deterministic unit test execution. These satisfy T-21 coverage requirements for the Status tab monitoring and sync triggering capabilities.

title: Conflicts tab unit tests implemented covering resolution flows and subprocess injection
content: Phase 3 T-20 implements unit tests for the Conflicts tab. The test suite covers all three resolution paths: w (accept winner, --keep 1), l (promote loser, --keep 2), and s (skip without removing). Tests use a fake runner injected into the model to verify subprocess arguments without spawning real processes. Each resolution path is tested for success (entry removed, list count decreases) and failure (error message set, entry remains). The skip path verifies that no command is returned and the selection advances without removing the entry. Detail view tests confirm winner and loser labels are rendered. These tests establish baselines for the core conflict resolution workflow and catch regressions in subprocess invocation, state management, and error handling. Tests use t.TempDir() for isolation and sample fixtures for determinism, satisfying T-20 coverage requirements.

title: Observations tab unit tests implemented covering filter logic and state transitions
content: Phase 3 T-19 implements unit tests for the Observations tab. The test suite covers both pure filter functions and stateful model behavior. filterRecords is tested with substring matching (title + content, case-insensitive), structured prefix queries (type:, kind:, provider:), and boundary cases (no match returns empty slice, not nil). Model tests verify UI state transitions: selection navigation via arrow keys, async reload command execution returning obsReloadMsg, filter input focus/unfocus, full-screen detail toggle, and kind filter cycling. Each test uses a fixed sample dataset and t.TempDir() for isolation, avoiding test interdependencies and external file system state. The tests establish baselines for future refactoring and catch regressions in filter logic and navigation behavior. These satisfy T-19 requirements for Observations tab coverage at the unit level.

title: Phase 3 TUI code passes existing test suite without regressions
content: Phase 3 TUI implementations pass integration with existing test suite. The PR-2 unit tests for root model routing, data loading, and subprocess execution remain passing with the new tab implementations in place. This confirms that the new subModel implementations (observations, conflicts, status, help) do not break message flow, state management, or the subModel interface contract. The test suite validates that window sizing, tab switching, and global key dispatch still work correctly with the real tab models replacing the stubs. No modifications to existing test files were needed, indicating clean backward compatibility.

title: Phase 3 TUI implementations compile successfully end-to-end
content: Phase 3 implementation passes compilation verification. All four tab models (observations, conflicts, status, help) compile cleanly alongside the root model and supporting utilities. The successful build confirms that the subModel interface contract is satisfied by all implementations, imports are correct, and there are no circular dependencies or unresolved symbols. This is the gate before any integration or unit testing can proceed. The full build-all-packages test also passed, indicating no regressions in other parts of the codebase (backend, CLI commands, store) from the TUI additions.

title: Model.go stub implementations removed; references real tab implementations in separate files
content: Model.go cleaned up to remove stub sub-model implementations, which are now fully realized in their own files. The stubs existed as placeholders to allow PR-2 compilation while PR-3 was in development. Now that all four tab models are implemented (observations.go, conflicts.go, status.go, help.go), the stubs are no longer needed. The Model struct and root routing logic remain unchanged; only the stub type definitions and factory functions are removed. This change signals that Phase 3 tab implementations are complete and ready for integration with the root model's message routing and lifecycle management.

title: Help tab refactored: extracted helpSection function and reorganized column layout
content: Help tab refactored for code quality and readability. The inline `section` function was extracted to a package-level `helpSection()` helper for clarity and testability. The column layout was rebalanced: Global (5 rows) + Conflicts (3 rows) = 8 rows on the left; Observations (10 rows) + Status (4 rows) = 14 rows on the right. This provides more visual balance than the previous Global+Observations (15) vs Conflicts+Status (7) split. Styling was simplified by removing redundant Render() calls inside helpSection() — the grid structure itself provides visual hierarchy. The 14-char key alignment accommodates longer sequences like "q / ctrl+c" and "↑ / k" without extra padding waste. These changes improve maintainability without affecting the output — the help cheatsheet still fits within 80 columns and presents all keybindings grouped by context.

title: Help tab implemented with two-column keybinding cheatsheet fitting 80-column terminals
content: Phase 3 T-18 implements the Help tab as a static reference cheatsheet. The tab shows all available keybindings grouped into four sections: Global (tab navigation and quit), Observations (list, filter, copy), Conflicts (resolution), and Status (sync). The layout is two columns of 38 characters each, joined horizontally for a compact fit. Every keybinding line is padded to fit within 78 characters (accounting for terminal margins and 80-column limit per REQ-TUI-09). The model has no state or user interaction — Init/Update/SetSize are no-ops, and View() always returns the same rendered cheatsheet. This satisfies REQ-TUI-09 for displaying a reference guide that works on minimal terminal widths without horizontal scrolling.

title: Status tab implemented with provider health glyphs and sync watermarks display
content: Phase 3 T-17 implements the Status tab as a read-only monitoring dashboard. The tab loads project metadata and provider status on Init() via statusDataMsg async messaging. The View renders project name (basename of dir), repo path, canonical store path (.wrapper-mems), and store statistics from store.Stats() call (line count, file size in human-readable KB/MB/GB, and schema version). For each configured provider, a health glyph (✓/✗) is shown alongside the name and sync watermarks (last pulled/pushed timestamps). If there are unresolved conflicts, a warning glyph and count appear. The keys s/p/P trigger sync subprocesses (full/pull/push) which emit to the root overlay; r refreshes the status view without clearing any sync states. Helper functions glyphFor() and renderProviderGlyphs() are exported for the root status bar to show an inline provider status summary. This satisfies REQ-TUI-08 for monitoring provider health and sync state without providing mutation capabilities — all actions delegate to subprocesses.

title: Conflicts tab implemented with winner/loser stacked detail and subprocess resolution
content: Phase 3 T-16 implements the Conflicts tab as a two-pane conflict resolver. The left pane uses bubbles/list to show unresolved conflicts (ConflictEntry items with r<revision>, detected timestamp, dup count). The right pane shows the selected conflict's winner and loser records stacked vertically. Pressing w calls resolveCmd(id, 1) which spawns a subprocess, emitting conflictResolvedMsg when it completes. On success (exit 0) the entry is removed from the list and the view re-renders; on failure the error and subprocess stderr appear in the detail pane. Pressing l promotes the loser (--keep 2) instead. Pressing s advances the list selection without removing the entry. The tab loads the conflicts list once on Init() and can be reloaded when the root model dismisses a sync overlay. This satisfies REQ-TUI-07 for conflict resolution via subprocess delegation (D8), avoiding in-process mutation logic and keeping the TUI read-only.

title: Observations tab polish: native OSC52 clipboard and code style improvements
content: Observations tab implementation polished for production. OSC52 clipboard functionality now uses the Go standard library only (base64 encoding), eliminating the placeholder functions and external library dependencies. The writeOSC52() function encodes the target ID as base64, then emits the OSC 52 escape sequence (ESC ] 52 ; c ; <base64data> BEL) directly to stdout. Terminals that don't support OSC 52 silently ignore it — no error handling required. Code style improvements include moving filter definitions closer to where they're used, renaming internal methods for clarity, and tightening comments. The ContentFormat fallback now treats empty or unknown formats as plaintext, allowing graceful degradation when markdown isn't available. These changes complete T-15 with a robust, standards-based clipboard solution and cleaner code organization.

title: Observations tab implemented with two-pane split layout and full-screen detail
content: Phase 3 T-15 implements the Observations tab with a two-pane split view. The left pane uses bubbles/list for a scrollable, filterable list; the right pane uses viewport for a scrollable detail preview. When the selected record changes, renderDetail() updates the detail pane with metadata + glamour-rendered content (if markdown format). Pressing enter expands detail to full-screen. Pressing / focuses the filter input where users can type plain substrings or use type:, kind:, provider: prefixes. The f and t keys cycle through kind and type filters. Pressing c copies the canonical_id via OSC52 escape sequence (placeholder awaits implementation in exec.go or utils). The glamour renderer is rebuilt whenever the pane resizes so word-wrap matches available width. This satisfies REQ-TUI-06 requirements for the Observations tab behavior and styling.

title: Glamour and teatest dependencies added for PR-3 tab and integration testing
content: PR-3 implementation requires markdown rendering (glamour) for observation content display in detail panes and an interactive test harness (teatest) for smoke tests. Both are now direct dependencies. The glamour addition brought ~10 KB of transitive markdown parsing (goldmark family) but stays within acceptable binary bloat. All Phase 3 external dependencies are resolved.

title: Charm dependencies fully resolved in go.mod for PR-3 tab implementation
content: After PR-2 completion, go.mod contains all charmbracelet libraries required by PR-3 tab implementations (observations, conflicts, status, help). Unlike PR-1 where charm deps were declared but not reflected on disk, the PR-2 dependency resolution is stable and will compile the tab models without requiring additional go get invocations. This unblocks PR-3 from proceeding directly to tab implementation rather than requiring a dependency resolution pass first.

title: SDD Apply Phase Complete: PR-2 Delivered with All Tests Passing
content: The SDD apply phase successfully implemented PR-2 TUI core infrastructure. All 9 tasks were completed, resulting in 5 well-structured commits organized by work unit (dependencies, implementation layers, testing). The test suite (28 tests across data, exec, and model layers) validates that file reading, subprocess execution, message routing, and overlay modal behavior work correctly in isolation. Notable risks for the next phase: glamour and teatest will be removed from go.mod by tidy since PR-2 code doesn't import them yet — PR-3 must explicitly re-add them before implementing tabs that need glamour (full-screen markdown rendering) and teatest (smoke test). The sub-model interface contract provides a clean handoff: PR-3 simply replaces the stub implementations without changing the root model routing logic.

title: PR-2 Branch Complete: 5 Commits on feat/tui-pr-2 Ready for Review
content: PR-2 is complete and ready for code review. The five commits are organized as a coherent work unit: dependencies first, then implementation layers in dependency order (styles and utilities, then data/exec infrastructure, then root model, then CLI wiring), followed by comprehensive tests. The commits are small enough to review independently but logically grouped so they tell a story of how the TUI system was built from the ground up. This follows the work-unit-commits pattern from the team's guidance. The branch is based on the completed PR-1 work, establishing a clear chain: PR-1 backend → PR-2 TUI core → (future) PR-3 tab implementations.

title: CLI Help Output Includes New tui and ui Commands; No-Args Exit Code Correct
content: The CLI integration is complete and correct. The new tui and ui subcommands are properly registered with the cobra command tree and appear in the help output alongside all existing commands. The no-args behavior meets the requirement to print help and exit 0 without launching the TUI automatically (REQ-TUI-01). Users can now discover and invoke the TUI via either `wrapper-mems tui` or `wrapper-mems ui`, and the help system provides clear documentation for both commands. This user-facing integration confirms that PR-2 is ready for end-user testing.

title: Full Project Build and Test Suite Passes; PR-2 Integration Verified
content: The complete project build and test suite confirms that PR-2 implementation integrates cleanly with the existing codebase without regressions. All prior test suites (cmd, adapters, store, sync) continue to pass, indicating that the new TUI package and its dependencies (charm libraries, bubbletea, etc.) do not conflict with or destabilize any existing code. The go vet check finds no issues, confirming code quality standards across the project. The TUI-specific tests run quickly (0.278s for 27 tests) because they use isolated fixtures and fake runners rather than real external services. This comprehensive validation establishes that PR-2 is ready for code review and merge.

title: PR-2 Complete: All 27 TUI Unit Tests Passing, Code Compiles and Vets Clean
content: PR-2 core infrastructure is complete and fully validated. The TUI package now has 27 passing unit tests covering all critical code paths: file reading and JSON parsing in the data layer, subprocess execution and message emission in the exec layer, and message routing and state management in the root model. The fast execution (0.447s for all tests) and use of isolated fixtures and fake runners confirms that tests are well-designed: they test behavior without external dependencies or side effects. The clean build and vet output indicates that code quality meets Go standards. All this work establishes a solid foundation for PR-3, which will add the four tab implementations (observations, conflicts, status, help) and their 9 remaining tests (T-15 through T-23). The stub tab models are already in place and the test infrastructure is proven to work.

title: TUI Root Model Unit Tests Implemented (PR-2, T-12)
content: The model tests establish correct message routing and state management in the root Model. Tab switching tests verify that each hotkey (O/C/S/?) changes activeTab correctly. Quit tests confirm that q and ctrl+c produce QuitMsg via a returned Cmd closure. The overlay modal tests are critical: they verify that while the overlay is active, tab-switching keys are intercepted and don't reach the routing logic, and that overlay dismissal requires enter key and only works when the subprocess has finished (overlay.running == false). The syncDoneMsg test verifies that subprocess completion messages correctly update the overlay state, including capturing error and splitting multiline output into lines for display. Rendering tests confirm that the View() method produces output containing expected content (tab names, quit hint). These tests provide confidence that the root model correctly orchestrates all interactions and prevents mode confusion while subprocesses are running.

title: TUI Subprocess Executor Unit Tests Implemented (PR-2, T-14)
content: The exec layer tests verify that subprocess command construction is correct for all TUI mutation scenarios: full sync, pull-only, push-only, and conflict resolution with keep-winner/keep-loser logic. The tests use injected fake runners to capture and verify argv without spawning real processes, keeping tests fast and avoiding circular execution risk. Error propagation is verified by checking that non-zero exit codes result in a syncDoneMsg with a non-nil error and the output is appended with an "error:" prefix. The test suite establishes confidence that the TUI can correctly invoke all sync subcommands and properly surface their results in the overlay modal.

title: TUI Data Layer Unit Tests Implemented (PR-2, T-13)
content: The data layer test suite provides high coverage of the critical file-reading and subprocess-calling code. Each test is isolated via t.TempDir(), avoiding file system conflicts and pollution. The loadRecords tests verify the collapse-by-canonical_id behavior from multiple angles: that higher revisions always win (regardless of file order), that tombstones are filtered, that blank lines and future-schema records are skipped, and that an empty store returns a valid empty slice. The callStatusJSON test injects a fake subprocess runner to verify that argv is constructed correctly and the JSON response is parsed without spawning a real process — this is critical for the TUI to avoid circular execution (running the test binary from within the test). These tests establish confidence in the data access layer before the tab sub-models (PR-3) begin using it.

title: PR-2 Build Verification Successful; Charm Dependencies Now Direct
content: The PR-2 implementation has successfully compiled and the module dependencies are now fully resolved. The go build verified that all TUI package code (styles.go, data.go, exec.go, model.go) and the tui command entry point (cmd/wrapper-mems/cmd/tui.go) have no compilation errors. The charm packages transitioned from indirect to direct dependencies once the TUI package was imported by the cmd package, as expected in Go module semantics. The build success confirms that the TUI core infrastructure is complete and ready for PR-2 integration testing and unit tests (planned in PR-2 tasks T-12 through T-14).

title: Charm Dependencies Successfully Added to go.mod; Indirect Status Clarified
content: The go get command successfully resolved all charm TUI dependencies and added them to go.mod. They currently appear in the indirect section because the internal/tui package (which imports them) has not yet been imported by the cmd/wrapper-mems executable. This is normal Go module behavior: once cmd/wrapper-mems/cmd/tui.go (or any main-package-importing file) imports `github.com/agustincastanol/wrapper-mems/internal/tui`, the next `go mod tidy` or `go build` will promote the charm packages from indirect to direct dependencies. The module graph is healthy and the blocker is resolved — TUI code can now import bubbletea, bubbles, lipgloss, glamour, and teatest without "no such package" errors.

title: Critical: Manual go.mod Edit Did Not Persist; Charm Dependencies Missing
content: A critical file persistence issue prevents go.mod updates from surviving on disk. The manual edit operation reported successful modification and the structured patch confirmed the change, followed immediately by `go mod tidy`. However, the next read of the file shows the original content without any charm dependencies. This leaves the TUI package implementation files (model.go, styles.go, data.go, exec.go, tui.go) with unresolved imports that will cause compilation failure. The root cause is unknown: possible explanations include (1) a git post-checkout or post-merge hook automatically reverting the file, (2) editor/IDE temporary file behavior, (3) file system caching issue, or (4) the Edit tool outcome being misleading about actual file state. This is a complete blocker for PR-2 — the code cannot compile without charm packages in the module graph. Immediate investigation required: verify git hooks, check file system state, and determine if there's a VCS auto-revert mechanism in place.

title: TUI Command Entry Point Implemented (PR-2, T-06)
content: The tui.go command file now provides proper entry points for both `wrapper-mems tui` and `wrapper-mems ui` subcommands. The implementation verifies that a .wrapper-mems/ store exists before attempting to launch the TUI, giving users a clear error message if they haven't run `wrapper-mems init` first. The Bubble Tea program is created with the root Model and alt-screen mode enabled for a clean full-screen experience. Errors are properly propagated via RunE so cobra can map them to exit codes through the ExitCode function in root.go, keeping the error contract unified.

title: TUI Root Model and Global Layout Implemented (PR-2, T-11)
content: The root model provides the complete architecture for tab-based navigation, message routing, and global state management.
