The tests validate all requirements from REQ-SE-63 (UpdateSyncState atomicity and idempotence), REQ-SE-64 (AppendConflict deduplication, RemoveConflict no-op behavior), and REQ-SE-15/16 (accessor safety and persistence). Tests verify that mutations are atomically persisted to index.json and visible on reopen, that deduplication by (canonical_id, revision) works correctly, and that the accessor methods return safe copies that don't expose internal state. The test suite ensures the store APIs correctly support the sync engine's watermark tracking and conflict detection requirements.

title: All store package tests pass with no regressions (PR-A validation)
content: The full store test suite validates that PR-A's changes do not regress existing functionality. The conflict detection code integrated into rebuildFromFile does not break the tiebreak winner selection, ByProvider population, or index cache validation. The new error sentinels are backward compatible: code checking errors.Is(err, ErrSchemaTooNew) still works because ErrSchemaTooNew wraps ErrSchemaMismatch. The sync-state and conflict types are successfully added to the index struct without breaking serialization or deserialization. The test pass rate demonstrates that the store package remains stable and correct after PR-A modifications.

title: PR-A implementation complete and compiles successfully
content: PR-A implementation is functionally complete. The store package now supports sync-engine requirements including per-provider watermark tracking, conflict detection and recording, and error classification for initialization failures. The successful build indicates all type definitions, method signatures, and error paths compile correctly. The next phase is testing (REQ-SE not yet validated) and then handoff to PR-B which adds the sync.Engine type that consumes these store APIs. The 300 LOC store changes enable the full sync pipeline to be built on top.

title: Completed error sentinel wiring for store initialization paths (PR-A T-11)
content: T-11 implementation is complete. The store initialization path (Open → loadOrBootstrapSchema → ErrSchemaTooNew; loadOrRebuild → loadIndex → ErrCorrupt) now signals corruption and schema mismatches via the new sentinel errors. The error hierarchy using fmt.Errorf wrapping allows existing tests to pass while introducing the new ErrSchemaMismatch classification. CLI code can now distinguish store initialization failures from other errors and map them to appropriate exit codes (1 for corruption or schema mismatch per REQ-SE-48 and REQ-SE-46).

title: Established backward-compatible error sentinel hierarchy for schema mismatches
content:
The error sentinel design uses error wrapping to create a hierarchy while maintaining backward compatibility with existing tests and code. ErrSchemaMismatch is the broad sentinel covering all schema version mismatches. ErrSchemaTooNew is now a wrapper error that includes ErrSchemaMismatch in its chain via fmt.Errorf(%w). This pattern allows:

1. Old code checking errors.Is(err, ErrSchemaTooNew) continues to work
2. New code can check errors.Is(err, ErrSchemaMismatch) for any schema mismatch
3. Both checks work on the same error via the error chain

This is deliberate API design to support gradual migration from ErrSchemaTooNew to ErrSchemaMismatch without breaking existing test code (schema_test.go, store_open_test.go). The tests continue to pass with ErrSchemaTooNew while the new implementation uses ErrSchemaMismatch, avoiding a flag-day refactor.

title: Updated schema validation to use ErrSchemaMismatch sentinel (PR-A T-11)
content: The schema validation path is updated to use the new ErrSchemaMismatch sentinel instead of the legacy ErrSchemaTooNew. This provides a unified error signal for all schema version mismatches detected during store.Open. The change completes T-11 wiring both ErrCorrupt (for JSON parse failures) and ErrSchemaMismatch (for version drift) throughout the store initialization path. Both sentinels are now used consistently so CLI code can identify and report initialization failures with appropriate exit codes.

title: Wired ErrCorrupt sentinel into index parsing error path (PR-A T-11)
content: The error sentinel wiring is minimal but important for error classification. When index.json is corrupted and cannot be parsed, loadIndex now returns ErrCorrupt instead of a bare JSON error. This allows CLI code to distinguish corruption from other file errors and exit with code 1 as required by REQ-SE-48. The error chain is preserved via %w, so the original JSON error is still available for debugging. This is the start of T-11 wiring ErrCorrupt and ErrSchemaMismatch throughout the store initialization path.

title: Integrated conflict detection into index rebuild and persistence (PR-A T-10)
content: The conflict detection implementation is now complete and integrated into the index rebuild pipeline. Detected collisions are accumulated in the conflicts slice during rebuildFromFile and returned in the index struct with all required metadata. When index.persist() is called (which happens in Store.Open and Store.Rebuild), the entire index including the Conflicts field is atomically written to index.json via the existing atomicWriteJSON helper. This ensures that conflict records are discoverable by query methods store.Conflicts() and can be displayed via status --conflicts and resolved via sync resolve commands. The nil-initialization of conflicts to empty slice ensures JSON serialization produces consistent output even when no collisions exist.

title: Implemented conflict detection during index rebuild (PR-A T-09)
content: The conflict detection implementation groups all lines by (canonical_id, revision) and identifies collisions where multiple lines share the same pair. For each collision, the lines are sorted using the same tiebreak rules as tiebreakWinner (updated_at DESC, then lineULID DESC), ensuring the winner is at position 0. This sorted group is then converted to ConflictDuplicate records preserving all metadata needed for resolution (line offset, ULID, timestamp, provider). A ConflictEntry is created with the collision key and the ordered duplicates list, capturing the collision detection timestamp. These conflicts are accumulated and will be persisted in index.Conflicts during index return. This satisfies REQ-SE-33 through REQ-SE-35 for conflict detection and recording.

title: Extended rebuild.go to support collision tracking and ConflictDuplicate building
content: Rebuild.go is being extended to support conflict detection by adding a revisionKey type (though not yet used in collision detection logic) and a provider field to lineCandidate to capture the origin provider during JSONL scanning. These changes set up the infrastructure needed for detecting (canonical_id, revision) collisions and building the ConflictDuplicate records that will be stored in ConflictEntry. The actual collision detection loop will be added in the next change, after tiebreakWinner selects the winning line from candidates.

title: Implemented sync-state mutation and accessor API (PR-A T-05 through T-08)
content: Internal/store/syncstate.go provides the full mutation API for managing sync watermarks and conflict records. All mutation methods follow the same pattern: acquire write-lock, update in-memory index struct, atomically persist index.json via the existing atomic-write helper, then release lock. This ensures no partial states are visible to concurrent readers. UpdateSyncState is called after each push or pull phase to advance the per-provider watermarks. AppendConflict is called during index rebuild when (canonical_id, revision) collisions are detected, with automatic DetectedAt timestamp and deduplication by key. RemoveConflict is called during conflict resolution to remove a collision once the user has chosen which duplicate to keep. The read accessors (Conflicts, SyncState) hold only read-locks and return copies to avoid exposing the live index map. This design satisfies REQ-SE-17's requirement that all mutations go through store-level methods that hold flock for the duration of the write.

title: Store schema extended with sync-state and conflict types (PR-A T-01 through T-04)
content: PR-A implementation begins by extending the store schema with two new data structures: ProviderSyncState for tracking per-provider watermarks (last_pulled_at, last_pushed_at) and record counts, and ConflictEntry/ConflictDuplicate for recording detected (canonical_id, revision) collisions during index rebuild. New sentinel error types ErrCorrupt and ErrSchemaMismatch are added to support error signaling on store corruption and schema version mismatches. The index struct gains two new fields (SyncState and Conflicts) with omitempty JSON tags to maintain backward compatibility with existing index.json files. These types form the foundation for sync-engine's conflict detection and watermark tracking required by REQ-SE-15 through REQ-SE-17 and REQ-SE-33 through REQ-SE-35.

title: Store package baseline gaps vs. sync-engine spec requirements
content: Architecture review of wrapper-mems shows the store package has a solid foundation (index/rebuild/record/schema/lock/recover) with 22 files and comprehensive test coverage. However, all sync-engine features are absent and must be added in PR-A. The baseline store supports append-only JSONL, index rebuild with tiebreak winner selection (lineULID DESC), ByProvider mapping, and recovery from partial writes. The spec requires wrapping this with SyncState watermarks (per-provider last_pushed_at/last_pulled_at), conflict detection and resolution, and sentinel errors for corruption and schema mismatch — none of which exist yet. This aligns with the staged delivery plan: PR-A adds store schema extensions and conflict detection (~300 LOC), then PR-B adds internal/sync/ Engine (~450 LOC), then CLI in PR-C, and push/pull I/O in PR-D/E.

title: Sync-engine specification (PRD-3) formalized with 60+ requirements across 14 sections
content:
The sync-engine specification (obs #91) formalizes the requirements for PRD-3 with 60 numbered requirements (REQ-SE-01 through REQ-SE-65) organized into 14 sections, binding the 12 architecture decisions (D1–D12) from obs #89 proposal and the design details from obs #90 into an implementable contract.

The specification covers the full scope: CLI command surface (init, sync, status, show with flags), state management (SyncState and Conflicts in index.json via flock-guarded store methods), push/pull algorithms with exact field comparison rules and error handling, conflict detection and resolution via dup_index (1-based), mirror-engram shell-out with 30s timeout, failure modes with exit code semantics, Engine API for programmatic access (for PRD-4 TUI), concurrency (single store.Open per run, flock held throughout), and store extensions (new errors, index fields, rebuild logic).

Key design constraints encoded: (D1) tiebreaker uses lineULID not canonical_id, (D2) SyncState+Conflicts in index.json with flock-guarded mutations, (D3) init is idempotent wrapper around store.Open + .gitignore/.config.yaml, (D4) single store.Open for entire sync run, (D5) recordsEqualIgnoringMetadata compares only payload fields (Title, Content, Type, etc.) and ignores all metadata/Origin, (D6) exit code 0/1/2 contract (success/hard-error/conflicts), (D7) unbounded first-run pull with 1000-record warning + --max opt-in, (D8) show defaults to table with --json escape hatch, (D9) engram update gap is silent skip + counter (not hard error), (D10) hexagonal Engine struct, (D11) mirror-engram 30s timeout warning-only, (D12) sync resolve uses dup_index not line numbers.

Scenario-based requirements throughout (e.g., "push skips unchanged record", "collision tiebreak by updated_at", "partial write recovery on next Open is transparent", "concurrent sync exits immediately with lock held message"). Traceability matrix maps each requirement to PRD-3 sections and decisions. Out-of-scope items listed (TUI, engram update path, batch pagination, delete propagation, cross-provider dedup). Seven spec-level assumptions documented where proposal left gaps (ProviderSyncState field set minimal, ConflictEntry.duplicates array structure, RemoveConflict method, commit message format, --conflicts table format, pull watermark on partial failure, run summary to stdout).

The specification is now the single source of truth for implementation: sdd-tasks will break down each requirement (or logical group of requirements per PR) into concrete implementation tasks, and test coverage will be driven by these requirement scenarios.

title: SDD design phase completed for sync-engine (PRD-3); 5-PR delivery strategy with ~2000 LOC identified
content:
The SDD design phase for sync-engine (PRD-3) has been completed by the Opus sub-agent and the design artifact has been persisted as obs #90. The agent synthesized the fixed architectural decisions (D1–D12) from obs #89 (proposal) and the current-state analysis (obs #88) into a concrete, implementable design with specific Go types, package structure, algorithms, and a staged delivery roadmap.

The design confirms the hexagonal architecture (Option A): a new internal/sync/ package containing an Engine struct with Push/Pull/Sync/Status/Resolve methods, backed by a thin cobra CLI in cmd/wrapper-mems/. The Engine holds a *store.Store and a map of Adapter implementations and orchestrates them according to the §6.1 push and §6.2 pull algorithms. Store extensions are purely additive: new syncstate.go file with UpdateSyncState/AppendConflict methods that hold the existing flock for atomic updates to the index; new SyncState and Conflicts fields in the index struct (mapped to JSON); conflict detection logic woven into rebuild.go during the next rebuild; two sentinel errors (ErrCorrupt, ErrSchemaMismatch) in errors.go.

The delivery is sliced into 5 stacked-to-main PRs, totaling ~2000 LOC, which exceeds the 400-line budget and will trigger an ask-on-risk gate after the sdd-tasks phase. Each PR is focused: PR-A on store schema extensions and rebuild conflict detection, PR-B on the Engine core with no CLI (equal, state, config, report modules), PR-C on the cobra skeleton and basic commands, PR-D on the push/pull/mirror algorithms wired to CLI, PR-E on conflict resolution and status polish. Rough LOC estimates per PR allow the tasks phase and the risk gate to make informed decisions about grouping or splitting.

Risks acknowledged in the design: adding conflict-detection logic to the shipped/tested rebuild.go module requires strong regression coverage in PR-A; PR-B builds Engine before push/pull are implemented, so Engine method signatures must stay thin to avoid coupling with stub implementations; the engram update-gap limitation (D9) means that records originating from other sources and pushed into engram once cannot receive content updates on subsequent pulls—they are silently skipped and counted only, documented as a v1 known limitation.

The design is ready to feed into the sdd-tasks phase, which will break down each PR into concrete implementation tasks.

title: Sync-engine (PRD-3) architecture and delivery design finalized
content:
The sync-engine design finalizes the orchestration layer for PRD-3, building on the mature storage (PRD-0) and adapter (PRD-1/2) layers. The Engine struct lives in internal/sync/ and exposes Push/Pull/Sync/Status/Resolve methods callable by both the CLI (cmd/wrapper-mems) and the future TUI (PRD-4). The design confirms architectural option A (vs cmd-driven option B or over-decomposed option C), creating a hexagonal boundary that mirrors the existing internal/adapter/ pattern. 

Key decisions finalized: (D1) tiebreak uses lineULID (already implemented in rebuild.go, cheaper than changing it), (D5) metadata fields (Revision, Origin, timestamps) are ignored in equality checks to avoid spurious revision bumps on re-pulls, (D9) engram WriteNative update gap handled as silent skip + counter (engram CLI v1.15 lacks update verb), (D11) mirror-engram shell-out has 30s timeout and never fails hard (housekeeping only), (D12) conflict resolution uses dupIndex (1-based position in Duplicates array) not line numbers (line numbers shift on rebuild).

The design maps concrete types (ProviderSyncState, ConflictEntry, ConflictDuplicate, RunReport, StatusReport, Options, Config) and algorithms (push loop per §6.1, pull loop per §6.2, conflict detection in rebuild.go, resolve via superseding line append). Package layout is additive: store gains SyncState and Conflicts in index.json plus syncstate.go helper methods; adapters unchanged; new cmd/wrapper-mems/ and internal/sync/ modules are greenfield.

Delivery is sliced into 5 PRs (~2000 LOC total, exceeds 400-line budget so ask-on-risk fires post-sdd-tasks): PR-A adds index schema + rebuild conflict logic + errors, PR-B builds Engine core (equal, state, config, report), PR-C adds CLI skeleton + init/status/show, PR-D wires push/pull/mirror, PR-E adds resolve and status --conflicts polish. Test strategy is strict with fake adapters, temp JSONL fixtures, and PATH-override mock for shell-out.

Risks acknowledged: engram update-path limitation (content changes to records re-exported to engram are silently skipped), conflict detection adds logic to shipped rebuild.go module (needs strong regression tests in PR-A), PR-B stubs push/pull until PR-D (keep Engine methods thin to avoid stale signatures), multi-machine sync_state merge out of scope (SyncState is per-machine, gitignored).

title: Sync-engine (PRD-3) architecture proposal with 12 explicit decisions
content: SDD proposal phase for PRD-3 sync-engine (wrapper-mems) completed successfully. The proposal documents 12 explicit architectural decisions with rationale and rejected alternatives. tiebreak semantics align PRD to shipped rebuild.go behavior (lineULID, not canonical_id). Sync state storage places SyncState and Conflicts inside index.json to maintain single fingerprint and avoid two-writer races, with mutations locked via new store methods. Store locking uses a single open for the entire sync run to atomically cover push, pull, and state updates. Record equality semantics explicitly enumerate payload fields (Title, Content, ContentFormat, Type, TopicKey, Tags, Kind) compared for equality while ignoring metadata fields (Revision, Supersedes, LineULID, SchemaVersion, timestamps, Origin). Exit codes use 0 for success, 1 for hard errors, 2 for unresolved conflicts present. First-run data is unbounded by default with optional --max and a stderr warning at ≥1000 records. show command defaults to table output with --json as escape hatch. Engram CLI v1.15 WriteNative limitation is handled as silent skip with structured warning, documented in the spec as a known constraint. Architecture confirms Option A: internal/sync/Engine struct with push.go, pull.go, state.go, equal.go, mirror.go subfiles, paired with thin CLI wrappers in cmd/wrapper-mems/cmd/. mirror-engram shell-out uses 30s CommandContext timeout and warning-only failure to avoid blocking sync for missing engram CLI. Conflict resolution UX uses --keep <dup_index> (stable references into ConflictEntry.Duplicates array) instead of fragile line numbers. Proposal estimates ~2000 LOC total delivery, triggering ask-on-risk for safe decomposition. Spec and design phases recommended to run in parallel; several details deferred: exact ProviderSyncState field set, ConflictEntry shape, config.yaml schema, shell-out test strategy.

title: SDD proposal stream timeout for sync-engine (PRD-3)
content: The SDD proposal phase for sync-engine (PRD-3) was initiated via sdd-propose subagent, targeting 12 binding decisions (D1-D12) and architecture confirmation based on exploration #88. The agent executed for approximately 46 minutes, completed 8 tool operations including file reads and lookups, but was interrupted by a stream idle timeout before generating the final proposal artifact. The stream terminated with a partial response containing only an error message, leaving the proposal incomplete. This indicates that long-running SDD proposal generation for complex features (sync-engine involves 9+ architectural questions + design tradeoffs) may exceed platform stream timeout tolerances.

title: SDD-explore phase completed for PRD-3 (sync-engine); ready to transition to proposal phase
content: The SDD-explore sub-agent completed a comprehensive investigation of PRD-3 (sync-engine) scope and architecture. The exploration confirmed that PRD-0 (schema) and PRD-1 (adapters) are production-ready with the store supporting Append/AppendBatch, advisory locking via gofrs/flock, fingerprint-based index validation, and crash recovery. The revision sentinel (-1 for existing records) is implemented identically in both adapters. The exploration identified that PRD-3 is entirely greenfield at the CLI and orchestration layers—there is no cmd/wrapper-mems/ entrypoint or orchestration code yet. Four modules need creation (cmd/wrapper-mems and internal/sync package with sub-files) and four need targeted modifications (index.go to add sync_state and conflicts, rebuild.go for collision detection, errors.go for new sentinels, go.mod to add cobra). Nine open questions were cataloged for the proposal phase, including critical architectural decisions about tiebreak rule alignment (spec vs implementation) and whether sync_state lives in index.json or a separate file. Three architectural approaches were evaluated; Option A (internal/sync/Engine struct) was recommended as the natural fit that mirrors the existing internal/adapter/ pattern, enables programmatic interface for PRD-4 (TUI), and keeps CLI commands thin. Five risks were documented, with the two-writer race on index.json (if sync updates watermarks independently while store.Close updates fingerprint) marked as the highest-priority issue to resolve in proposal. The artifact is saved and the project is ready to transition to the proposal phase.

title: PRD-3 exploration completed and saved; ready for proposal phase
content: User completed the SDD exploration phase for PRD-3 (sync engine). The exploration document comprehensively maps the current state (PRDs 0-2 built, PRD-3 CLI net-new), affected modules (cmd/, internal/sync/, index.go modifications), and key constraints from prior decisions (Revision=-1 sentinel, append-only invariant, advisory lockfile via gofrs/flock). Nine open questions are explicit: whether the tiebreak rule should use canonical_id (spec) or lineULID (implementation), whether sync_state lives in index.json or separate sync_state.json file, what fields constitute "metadata" for the equality check, CLI exit code semantics for partial-provider success, etc. Six risks are documented, including a two-writer race on index.json if sync updates watermarks directly, and a gap where engram CLI v1.15 has no update path so pull cannot re-import records that changed in canonical and were already pulled once. Three architectural approaches were evaluated; Option A (flat internal/sync/Engine package) is recommended as the natural fit that mirrors the existing internal/adapter/ pattern, creates a clean seam for PRD-4 (TUI), and keeps the CLI thin. The exploration is complete and saved (obs #88); the next phase (proposal) should resolve the nine open questions and flesh out the Engine API surface.

title: Complete codebase review: PRD-0/1 implementations and PRD-3 foundation analysis
content: User completed comprehensive codebase review to understand the complete implementation of PRD-0 (canonical schema) and PRD-1 (adapters) before designing PRD-3 (sync engine). Key architectural insights: (1) The store is append-only with crash recovery, two-pass index rebuild, and deterministic tiebreak logic for collision resolution that prioritizes revision descending, then updated_at descending (leveraging RFC3339 byte-comparable format), then line_ulid descending. (2) Engram adapter fixed DEFECT-LN-01 by switching from CLI search (which rejected empty queries in v1.15) to HTTP GET /export for full deterministic enumeration. (3) Claude-mem adapter handles read-only constraint by returning ErrUnsupported from WriteNative and managing append-only semantics (no updated_at field). (4) Both adapters signal "existing record" to the sync orchestrator via shared revision sentinel (-1), allowing PRD-3 to compute increment atomically rather than having adapters track state. (5) The store enforces O(1) provider-ID lookups via ProviderIDMap snapshots, exposed through a boundary wrapper to avoid coupling internal/store with internal/adapter. This foundation allows PRD-3 to implement watermark-driven incremental sync, conflict detection via the existing tiebreak invariant, and clean error handling for degraded providers.

title: Transition to PRD-3 (Sync Engine) phase with specification review
content: User is transitioning from PRD-2 (claude-mem adapter) completion to PRD-3 (sync engine) development phase. They reviewed the full PRD-3 specification, which defines a lightweight sync orchestrator for moving observations between providers and the canonical JSONL store. The spec covers deterministic command surface (init, sync pull/push, status, show), watermark-based incremental sync, conflict detection via tiebreaker rules, graceful degradation on provider failures, and explicit non-goals (no real-time daemons, no conflict UI, no remote backend sync in v1). User also examined the sdd-explore skill definition to understand the structured exploration phase methodology for design questions. This confirms readiness to begin the SDD cycle for PRD-3.

title: Memory tools available through MCP plugin namespace
content: When searching for memory-related tools in the wrapper-mems project, an initial search using generic selectors (mem_search, mem_get_observation) returned no matches. A subsequent search using the fully qualified MCP plugin namespace path (mcp__plugin_engram_engram__mem_search, mcp__plugin_engram_engram__mem_get_observation) successfully resolved both tools. This indicates that memory tools in this system are organized under the MCP plugin engram namespace and require that full path qualification to be discovered and accessed. The system maintains 68 total deferred tools across all namespaces.

title: Claude Mem API Per-ID Endpoint Uses Query Parameter, Not Path Style
content: The Claude Mem API endpoint for fetching individual observations by ID uses query parameter syntax rather than RESTful path parameters. The endpoint returns a paginated envelope structure requiring unwrapping. This routing mismatch caused initial adapter integration test failures. The transport layer now correctly handles envelope unwrapping and missing routes by degrading to scan operations.

title: Claude Mem Record Schema Reworked to Match Live API Shape
content: The adapter struct and canonical mapping logic were completely reworked to align with the actual live Claude Mem API schema discovered during integration testing. The live API returns a minimal observation record with integer IDs, a single project name field, type vocabulary, narrative content, and creation timestamps only. Fields like UpdatedAt and Tags present in the provisional struct do not exist in the live schema. Filtering and transformation logic was rewritten accordingly with comprehensive test coverage.

title: Claude Mem Adapter Pre-Merge Gate Discharged—PRD-2 Complete
content: PRD-2 (Claude Mem adapter) pre-merge gate has been successfully discharged. The adapter now correctly interfaces with the live Claude Mem API after resolving schema mismatches and endpoint routing issues. Integration tests provide proof of contract compliance against a live worker instance. The work is merged to master and ready for downstream consumption. PRD-3 (orchestrator) can now proceed with a validated claude-mem adapter contract.

title: Claude Mem Adapter API Shape Validation Fixed and Merged to Master
content: The wrapper-mems adapter implementation for Claude Mem underwent significant refactoring to ensure correct handling of the live API response shape. A pre-merge validation gate confirmed the adapter's interface matches the actual API contract. Integration tests received substantial updates indicating improved coverage of API interaction scenarios and edge cases. The transport layer and core adapter logic were both modified, suggesting both the communication layer and response handling were corrected.

title: Update TestToCanonical_HappyPath test to match verified implementation decisions
content: The TestToCanonical_HappyPath test was updated to reflect the verified schema and implementation decisions finalized on 2026-05-20. The test fixture construction now uses the verified makeRec signature with int64 id and verified field names. Test assertions were rewritten to validate the rework decisions: Type field preservation (decision 1A, passing through claude-mem's discovery/feature/bugfix/decision vocabulary as a sub-classification within Kind=session_summary); empty tags (decision D4, recognizing claude-mem has no native tags); and UpdatedAt mirroring CreatedAt (decision D7, reflecting that claude-mem records are append-only with no updated_at field). These changes ensure the test validates the actual behavior the implementation provides, not the provisional assumptions from design #77.

title: Update test fixture helper makeRawItem for verified schema and add idStr convenience
content: Test fixture helper makeRawItem was refactored to match the verified live API schema discovered on 2026-05-20. The function now accepts numeric id (int64) and createdAt as parameters (instead of hardcoded zero value), eliminating fixture data inconsistencies. Field name changes in the marshaled record (ProjectID→Project, Summary→Narrative, removal of non-existent UpdatedAt) bring test fixtures in line with actual API response structure. A convenience helper idStr was added to simplify test code that compares adapter.NativeID (which is a string representation of the numeric id) against int64 values—this reduces boilerplate in tests that work with both representations. These changes are purely infrastructural; test logic and assertions remain unchanged, only the test data construction is corrected to reflect the verified schema.

title: Update unit test helper claudemem_test.go to match verified schema
content: Unit test file claudemem_test.go was reviewed and found to already contain comprehensive test coverage (~58 test functions across 1389 lines). The makeRec helper function, which constructs test fixtures, was updated to use the verified live API schema discovered on 2026-05-20 (id as int64, memorySessionID field, project name, type vocabulary, narrative field, created_at only—no updated_at, no tags). This change aligns test fixtures with the implementation rewrite completed in the previous task. Test structure remains intact: Transport-level tests, adapter contract verification, pure function assertions (panicTransport proves no I/O), and I/O method tests with controlled fake transport. The fakeTransport mechanism allows scripted multi-page responses and configurable errors, enabling deterministic testing of pagination, degradation (ADR-5), and error mapping without real network calls.

title: Rewrite claudemem.go adapter implementation against verified schema from live worker
content: Implementation of claudemem.go proceeded with verified schema from live worker (tested 2026-05-20). The initial design #77 used PROVISIONAL field names pending reconfirmation against a live instance; the rework discharges that gate by implementing against the real API shape discovered during live testing. Key divergences from provisional design: claude-mem ID is numeric (int64), not string; the project filter is on `project` field (project name) not `project_id`; the session ID field is `memory_session_id` not `session_id`; narrative content is in `narrative` not `summary`; the API has no `updated_at` field (records are append-only), forcing the since-filter to rebase on `created_at` and canonical UpdatedAt to mirror CreatedAt. Type field (discovery/feature/bugfix/decision/session_summary/etc.) is now preserved in the canonical record (D1 decision, refining initial design assumption that Type would be empty). All struct field names and mappings align to the verified live schema; PRE-MERGE GATE comments are removed, signalling that the gate is discharged. The Revision sentinel follows ADR-12 (shared cross-adapter convention with engram), enabling PRD-3 orchestrator to use a single uniform code path for both adapters. Implementation is pure (no I/O in ToCanonical/FromCanonical), read-only (WriteNative→ErrUnsupported), and maintains CON-01 boundary (IDMap wrapped, no store import).

title: Claude-mem adapter contract decisions finalized after live worker shape discovery
content: Live worker re-testing on 2026-05-20 revealed the actual claude-mem record schema, which differed from initial design assumptions in design #77. Key divergences: absence of updated_at (records immutable after creation), absence of native tags, and stringified JSON for structured fields. User confirmed four rework decisions (D1–D4) that address these discoveries and finalize the adapter implementation contract. D1 preserves the Type vocabulary for claude-mem within the session_summary Kind, maintaining ADR-9 semantic honesty while retaining information. D2 adapts the since-filter to created_at and uses the cross-adapter revision sentinel (-1/1) to maintain uniform PRD-3 orchestration with engram. D3 focuses v1 on narrative-only content, deferring structured field surfacing to PRD-3 decision. D4 rejects tag synthesis as speculative. All decisions minimize pre-merge risk and maintain architectural consistency (ADR-9 Kind constraint, ADR-12 revision sentinel).

title: Integration Test Pins Verified claude-mem API Schema and Gates Merge
content: Primary session examined the integration test (integration_test.go) which implements the pre-merge gate for PRD-2. The test file documents the verified real API schema pinned from observation #82 and intentionally fails to block the 4-PR feature chain (PR#1–PR#4) from merging until the provisional schema is reworked. The test validates envelope shape (items, hasMore, offset, limit) and item shape (10 verified fields), and explicitly checks that known-absent fields (tags, updated_at, session_id, project_id, summary) are absent from real responses. The primary finding: `id` field in real API is a JSON number, but claudeMemRecord.ID is declared as string, causing json.Unmarshal to silently produce zero-value strings and breaking all ID-based lookups. The gate is deliberate and necessary: it prevents downstream PRD-3 (orchestrator) from being built on a broken adapter contract. Rework scope is explicit: rewrite claudeMemRecord struct with correct field names/types, rewrite ToCanonical, revise specs and design docs, re-run test to discharge gate.

title: claudeMemRecord Struct Field Names Contradicted by Real API
content: Primary session examined claudemem.go implementation and discovered that the entire adapter was built on provisional field names that contradict the verified real API schema. The claudeMemRecord struct uses json tags for project_id, session_id, summary, tags, and updated_at — none of which exist in the actual /api/observations response. The implementation's ToCanonical method maps rec.ProjectID and rec.SessionID to Origin fields, but real data comes in as project (name) and memory_session_id. The ListNative method filters by rec.ProjectID and validates rec.UpdatedAt, both of which are absent in real responses, making the filtering logic incorrect. This is the contract mismatch the pre-merge gate catches. Every json tag line is annotated with `// PRE-MERGE GATE`, indicating the developers were aware these were provisional and needed verification.

title: PRD-2 Integration Gate Blocking Merge Due to Schema Mismatches
content: Primary session probed the live claude-mem API endpoint (/api/observations) and discovered active integration gate blocking PRD-2. The gate failure (obs #265) documents systematic field name and type mismatches between the provisional `claudeMemRecord` struct and the actual /api/observations response. The integration test in PR#4 explicitly validated against the live worker and pinned the real schema. The gate deliberately blocks the 4-PR feature series (PR#1–PR#4) from merging until the adapter contract is corrected. Concrete rework scope: rewrite `claudeMemRecord` struct with correct field names/types, update `ToCanonical` mapping, revise specs #76 and #77, and re-run integration test to clear the gate. This blocks PRD-3 (orchestrator) work downstream.

title: PRD-2 Integration Gate Failed: Live claude-mem API Shape Contradicts Provisional Mapping
content:
Integration testing PRD-2 (claude-mem-adapter) against the live claude-mem worker revealed systematic misalignment between the provisional `claudeMemRecord` struct definition and the actual `/api/observations` response shape. The gate tests connected, fetched real observations, and conclusively verified that the provisional claudeMemRecord struct mapping in PR#1/PR#2 is fundamentally broken. title: Integration test (PR#4 T-16) implements pre-merge gate mechanism; codifies field mapping mismatch
content: PR#4 task T-16 implemented the integration test mechanism that discharges the pre-merge gate (spec REQ-CM-20, design §11). The test probes a live claude-mem worker instance (defaulting to localhost:37701 via ADR-3 fallback) and validates that the real JSON response shape matches a hardcoded verified contract pinned from observation #82. The test discovers and asserts the primary hard-break: the real API returns id as a JSON number, but the provisional claudeMemRecord struct declares ID as string with json:"id,omitempty" — this causes json.Unmarshal to silently produce empty strings for all IDs, breaking ListNative and ReadNative entirely. The test also confirms that the known-absent fields (updated_at, session_id, project_id, summary, tags) are definitively absent, meaning the provisional field mapping in REQ-CM-11 and the spec's assumptions about incremental updated_at filtering are fundamentally wrong. The gate verdict is hardcoded as FAILED, with a structured comment documenting the exact rework required in each artifact (PR#1, PR#2, spec, design) before merge approval. The test skips gracefully on unreachable worker (no CI blocker), enabling safe merge-gate behavior in offline environments.

title: PRE-MERGE GATE TRIPPED: Real claude-mem JSON shape contradicts provisional adapter mapping

title: PR#3 T-15 — Comprehensive unit tests for Health, ListNative, ReadNative (fakeTransport + 16 test cases)
content:
PR#3 T-15 implements comprehensive unit tests for all three I/O methods (Health, ListNative, ReadNative) using a configurable fakeTransport that enables deterministic testing without network I/O. The test infrastructure consists of:

1. **fakeTransport** (configurable fake): Records ListPage calls for verification, supports scripted page responses, toggleable GetByID behavior, and error injection. Implements Transport interface to satisfy compile-time assertion.

2. **Helpers**: `buildPages` constructs observationsPage slices with proper HasMore semantics (last page=false, earlier pages=true); `makeRawItem` encodes test claudeMemRecord objects with consistent structure.

**Health Tests (4 tests, T-12)**:
- `TestHealth_NilTransport_ErrUnavailable`: Nil transport returns ErrUnavailable immediately.
- `TestHealth_OK`: Nil error from transport → nil returned.
- `TestHealth_TransportError_WrapsErrUnavailable`: Transport errors wrapped in ErrUnavailable.
- `TestHealth_ContextCancelled_Propagates`: Context cancellation propagates raw (not wrapped).

**ListNative Tests (6 tests, T-13)**:
- `TestListNative_ThreePagePagination_ProjectFilter` (Scenario D): 3 pages, 60+40+100+100+50 total records; project filter (strict equality) returns 210 my-project IDs; 40+100+50 = 190 other-project records discarded. Verifies 3 ListPage calls at offsets 0/100/200 with limit=100 each.
- `TestListNative_SinceFilter` (Scenario E): 10 records total, 4 before since cutoff, 6 at/after. Verifies exact timestamp comparison boundary and filtration.
- `TestListNative_CrossProjectIsolation` (Scenario G): 7 intermixed records (proj-A, proj-B, proj-C). Returns only 3 proj-B IDs; zero leakage from A/C.
- `TestListNative_TimestampNormalization` (Scenario H): SQLite format "2026-05-16 15:04:05" normalized and passed since filter against RFC3339 cutoff.
- `TestListNative_UnparseableUpdatedAt_Skipped` (ADR-6): Bad timestamp record skipped (permissive), good record retained. Zero records with unparseable timestamps stored.
- `TestListNative_ContextCancellation_Aborts`: Context cancellation propagates; no partial result.

**ReadNative Tests (6 tests, T-14)**:
- `TestReadNative_HappyPath_PerID`: GetByID succeeds, record unmarshaled, Raw field populated, returned.
- `TestReadNative_CleanNotFound_ErrNotFound` (ADR-5): Clean 404 from GetByID → ErrNotFound immediately, no degrade.
- `TestReadNative_DegradeToScan_Found` (ADR-5): GetByID returns ErrUnavailable (endpoint missing/5xx) → degrade path activates, paginate+scan finds record on second item of first page.
- `TestReadNative_DegradeToScan_Exhausted_ErrNotFound` (ADR-5): Degrade path scans 2-item page, record absent → ErrNotFound after exhaustion.
- `TestReadNative_NilTransport_ErrUnavailable`: Nil transport → ErrUnavailable.
- `TestReadNative_DegradeToScan_TransportError_ErrUnavailable`: Transport error during degrade scan → ErrUnavailable.

All tests use fakeTransport to isolate behavior verification from network I/O, avoiding flakiness and enabling exhaustive error path testing. The test suite validates both happy paths and all error modes (context cancellation, transport unavailability, parse failures, degrade fallback, cross-project isolation, timestamp normalization). Test code totals ~477 lines, bringing the full claudemem_test.go to ~1,300+ lines across PR#1 (transport + resolveBaseURL + Name + WriteNative), PR#2 (pure functions + 30 cases), and PR#3 (I/O methods + 16 cases).

With T-15 complete, all three I/O methods (Health, ListNative, ReadNative) and their unit tests are shipped. Only T-16 (integration test with real claude-mem instance) and T-17 (pre-merge gate checklist) remain for PR#4.

title: PR#3 T-14 — ReadNative with per-ID lookup and degrade-to-scan fallback
content:
ReadNative (T-14) completes the I/O layer of the claude-mem adapter with a resilient per-ID lookup strategy. The design (ADR-5, explore Q2) acknowledges that the GET /api/observations/:id endpoint is unverified: it may not exist in the live claude-mem API, it may return 5xx under load, or it may be missing entirely in some deployments. Rather than failing hard on endpoint unavailability, ReadNative tries the fast path first and gracefully degrades to a full paginated scan when the endpoint is unreachable.

The primary path (ADR-5) invokes transport.GetByID(ctx, id), which returns (body, found, err). Three error cases are handled:
1. **Context error (Canceled/DeadlineExceeded)**: Returned raw immediately, bypassing the degrade check. The context signal propagates cleanly through the call stack.
2. **Clean 404 (ErrNotFound)**: Indicates the per-ID endpoint exists but the record doesn't. This is a definitive not-found; no degrade happens. The adapter returns ErrNotFound immediately.
3. **ErrUnavailable** (connection refused, 5xx, missing endpoint): Transport signals that the endpoint is unreachable or permanently broken. Execution falls through to the degrade path.

On successful primary path (found=true, err=nil), the record is unmarshaled, the Raw field is populated from the response body, and the record is returned immediately.

The degrade path activates when the primary per-ID endpoint fails with ErrUnavailable. It reuses the same pagination loop as ListNative (limit=100, offset=0/100/200/...) and scans through all pages seeking a record with ID matching the requested id. For each page, items are decoded and compared against the target ID. Item-level decode failures are skipped silently (continue), implementing REQ-CM-18 permissive parsing. If the page-level observationsPage envelope fails to decode, the scan aborts and returns ErrUnavailable (signalling a provider problem, not a data shape issue). When a matching record is found, its Raw field is populated from the raw item JSON and the record is returned.

If all pages are exhausted (hasMore=false on the final page) without finding the record, ErrNotFound is returned. This is a definitive not-found after a complete scan, semantically equivalent to the clean 404 from the primary path.

Error propagation distinguishes between fatal (context errors, transport failures) and recoverable (item parse failures) errors, maintaining the design §8 contract that context signals propagate transparently and provider unavailability is always mapped to ErrUnavailable. The degrade strategy trades latency for availability: a full scan of the observations table is O(n) but always works when the list endpoint works, enabling the adapter to survive per-ID endpoint outages without data loss.

With ReadNative complete, all three I/O methods (Health, ListNative, ReadNative) are now implemented. `go build ./...` compiles cleanly, confirming no panics remain. T-15 (unit tests for all three methods) completes PR#3.

title: PR#3 T-12/T-13 — Health probe and ListNative pagination with full filtering
content:
PR#3 T-12 and T-13 implement the Health probe and full-pagination ListNative method for the claude-mem adapter. Health (T-12) is a simple delegation to Transport.Health(ctx) with nil-guard and proper error propagation: context cancellation and deadline exceeded are returned raw (not wrapped), while connection failures and non-2xx responses are wrapped in adapter.ErrUnavailable. This maintains the design §8 contract that context errors propagate transparently while all transport-level issues resolve to the same ErrUnavailable sentinel.

ListNative (T-13) implements the core data ingest path: pagination over GET /api/observations with limit=100, offset-based continuation until hasMore==false. The implementation enforces four critical behaviors:

1. **Nil-guard**: Returns ErrUnavailable immediately if transport is nil, matching design §8 (no panic on missing transport).

2. **Project isolation** (REQ-CM-07, ADR-11): After decoding each claudeMemRecord, it filters by strict equality of ProjectID against the requested project parameter. This is a hard boundary—no substring match, no prefix match—ensuring that a single worker holding multiple projects never leaks observations across project boundaries. Records from other projects are silently skipped (continue, no error).

3. **Temporal filtering** (REQ-CM-08, REQ-TS-03): The since timestamp is pre-normalized once to rfc3339NanoFixed (UTC, 9-digit nanoseconds with Z suffix) before the pagination loop. Each record's normalized updated_at is then compared lexicographically (string < comparison), exploiting the byte-comparable invariant to avoid parsing overhead per item and to ensure the filter honors chronological ordering. Records with unparseable updated_at are skipped with a WARN log, never stored—adhering to ADR-6 (non-silent failure) and DEFECT-LN-01 (silent timestamp mismatches corrupt tiebreakWinner).

4. **Forensic logging** (REQ-CM-17, §9): The first observation decoded from the entire list is logged at INFO with its raw JSON bytes. This is the artifact the pre-merge gate (REQ-CM-20, T-17) inspects to verify that the provisional JSON field names (id, project_id, session_id, title, summary, tags, created_at, updated_at, items, hasMore) match the real shape of a live claude-mem instance. Subsequent observations are not logged, avoiding log spam.

Error handling distinguishes between decode failures (permissive, skip + warn per REQ-CM-18) and page-level failures (non-permissive, return ErrUnavailable): if the observationsPage structure itself cannot be decoded, the entire list operation fails with ErrUnavailable (indicating a provider problem, not a data shape issue). Item-level decode failures return no error to the caller but log a WARN and continue, implementing the tolerant parsing mandated by the provisional JSON spec.

Together, Health and ListNative complete the read surface of PR#3. ReadNative (T-14, degrade-to-scan on per-ID endpoint failure) and unit tests for all three methods remain.

title: PR#1 & PR#2 claude-mem adapter core implementation (ToCanonical, FromCanonical, Transport, unit tests)
content:
The claude-mem adapter PR#1 and PR#2 implement the foundational data layer and test seam for reading session narratives from the claude-mem memory daemon over HTTP. PR#1 establishes the Transport abstraction and port resolution (supervisor.json → fallback 37701), enabling unit tests via a fake transport and production via httpTransport with defensive 10s timeout. PR#2 adds the native record struct (claudeMemRecord with PROVISIONAL JSON tags gated by PRE-MERGE GATE comments) and the core mapping logic (ToCanonical/FromCanonical). 

ToCanonical implements the full REQ-CM-11 field mapping: native id → origin.provider_id, session_id → origin.session_id, title/summary → title/content with automatic derivation when title is absent, timestamps normalized to RFC3339Nano with Z suffix via rfc3339NanoFixed to protect byte-comparable ordering (REQ-TS-03). IDMap resolution assigns either an empty CanonicalID (new record, store mints ULID on Append) or a pre-existing mapping (revision sentinel -1, PRD-3 orchestrator computes final priorRevision+1). The revision sentinel was resolved 2026-05-18 (Decision B, obs #81) to align claude-mem and engram adapters on a shared -1 convention, enabling PRD-3 to handle all adapters through a single `revision < 0` branch instead of provider-specific logic.

FromCanonical is implemented pure but intentionally omits timestamps—read-only v1 has no write surface, and any future write path must re-read CreatedAt/UpdatedAt from the provider rather than round-trip through FromCanonical, which would break timestamp freshness guarantees.

Timestamp normalization tolerates 3 layouts (RFC3339Nano, RFC3339, SQLite datetime) per ADR-6—claude-mem's datetime format is unverified, and DEFECT-LN-01 proved that silent format mismatches silently corrupt the byte-comparable invariant. Unknown formats are rejected in ToCanonical (error returned, record never stored) and skipped in ListNative (WARN logged, record omitted).

PR#2 post-review fixes included resolving 2 CRITICAL + 3 WARNING findings: normalizeTimestamp now wraps errors with %w for composable error chains (CRITICAL-01); revision sentinel changed from 2 → -1 for known records to match engram (CRITICAL-02); fixed reversed strings.HasPrefix args and added explicit truncation test (WARNING-01); added panicTransport interface guard (WARNING-02); added note on FromCanonical timestamp omission contract (WARNING-03).

Unit tests (45+ across both PRs) use table-driven approach and prove purity via panicTransport—any I/O attempt during ToCanonical/FromCanonical panics, verifying that the pure functions never call Transport. Error mapping tests verify ErrUnavailable on connection failure/non-2xx, ErrNotFound on clean 404 (GetByID), ErrUnsupported on WriteNative. Timestamp tests assert byte-comparable normalization across layouts. IDMap tests verify new-record (empty CanonicalID, revision=1) and known-record (CanonicalID set, revision=-1 sentinel) paths.

PR#3 (T-12/T-13/T-14) will implement Health (HTTP GET /health), ListNative (paginated GET /api/observations with project/since filters), and ReadNative (per-ID endpoint with degrade-to-scan fallback per ADR-5), plus unit tests covering all three methods (~230 lines). PR#4 will be the integration test (pre-merge gate), querying a real claude-mem instance and dumping raw JSON to verify field names reconfirm against live data (REQ-CM-20 hard blocker).

title: Apply-Progress Updated: PR#2 Amendment Finalized with SHA 7d32adb
content: The apply-progress artifact is finalized with the correct amended SHA for PR#2, closing the SDD artifact reconciliation loop. The commit lineage (dc6b8d5 → 163d496 → 7d32adb) documents the three stages: initial implementation, post-review fixes, and Decision B comment amendment. All verification checks are clean and recorded. The artifact now serves as the authoritative progress record for the claude-mem adapter implementation (PR#1 and PR#2), with the contract conflict resolved and documented across design #77, spec #76, code (claudemem.go), and decision (obs #81). The pre-merge gate remains open pending integration test execution (PR#3/PR#4).

title: PR#2 Amended: Decision B Comment Change Committed
content: PR#2 is amended with the Decision B comment revision. The code comment explaining the revision sentinel (-1 for known records) is updated from a "CONTRACT CONFLICT" narrative to a "DECISION 2026-05-18 (RESOLVED)" narrative, with explicit cross-references to design ADR-12 (obs #77) and spec REQ-CM-11/14 (obs #76). The amendment preserves all existing code logic and test coverage; only the comment explanation is updated to reflect the resolved decision. This amendment finalizes the SDD artifact reconciliation: design, spec, code comment, apply-progress, and Decision B artifact are now all aligned to the shared -1 revision sentinel convention. The new SHA (`7d32adb...`) should replace the pending notation in apply-progress #80.

title: SDD Reconciliation Verified: Build/Vet/Test Clean, Ready for Final Commit
content: The final verification confirms that all SDD reconciliation changes—design #77 (ADR-12, §6 revision), spec #76 (REQ-CM-11/14, scenarios), code comment in claudemem.go (DECISION 2026-05-18), apply-progress #80 (RESOLVED status), and Decision B artifact (obs #81)—are syntactically correct and do not break any existing tests. The claudemem adapter test suite (30 unit tests) ran fresh in 0.467s, confirming the code comment change is valid. No merge conflicts, no test failures, no build errors. The change is ready for `git commit --amend` to finalize the PR#2 work-unit with the Decision B resolution applied across all artifacts.

title: SDD Artifact Reconciliation Completed: Decision B Applied Across Design, Spec, Code, and Progress
content: A comprehensive SDD artifact reconciliation was executed to bring all design, spec, code, and progress artifacts into alignment with the shared -1 revision sentinel decision (Decision B). The task coordinated updates across five separate artifacts: (1) design doc added ADR-12 and revised the Revision rationale to explain the shared sentinel; (2) spec doc rewrote REQ-CM-11 and REQ-CM-14 to mandate -1 for known records and corrected acceptance scenarios; (3) code comment in claudemem.go was changed from "CONTRACT CONFLICT" framing to "DECISION 2026-05-18 (RESOLVED)" with cross-references to the design and spec updates; (4) apply-progress was updated to mark the contract conflict as RESOLVED; (5) Decision B was formally saved as a decision artifact explaining the rationale and implications. All changes enforce the uniform convention: new records → Revision 1, known records → Revision -1 (shared cross-adapter sentinel signaling PRD-3 to compute priorRevision+1). The reconciliation preserves the distinction between design intent (what was originally proposed), spec requirements (what must be implemented), code comment (why it is this way), and progress tracking (where we are now). No artifacts contain conflicting guidance; the -1 sentinel convention is now consistently documented across the entire SDD ecosystem.

title: Decision B Formally Recorded: Shared -1 Sentinel for Revision Across All Adapters
content: Decision B is formally recorded as a standalone decision artifact documenting why claude-mem uses the shared -1 sentinel for known records instead of the originally designed "always 1" approach. The decision captures the definitive constraint: the pure adapter layer cannot compute priorRevision+1 because the IDMap interface intentionally does not expose the existing revision (to prevent bidirectional coupling with the store). This is the W-03 trap—any attempt to read the prior revision would violate the pure-function guarantee required by REQ-CM-10. Instead of designing around this constraint, the better choice is to adopt the engram adapter's proven pattern: -1 sentinel signals the PRD-3 orchestrator to assign the real revision. This enables cross-adapter uniformity and gives PRD-3 a single code path. The decision explicitly acknowledges the design evolution and explains why fresh-context review (CRITICAL-02) was right to flag the inconsistency. This artifact serves as a reference for understanding the adapter contract's true value: not individual adapter features, but consistent behavior across all adapters.

title: Apply-Progress Updated: Decision B Revision Sentinel Resolution Documented
content: The apply-progress artifact is updated to formally document the Decision B resolution of the revision sentinel conflict. The narrative now records: what the conflict was (design intent vs. review requirement), when it was resolved (2026-05-18), which artifacts were updated (design #77, spec #76, code comment in claudemem.go), and the rationale for the chosen resolution (cross-adapter consistency via shared -1 sentinel enables PRD-3 to handle all adapters through a single code path). The update includes a verification note confirming that builds, vetting, and tests are clean before the final commit amendment. This creates a durable record of the decision-making process for future maintainers and auditors investigating why the revision sentinel convention is what it is.

title: Spec Updated: -1 Sentinel Formalized as Requirement for Known Records
content: The specification artifact (obs #76) is updated to formalize the -1 sentinel as a hard requirement for known records across both adapters. The key requirement updates—REQ-CM-11 and REQ-CM-14—now explicitly document that the adapter MUST return -1 for known records (not some incremented value) because the pure adapter cannot read the prior revision from IDMap (the W-03 trap). This makes the -1 sentinel a spec-level contract, not just an implementation detail. The corresponding acceptance scenarios (I and K) are corrected to assert the -1 value and explain that PRD-3 (the orchestrator) is responsible for computing the real incremented revision on re-ingest. This alignment ensures implementers understand that the -1 sentinel is intentional, shared across all adapters, and part of the boundary contract with the orchestrator.

title: ADR-12: Revision Sentinel Cross-Adapter Consistency Documented in Design
content: The design artifact for the claude-mem adapter (PRD-2) is updated to formally document ADR-12, a critical cross-adapter consistency decision made during the implementation review. The decision resolves the earlier design intent ("always Revision 1") with the engram adapter's proven pattern (shared -1 sentinel for known records). ADR-12 is now explicitly listed in the architecture decisions table with full rationale: the shared sentinel enables PRD-3 (the orchestrator) to handle all adapters through a single `revision < 0` code branch, avoiding duplication and keeping the adapter layer pure (no store read required). The timestamp "DECISION 2026-05-18" and the phrase "supersedes prior" make the design evolution transparent to future readers. This ensures consistency across the adapter ecosystem and documents why the -1 sentinel is essential to the adapter abstraction's correctness.

title: Revision Sentinel Comment Refactored from Conflict to Resolved Decision
content: Post-review documentation cleanup in `claudemem.go` transforms the inline comment explaining the revision sentinel from a conflict-resolution narrative ("we had a conflict, here's why we chose X") into a cleaner decision narrative ("here's the resolved convention across both adapters"). The substance is unchanged—the code still uses -1 sentinel for known records—but the framing is now more professional and forward-looking, making it easier for future readers to understand the rationale without having to parse a conflict history. The comment explicitly cross-references the design artifact (obs #77) and spec requirements (obs #76) where the reconciliation occurred.

title: Revision Sentinel Alignment: claude-mem Adapter Aligns to engram Cross-Adapter Convention
content: During review of the claude-mem adapter implementation, a design-vs.-contract conflict emerged: the design document specified that claude-mem v1 would always set Revision:1 and defer all revision bumping to the orchestrator, diverging from engram's -1 sentinel pattern. However, a review instruction (CRITICAL-02) requires cross-adapter consistency to ensure the orchestrator (PRD-3) can treat both adapters' revision signaling identically. The implementation resolves this by adopting engram's -1 sentinel for known records (indicated by IDMap presence), ensuring uniform orchestration logic across both adapters. The code explicitly documents this conflict and the resolution rationale, preserving the decision context for future maintainers.

title: CONTRACT CONFLICT: claude-mem Revision Encoding vs. engram Sentinel
content: During code review, a conflict surfaced between the claude-mem adapter specification and its implementation regarding revision field encoding. The design spec (PRD-2 §6, ADR-9) explicitly states that claude-mem v1 will set Revision=1 always and defer all revision bumping to the PRD-3 orchestrator, simplifying the read-only adapter's contract. However, the code review instruction CRITICAL-02 mandates cross-adapter consistency by using the -1 sentinel (matching engram's pattern where -1 signals "known record, orchestrator must increment"). The code currently implements the -1 sentinel approach (claudemem.go:317-318), effectively overriding the design spec. This unresolved conflict must be settled before merging: either the design spec must be updated to reflect the -1 decision, or the code must revert to always-1 and the orchestrator (PRD-3) must be designed to handle the two different revision strategies per adapter.

title: claude-mem Adapter Pre-Merge JSON Field Mapping Gate (HARD BLOCKER)
content: The PRD-2 specification for the claude-mem adapter (read-only v1) establishes a HARD pre-merge gate blocking any apply/merge until the JSON field mapping from claude-mem's /api/observations response is verified against a live instance. This decision reflects deep uncertainty about claude-mem's actual HTTP API schema (marked UNVERIFIED in explore phase). The spec makes this gate explicit and enforceable: the implementation must log the raw JSON of the first observation seen, an integration test must dump this raw output, and a human must compare the actual field names against the provisional mapping in REQ-CM-11 before approval. If field names mismatch (e.g., "project_id" vs "project"), the permissive struct design (all fields omitempty, Raw capture) makes forensic logging possible but the record will silently fail field binding, triggering the "empty title and summary" WARN log (REQ-CM-13). This gate is intentionally placed at the specification level, not deferred to testing, to prevent accidental deployment of a read with wrong field names.

title: claude-mem Adapter Architecture: 14 ADRs + 20 Detailed Requirements
content: The claude-mem adapter design (PRD-2, obs #77) is a mature architecture document spanning 14 architecture decision records (ADRs) and laying out 20 formal requirements with 13 acceptance scenarios. The design explicitly acknowledges six intentional divergences from the engram adapter pattern: (D1) HTTP-only with no Commander, (D2) read-only with single-line WriteNative, (D3) Health via /health not CLI version probe, (D4) kind always "session_summary" not conditional, (D5) provisional JSON field tags (pre-merge gate), (D6) pagination-based ListNative not single export dump. The architecture mirrors engram's package structure and Transport seam pattern but simplifies for read-only semantics. ADR-5 is pragmatic resilience: try fast per-ID lookup, gracefully degrade to full scan if that endpoint is unverified/missing. ADR-6 tackles DEFECT-LN-01 (timestamp format mismatch corrupts REQ-TS-03 byte-comparable invariant) with tolerant multi-layout parse and skip+warn discipline. The design is implementation-ready but depends critically on the pre-merge JSON field mapping gate (obs #76 REQ-CM-20) being discharged against a live instance.

title: Fix WARNING-01 — Test Assertion Logic Corrected and Truncation Case Added
content: WARNING-01 from the code review has been fixed by correcting the test assertion logic and adding comprehensive truncation coverage. The original TestToCanonical_TitleDeriveFallback had inverted arguments to strings.HasPrefix — it was checking `HasPrefix(fullSummary, derivedTitle)` which always evaluates to true when derivedTitle is a prefix (by definition). The vacuous second condition `&& got.Title != fullSummary` was then catching the case where summary is short enough to fit under titleMaxRunes, masking the broken logic for the truncation case. The fix simplifies the assertion to the correct `HasPrefix(fullSummary, derivedTitle)` and adds a new dedicated test TestToCanonical_TitleTruncatesLongSummary that explicitly: (1) builds a summary longer than titleMaxRunes, (2) verifies the derived title is exactly titleMaxRunes runes, and (3) confirms it's a prefix of the original summary. Comments now explicitly clarify the argument order.

title: Fix WARNING-02 — Compile-Time Interface Guard Added to panicTransport
content: WARNING-02 from the code review has been fixed by adding the standard Go compile-time interface assertion. The panicTransport test helper now declares `var _ Transport = panicTransport{}` immediately after its type definition. This is the idiomatic pattern in Go for proving at compile time that a type satisfies an interface. Without it, if the Transport interface changes (e.g., in PR#3 when new methods are added), panicTransport could silently fall out of conformance and errors would only surface at the call sites where `New(panicTransport{})` is used. The assertion makes the contract explicit and catches typos or missing method implementations early.

title: Fix WARNING-03 — Timestamp-Omission Seam Guard Documented at WriteNative
content: WARNING-03 from the code review has been addressed by adding comprehensive documentation at the FromCanonical/WriteNative seam. The issue was that FromCanonical intentionally omits CreatedAt/UpdatedAt (because claude-mem is read-only in v1 and there is no write surface), but this contract was not explicitly documented at the write-path boundary. When PR#3 implements the write stub that currently returns ErrUnsupported, developers might be tempted to reuse FromCanonical output as the source for persisting timestamps, silently corrupting the data. The fix adds explicit NOTE comments at both FromCanonical and WriteNative docstrings stating that: (1) timestamps are intentionally omitted, (2) any future write path MUST re-read from the provider, and (3) FromCanonical output must never be used as the source for timestamp round-trip. This makes the contract explicit and defensible during PR#3 review.

title: Fix CRITICAL-02 — Revision Sentinel Aligned to Cross-Adapter Convention
content: CRITICAL-02 from the code review has been fixed by aligning the revision sentinel to the engram adapter's convention. The ToCanonical method now sets revision = -1 when a prior IDMap mapping exists (known record) and revision = 1 for new records. This establishes a shared cross-adapter contract: both adapters use -1 as the sentinel meaning "orchestrator must replace with priorRevision+1". Previously, claudemem used 2, which the orchestrator would silently treat as finalized, causing phantom revision mismatches in the canonical store. The extensive comment block documents the contract conflict with the original design (obs #77) which explicitly stated "claude-mem v1 sets Revision=1 always", but explains why the review instruction to align to engram's convention takes precedence for correctness. This becomes a binding requirement for PRD-3's orchestrator implementation: it must recognize and handle -1 from both adapters uniformly.

title: Fix CRITICAL-01 — normalizeTimestamp Error Wrapping Restored
content: CRITICAL-01 from the code review has been fixed. The normalizeTimestamp function now properly wraps the underlying time.Parse error with %w, preserving the error chain that ADR-6 ("documented non-silent failure") requires. The loop now captures lastErr from each failed parse attempt and returns it wrapped in the final error message. This allows callers (particularly in PR#3's ListNative degrade path) to use errors.Is or errors.As to distinguish between different failure modes. The doc comment has been updated to clarify that the error is composable and non-silent, matching the engram adapter's behavior while preserving claudemem's extension to accept SQLite datetime format.

title: PR#2 Code Review Complete — Critical Issues Require Resolution Before Merge
content:
Comprehensive code review of PR#2 (commit dc6b8d5) claude-mem adapter implementation completed via fresh-context code-reviewer agent (Sonnet model, 86.4s runtime, 50.9k tokens). The review examined ~717 lines of implementation (claudemem.go +234, claudemem_test.go +483) and 27 unit tests against the binding contracts: PRD-2 spec (#76), design (#77), and tasks (#78).

**Verdict: PASS-WITH-WARNINGS**

Two critical issues block merge without fixes:

**CRITICAL-01 (confidence 97%)**: normalizeTimestamp error path diverges from engram original. The engram version wraps the final parse error with `fmt.Errorf(...: %w, err)` allowing error-chain introspection via `errors.Is/errors.As`. Claude-mem version drops the `%w` and emits a fixed string message, breaking the error-chain contract documented in ADR-6 ("documented non-silent failure"). The test (TestNormalizeTimestamp_UnknownFormat_ReturnsError) only checks string containment, not unwrappability, so the regression is undetected. Additionally, claudemem intentionally extends the three-layout set to include SQLite datetime format (per ADR-6 extension), which is correct, but the error path still must wrap to maintain composability.

**CRITICAL-02 (confidence 92%)**: Revision sentinel divergence between adapters. Engram sets `revision = -1` for "prior mapping exists, orchestrator owns final revision"; claudemem sets `revision = 2` ("minimum-valid existing signal"). Both encodings signal the same semantic state (known record per REQ-CM-14) but to different sentinels. Without a documented cross-adapter convention (e.g., "orchestrator checks revision < 0 OR revision == 2"), the orchestrator will silently pass claudemem records with revision = 2 as finalized, causing phantom revision mismatches in the canonical store when canonical records are re-ingested.

Three warnings (80–90% confidence) include: test assertion logic inversion in title-derive case (strings.HasPrefix args swapped), missing compile-time interface guard in test (idiomatic pattern), and undocumented timestamp-omission contract at the WriteNative seam (latent PR#3 hazard once PR#3 implements the write stub).

**Positive Findings**: §6 field mapping (REQ-CM-11) correct end-to-end: id→Origin.ProviderID, session_id→Origin.SessionID, title (derived when absent), summary→Content, tags→Tags, timestamps normalized across three layouts (byte-comparable invariant verified), Kind always "session_summary", Type/TopicKey empty, Origin.Provider always "claude-mem", SchemaVersion not set by adapter. Duplication fidelity passes: authorAttribution, providerIDMapWrapper/WrapIDMap, rfc3339NanoFixed all verbatim from engram with only package-name differences. Scope discipline clean: no PR#3 logic leakage, Health/ListNative/ReadNative remain stubs; CON-01 honored (no adapter.go or store edits); tests use panicTransport to prove ToCanonical/FromCanonical purity (zero I/O calls).

**Pre-Merge Gate (Open)**: Spec-level blocker remains: real claude-mem instance with ≥1 observation has not yet been queried to reconfirm the PROVISIONAL JSON field names (claudeMemRecord struct tags marked `// PRE-MERGE GATE`). Integration test T-16 (PR#4) must discharge this gate by capturing and comparing raw JSON bytes against PRD-2 §5 expected shape.

**Path Forward**: PR#3 implements T-12/T-13/T-14 (Health delegate, ListNative pagination + project/since filters, ReadNative with degrade-to-scan) + unit tests (~230 lines). PR#4 implements T-16/T-17 (integration test + pre-merge gate checklist) (~90 lines). Estimated total for PR#2-4 combined: ~717 + 230 + 90 = ~1037 lines (stacked under 1200 per delivery strategy's medium-risk handling).

title: PR#2 Claude-Mem Adapter Code Review — Critical Issues Identified
content:
A fresh-context code review (code-reviewer subagent, Sonnet model, 86.4s runtime) of PR#2 claudemem adapter changes found material correctness issues across error handling, adapter consistency, test assertions, and future-proofing. The two critical issues require resolution before merge:

1. **CRITICAL-01 (confidence 97%)**: normalizeTimestamp (lines 155–166) drops the %w error wrapping present in the engram original (engram.go:141). The claudemem version emits a fixed string error message without wrapping the underlying parse error, breaking the error-chain contract documented in ADR-6 ("documented non-silent failure"). Callers cannot use errors.Is/errors.As on the root cause. The test (TestNormalizeTimestamp_UnknownFormat_ReturnsError, lines 402–410) only asserts the string contains "normalizeTimestamp", not unwrappability, so the regression is undetected. Additionally, claudemem intentionally extends normalizeTimestamp to accept SQLite datetime layout (2026-05-16 15:04:05), which is correct per ADR-6 extension, but the error path still needs %w to maintain composability.

2. **CRITICAL-02 (confidence 92%)**: Revision sentinel divergence (lines 291–302). The engram adapter sets revision = -1 for "prior mapping exists, orchestrator must set final value"; claudemem sets revision = 2 with a comment saying it's "minimum-valid existing signal". Both adapters emit different sentinels for the same semantic state (known record, orchestrator owns final revision per REQ-CM-14). Without a documented cross-adapter convention (e.g., "orchestrator checks revision < 0 OR revision == 2"), the orchestrator will silently pass claudemem records with revision = 2 as if they are finalized, causing phantom revision mismatches in the canonical store.

Three warnings (severity 80–90%) include test assertion logic inversion (TestToCanonical_TitleDeriveFallback), missing compile-time interface guard (panicTransport), and undocumented timestamp-omission contract at the WriteNative seam (latent PR#3 hazard).

Positive findings: Field mapping (§6) correct; timestamp normalization across three layouts verified via byte-comparable invariant test; Kind always "session_summary"; tags nil-to-empty-slice normalization correct; scope discipline clean (no PR#3 logic leakage); CON-01 honored (adapter.go and store/ unmodified); duplication fidelity passes for authorAttribution, providerIDMapWrapper/WrapIDMap, and rfc3339NanoFixed; commit hygiene clean.

The session added a missing import (store package) to claudemem_test.go to allow the test helper toCanonicalOrFatal to reference store.CanonicalRecord type. title: PR#2 Claude-mem Adapter Implementation Complete (Data Layer + Pure Functions)
content: PR#2 implementation completes the data-layer and pure-function surface of the claude-mem adapter. Two new native structs (claudeMemRecord and observationsPage) capture the PROVISIONAL shape of claude-mem's HTTP API, tagged with PRE-MERGE GATE comments to block merge until field names are reconfirmed against real instances. Helper functions copy utilities from the engram adapter (authorAttribution, normalizeTimestamp, rfc3339NanoFixed) to ensure timestamp byte-comparability across multiple input layouts and derive titles from summaries when absent. The WrapIDMap boundary adapter bridges store.providerIDMap's plain-string interface to the adapter.IDMap named-type contract, resolving the W-01 isolation constraint. ToCanonical fully maps native records to canonical form per the specification (REQ-CM-11), resolving IDs via IDMap, normalizing timestamps, deriving titles, and setting Kind and Provider fields. FromCanonical provides the reverse mapping for future bidirectional reuse (v1.1). Three I/O methods (Health, ListNative, ReadNative) are left as panicking stubs for PR#3. Unit tests comprehensively cover all pure functions with table-driven cases, multibyte Unicode handling, timestamp layout tolerance, IDMap boundary crossings, and type guards. A panicTransport fake proves that ToCanonical and FromCanonical make no transport calls. The codebase builds cleanly after these changes.

title: PR#2 Claude-mem Adapter Implementation State

Main implementation file (claudemem.go) grew substantially with 234 lines of new code, while test coverage expanded to 482 lines ensuring comprehensive testing of adapter behavior. Skill registry system saw cleanup with 157 lines removed, suggesting optimization of registry metadata. The branch state shows uncommitted changes across adapter and registry systems, indicating work was interrupted during active implementation.

title: All three code review findings fixed and verified in PR#1 (commit a8b0d1d)
content: All three post-code-review findings have been successfully fixed and verified within PR#1 scope. The CRITICAL missing provisional record struct is now defined with proper PROVISIONAL/PRE-MERGE GATE documentation. The test helper error chain walker has been replaced with the standard errors.Is function for correct Go 1.20+ behavior. The redundant cleanup pattern in the test setup helper has been eliminated in favor of relying on t.Setenv's built-in cleanup. The commit is clean, the build/test/vet suite passes completely, and the pre-merge gate checklist remains appropriately open for the integration test phase (PR#4). title: PR#1 Task T-01/T-02/T-03 implemented: Transport interface, supervisor.json port resolution, httpTransport with Health/ListPage/GetByID
content: The Transport seam for PRD-2 PR#1 is now complete. The transport.go file implements the three-method interface required for HTTP communication with the claude-mem daemon. Port resolution follows the design's 3-level priority (explicit > supervisor.json > fallback 37701), with defensive error handling on the peripheral supervisor.json file to ensure adapter construction never fails on a missing/malformed config file (ADR-3). The httpTransport implementation maps HTTP status codes to canonical sentinel errors (ErrUnavailable for connection/non-2xx, ErrNotFound for 404), and the GetByID triple-return (body, found, err) enables ReadNative to distinguish a clean 404 (definitive not-found, no degrade) from an unavailable endpoint (trigger scan fallback per ADR-5). Context cancellation and deadline are propagated raw so callers can inspect them. HTTP client carries a defensive 10-second timeout matching PRD-2 §4 design note.

title: SDD Tasks Phase Completed for claude-mem-adapter — 4-PR Stacked-to-Main Strategy Confirmed
