title: Glia configuration uses dual-transport sync to wrapper-mems project
content: The .glia/config.yaml reveals a multi-transport sync strategy for the wrapper-mems project. Engram uses CLI-based transport (direct command invocation), while claude-mem uses HTTP-based transport via a local daemon on port 37701. The sync is configured for full synchronization (not incremental delta) with engram mirroring disabled and a 5-second timeout for mirror operations. Auto-commit is off, meaning changes are not automatically persisted. This configuration explains the previous sync behavior: records are pushed to both providers via their respective transports, but pulling operates under different constraints (mirror_engram disabled) which may explain the 0 records pulled despite the push activity.

title: Glia sync details show asymmetric record flow: 760 records pushed, 0 pulled

This asymmetry explains why the earlier sync showed "no changes"—the user's local memory state was successfully synchronized outbound to both providers, but neither provider had new data to return. No conflicts arose during the operation, indicating clean write capability and schema compatibility.

title: Documented decision: glia does NOT back up individual providers (PRD-9 §3.1)

title: Provider backup explicitly documented as Non-Goal in PRD-9 (decided 2026-05-29)

title: PRD-9 scope is glia's local store backup, not provider data snapshots
content: PRD-9 is explicitly scoped to glia's own local store durability and recovery, not backing up external provider data. The design calls for cheap, automatic snapshots before risky operations, with atomic restore semantics and schema version validation. This clarifies the distinction: PRD-9 solves glia's own version-safety problem (what if an upgrade breaks glia's store?), while provider backup is out of scope because providers manage their own durability and glia lacks the APIs to restore them anyway.

title: Local Store Backup & Rollback Specification (PRD-9)
content: PRD-9 specifies a local backup and rollback system to protect glia's canonical memory store from corruption due to bad migrations or buggy sync operations. The system captures point-in-time snapshots of glia's local store files and allows atomic restoration. Snapshots are stored as gzip-compressed tarballs with embedded metadata for fast listing. The restore operation acquires the store lock, validates schema compatibility, writes to a temporary location, then atomically swaps into place via rename—ensuring that a crash leaves either the old or new store intact, never a partial state. Configuration allows optional pre-sync snapshots and retention limits (default 10 snapshots). Intentional scope exclusions: the system does not back up provider-native data or provide undo for records already pushed to external providers—it protects glia's local store only. Open design questions remain regarding IDMap storage location, whether to include the derived index in snapshots or rebuild on restore, and compression format (leaning gzip).

title: Shelved claude-mem server-beta Integration (PRD-8)
content: Investigation of claude-mem's capabilities revealed two distinct HTTP surfaces: the Worker (default, already integrated into glia) and server-beta (opt-in API version). The Worker only supports POST /api/memory/save and does not return metadata in responses, despite storing it internally. The server-beta surface provides full CRUD including GET /v1/memories/:id and PATCH operations, which would enable metadata round-trip and updates. However, analysis determined that glia's local IDMap implementation (PRD-7) already provides idempotency without server-beta, making metadata read-back a convenience rather than a correctness requirement. Combined with the operational overhead of running a separate process and managing API-key authentication, the decision was made to shelve PRD-8 and not pursue server-beta integration at this time. The PRD was documented as a decision record to prevent re-litigating the choice, with clear conditions listed for when to revisit (e.g., if server-beta becomes default, or if users need update/delete capabilities).

title: Claude-Mem Local Storage Architecture Mapped
content: Exploration of the Glia project revealed a mature local-first memory storage architecture. The .glia directory serves as the local store, containing memory records in JSONL format with a JSON index for fast lookups. The internal/store package implements a sophisticated persistence layer with first-class support for conflict resolution, crash recovery, index rebuilding, and state synchronization—all foundational requirements for the planned backup and multi-provider sync features. The existence of syncstate.go and ULID generation suggests the architecture was designed with eventual consistency and multi-source synchronization in mind from the start.

title: claude-mem write endpoint investigation: POST /api/memory/save verified with constraints
content: The user conducted a systematic investigation of claude-mem's write capabilities to design bidirectional sync (PRD-7). Research included source code inspection of thedotmack/claude-mem MemoryRoutes.ts and ServerV1Routes.ts, plus live probes against a running worker at localhost:37701. Key finding: the write endpoint already exists on the worker (same transport as read), but has architectural constraints that reshape the sync design. Metadata stashing cannot provide idempotency because GET does not return metadata — glia must maintain its own IDMap store. The worker's auto-generation of AI summaries during writes requires loop-prevention filters (do not re-push claude-mem-origin records). The append-only constraint (no DELETE/PATCH) means glia cannot sync updates or corrections backward to claude-mem. These constraints are documented in PRD-7 §2 with verification sources and inform the adapter contract in §5.

title: Bidirectional sync design: local IDMap + loop prevention + graceful degradation
content: Based on the write endpoint investigation findings, the user designed a comprehensive bidirectional sync strategy that works around claude-mem's append-only constraint and metadata write-only limitation. Since glia cannot rely on claude-mem to store or return canonical_id metadata, a local IDMap becomes authoritative — eliminating the need for round-trip lookups. Loop prevention through provider-origin filtering ensures that observations read from claude-mem in sync N are never re-pushed in sync N+1. The metadata fields are stamped anyway for forward compatibility when claude-mem eventually exposes metadata in GET. Graceful degradation via endpoint detection (probe on first write) ensures older workers gracefully downgrade to read-only without manual intervention. The write_enabled config flag provides a rollback switch for users who need to revert to v1.0 behavior. Sync observability improves with counters for successful writes and write errors, allowing honest reporting of degraded state when schema drift occurs.

title: OSS launch security preparation completed and live
content: The glia project completed its transition to open source. All security governance infrastructure was put in place and verified working: SECURITY.md establishes vulnerability reporting procedures with clear SLAs, CODEOWNERS automates review requests to the maintainer, the repository is now publicly visible on GitHub, and private vulnerability reporting is enabled for security researchers. Before going public, a comprehensive secret scan confirmed no sensitive credentials were committed. The main branch protection ruleset (requiring PR + CI checks + 1 approval + linear history) is actively enforcing governance, demonstrated by PR #9's successful merge under these rules. The project is now ready for public contributions with appropriate safeguards.

title: Repository visibility changed to PUBLIC; OSS launch executed
content: The glia repository transitioned from private to public visibility, marking the open-source launch. The repository is now accessible to the public on GitHub with all prepared governance infrastructure in place: main branch protected by ruleset 17128772 requiring PR reviews and CI checks, SECURITY.md documenting vulnerability reporting procedures, CODEOWNERS automating review requests, and private vulnerability reporting enabled for security researchers to report issues confidentially. The project is ready to accept external contributions under these governance guardrails.

title: PR #9 merged to main with security governance files
content: Pull request #9 containing security governance files was merged to main using squash strategy, collapsing the feature branch into a single commit. The maintainer used admin bypass to merge their own PR while still respecting the branch protection ruleset. The SECURITY.md vulnerability reporting policy and CODEOWNERS file are now live on main. The source branch was automatically deleted as part of the merge. This completes the security governance setup for the OSS launch: main branch now has protection rules (PR + CI + 1 approval), security policy documentation, and code owner automation.

title: CI test check passed on PR #9
content: The CI test job on PR #9 executed and completed successfully in 33 seconds. This satisfies the first branch protection requirement (required status checks). The PR now awaits the second requirement: one approving review from a designated reviewer. Since CODEOWNERS designated @AgustinCastanol as the owner, the maintainer's review is what's needed to unblock the merge to main.

title: Pull request #9 opened for security governance files
content: Pull request #9 was opened to merge the security governance files (SECURITY.md and CODEOWNERS) into main. The PR is now subject to the branch protection ruleset: it requires 1 approving review and CI checks to pass before it can be merged. Because CODEOWNERS designates @AgustinCastanol as the owner of all files, GitHub will automatically request review from the maintainer. The PR description documents the purpose of each file and their relationship to the new branch protection rules, providing context for the OSS launch preparation.

title: Security files committed and pushed to chore/oss-security-files branch
content: The SECURITY.md vulnerability reporting policy and CODEOWNERS file were committed to the chore/oss-security-files branch with a message documenting the OSS launch context. The branch was pushed to GitHub and configured to track the remote, making it ready for submission as a pull request. This staging of security governance files completes the preparatory work for opening the glia repository to public contributions.

title: High-signal secret scan on tracked files — clean result
content: A comprehensive high-signal pattern scan was performed on all files tracked in git to detect common secrets (AWS credentials, SSH private keys, GitHub personal access tokens, Slack tokens, hardcoded API keys/passwords). The scan uses regex patterns with high specificity to minimize false positives and filters out known safe contexts like documentation, test files, and type declarations. The result shows zero matches, confirming that no obvious secrets are committed to the repository. This provides strong confidence that the project is safe to make public without risk of exposing credentials.

title: CODEOWNERS file created for PR review automation
content: A CODEOWNERS file was created in the .github directory to establish code ownership and automate PR review assignment. The configuration designates @AgustinCastanol as the owner of all repository files, which means GitHub will automatically request reviews from the maintainer when external contributors open pull requests. This integrates with the branch protection ruleset that requires 1 approving review, ensuring review requests are consistently sent to the maintainer for all incoming changes. The file provides a clear signal to contributors about who maintains the code and streamlines the contribution workflow.

title: SECURITY.md policy created for vulnerability reporting
content: A SECURITY.md policy was created to establish clear procedures for security vulnerability reporting in the glia project. The policy directs reporters to use GitHub's private vulnerability advisory system for confidential disclosure, with an email fallback for those unable to use GitHub. The document sets response time expectations (72 hours acknowledgement, 7 days assessment) and commits to coordinated disclosure with reporter credit. The security scope is clearly defined based on glia's architecture as a local-first CLI: it includes file handling, provider communication, subprocess execution, and credential paths, while explicitly excluding upstream provider vulnerabilities and scenarios requiring pre-existing local access. This establishes trust with the OSS community by providing clear vulnerability handling procedures before the repository becomes public.

title: Repository secrets sanity check before OSS launch
content: Before proceeding with OSS security configuration, a quick audit was performed to ensure no sensitive information (environment variables, tokens, credentials, private keys, or local state) is committed to the repository. The .gitignore is properly configured to exclude .glia/ state files (index.json and .lock). No obviously sensitive files were found in the tracked repository, indicating the project is clean from a secrets-leakage perspective and safe to make public. This scan forms the baseline before adding formal OSS security documentation (SECURITY.md, CODEOWNERS).

title: Branch protection configured as OSS launch preparation step
content: The branch protection ruleset was created as a preparatory step for the glia OSS launch. The chosen governance model (1 approval with admin bypass) provides safety for external contributions while allowing the solo maintainer to merge their own PRs when needed. The ruleset integrates with the existing CI pipeline by requiring the "test" job to pass. A key follow-up item is making the repository public on GitHub to complete the OSS launch, as it is currently still in private visibility state.

title: Branch ruleset created for main branch protection
content: A comprehensive branch ruleset was created to protect the main branch in the glia repository. The ruleset implements a governance model that requires all changes to main to go through pull requests with at least one approval, pass the CI test suite, and resolve all review comments. Force pushes and branch deletion are prevented. The ruleset maintains linear history by disallowing merge commits. While the ruleset is active and enforced, repository admins retain the ability to bypass the requirements if necessary for emergency fixes. This brings the repository to a higher safety standard by preventing direct commits to main and ensuring code review and CI validation before any changes can merge.

title: GitHub Actions workflows configuration
content: The glia repository uses GitHub Actions for automation with two main workflows: a ci.yml workflow that runs tests on every push and pull request, and a release.yml workflow for handling releases. The CI workflow includes a test job executing on ubuntu-latest, providing automated testing coverage for all code changes before they reach main.

title: Repository branch protection and visibility configuration
content: The glia repository is configured as private with the user having full admin access. Currently, the main branch has no formal protection rules or governance mechanisms in place—no require-reviews, no required status checks, no dismiss-stale-reviews, and no rulesets. This means anyone with push access to the repository can directly commit to and push to main without requiring pull request reviews or passing CI checks, though the release workflow is triggered by tag pushes. The repository's private status limits access to explicitly granted collaborators.

title: v1.3.0 versioning and release decision rationale
content: The decision to create v1.3.0 as a minor version bump (rather than v1.2.1 patch) was justified by the inclusion of new user-facing TUI navigation features (tab cycling, number-key navigation) alongside the async load routing bugfix. These features improve the user experience while maintaining backward compatibility. A documentation gap was identified: while the README documents the --provider flag and lists sync pull/push subcommands, it lacks concrete usage examples showing how to restrict operations to a single provider (e.g., `glia sync pull --provider engram`). Additionally, the documentation shows --provider only on the parent sync command, not clearly indicating that it works as a persistent flag on the pull and push subcommands.

title: Release workflow queued after v1.3.0 tag push
content: The release workflow was triggered when v1.3.0 was pushed to the repository and is currently in queued status, waiting to execute. The CI workflows for the recent PRs (#7 and #8) that were merged into this release have already completed successfully, indicating that the code passed all automated tests before the tag was created.

title: Release v1.3.0 pushed to GitHub
content: The v1.3.0 release tag was successfully pushed to the remote GitHub repository, making the release publicly available for users. The tag is now visible in the glia repository on GitHub and can be downloaded or referenced in documentation.

title: Release v1.3.0 tagged with TUI fixes and documentation updates
content: Release v1.3.0 was created to capture the 7 untagged commits on main since v1.2.0. The release includes a significant TUI bugfix that improves the application's startup behavior by ensuring async load results (Conflicts and Status panels) populate correctly on initial load without requiring manual reload. The release also improves keyboard navigation usability with tab cycling and direct number-key navigation, while fixing an issue where shortcuts were interfering with the filter input. Additionally, two new PRDs documenting upcoming features (claude-mem file source and openspec integration) were added to the documentation.

title: Provider filter flag and sync commands already documented in README
content: The --provider flag and sync pull/push commands are already documented in the main README.md in a command reference table. Line 108 lists all sync flags including --provider, and lines 109-110 document the pull and push subcommands with their purposes. The documentation uses pull/push terminology for the user interface, while internal architecture documentation uses import/export terminology to describe data flow direction (adapter import/export cycle). This means users looking at the README already have clear information about how to restrict sync operations to specific providers.

title: Repository release state and untagged commits on main
content: The glia repository has accumulated 7 commits on origin/main that are not yet tagged as a release since the last tag (v1.2.0). These commits include a significant TUI bugfix (PR #7) that redesigned tab navigation and improved async load routing, along with documentation enhancements including new PRDs, README rewrite for public OSS distribution, and tool renaming to glia. The repository appears ready for a new release or version bump to capture these changes.

title: ProviderFilter implementation in sync engine
content: The sync engine fully implements provider filtering through the ProviderFilter option. When ProviderFilter is set with one or more provider names, the engine restricts all sync operations to only those named providers. The implementation creates a map of allowed provider names and filters the active providers list accordingly. This feature is comprehensively tested with multiple test cases covering different sync operations (Status, ActiveProviders, Sync), confirming that the filtering works correctly across the engine.

title: Existing provider filtering flag in sync commands
content: The glia sync command already implements provider filtering through a --provider flag mechanism. The flag is defined as a persistent flag on the parent syncCmd, meaning it automatically propagates to all subcommands including sync pull and sync push. The flag accepts multiple values (repeatable), allowing users to specify one or more provider names to restrict operations to. The values are stored in syncFlags.providers and passed to the sync engine's ProviderFilter configuration option, which controls which providers are processed during sync operations.

title: Submitted PRD-10 and PRD-11 for review via PR #8
content: The draft PRDs for claude-mem file transport (PRD-10) and openspec artifact ingestion (PRD-11) were formally submitted for review via GitHub PR #8. The PR description provides comprehensive context: PRD-10 adds a file-based transport peer to the existing HTTP transport, reusing PRD-2's field mapping so both converge to identical canonical records with idempotency via IDMap. PRD-11 ingests markdown artifacts from openspec/ into the canonical store using content-hash versioning and sdd/<change>/<artifact> topic keys. The PR highlights that PRD-11 requires a PRD-0 amendment to introduce kind:spec_artifact, and both PRDs propose a shared sources/providers architectural distinction. Both PRDs are marked Draft pending team review of the design decisions.

title: Added PRD-10 and PRD-11 for static file and openspec artifact ingestion
content: Two product requirement documents were committed that extend glia's ingest architecture. PRD-10 adds a file-based transport layer for claude-mem integration, leveraging existing field mapping and IDMap idempotency mechanisms so both HTTP and file transports produce identical records. PRD-11 introduces a new openspec source that consumes markdown artifacts from the sdd/<change>/<artifact> convention, versioning records via content-hash and storing them in the canonical store. PRD-11 flags a blocking amendment to PRD-0 for a new kind and proposes a first-class sources concept (read-only) distinct from bidirectional providers, shared with PRD-10. These PRDs represent architectural decisions to expand glia's data ingest surface with simpler, daemon-free mechanisms.

title: PRD-11: Design read-only source for OpenSpec artifacts
content:
PRD-11 designs a read-only static source for OpenSpec artifacts — the markdown documentation trail from spec-driven development (proposal, delta specs, design, tasks). These files represent the reasoning behind code changes but are currently invisible to glia, unsearchable in the TUI, and not synced to engram/claude-mem alongside the observations they relate to.

The solution ingests artifact files directly from the openspec/ directory (configurable, repo-local by default) into the canonical store. Each artifact maps to one record, keyed on its relative file path (origin.provider_id), enabling stable re-ingestion. Content hashing drives revisioning: unchanged files are no-ops; changed files increment revision. The topic_key convention (sdd/<change>/<artifact>) mirrors the SDD grouping already used in engram, so artifacts cluster naturally with their corresponding observations.

Critically, PRD-11 is designed as a peer to PRD-10: both read static files with no daemon/network requirement, both use content hashing for idempotency, both require a shared "static file source" mechanism. The design formalizes a distinction between read-only sources (openspec, claude-mem file transport) and bidirectional providers (engram, claude-mem HTTP), making the asymmetry structural rather than implicit.

Blocking decisions: PRD-0 must be amended to introduce kind: spec_artifact with type ∈ {proposal, spec, design, tasks}, to keep artifact records visibly distinct from observations and session summaries. Open questions deferred to implementation: timestamps (git log vs mtime), state.yaml handling, archived change ingestion, and whether file absence should tombstone or leave records stale.

title: PRD-10: Design file-based transport for claude-mem adapter
content:
PRD-10 designs a second transport for the existing claude-mem adapter, addressing a hard operational constraint: PRD-2's HTTP-only design requires the claude-mem daemon to be running, making sync fail silently in offline scenarios, CI, ephemeral containers, and one-shot forensic imports.

The solution adds file-based transport as a peer behind the same Adapter interface. Both http and file transports normalize their source (HTTP /api/observations or static export files) into the same internal ClaudeMemRecord, then apply the unchanged PRD-2 §6 mapping to canonical. This design guarantees that the same observation ingested via http or file produces byte-identical canonical records, keyed on origin.provider_id, preventing duplicates across transport switches.

Configuration is explicit: `transport: http` (default, preserves existing behavior) or `transport: file` with `file.path` pointing to the export location. The file transport walks single files or directories (sorted, deterministic), filters by project_id and updated_at, and surfaces transport choice and source freshness in glia status.

Critical constraints: project_id filtering is strict to prevent cross-project leakage from mixed exports (same risk as PRD-2 §9.4); revision bumps only on updated_at advance, never from transport changes; malformed records are logged and skipped, never aborting. Open questions deferred to implementation: exact export format (JSONL vs markdown archive), path auto-discovery via ~/.claude-mem/supervisor.json analogue, and whether v1 should support transport: auto fallback or require explicit choice.

title: Create documentation branch for PRD static sources
content: User switched to main branch and created a new feature branch docs/prd-static-sources to organize documentation work related to PRD static sources. Main was already up to date with the remote, providing a clean starting point. This branch isolation pattern allows focused work on documentation without affecting the main branch.

title: PR #7 created for TUI async routing and navigation fixes
content: The TUI async routing and navigation fix was packaged as PR #7 against main. The PR describes two critical problems: async load results (status, conflicts, observations) were silently dropped at startup because they routed to the active tab instead of their owner, and tab navigation required Shift+letter combinations while stealing characters from the filter input. The fix routes messages to their owning sub-model regardless of active tab, redesigns navigation to use standard tab/shift+tab cycling with 1-4 jump shortcuts, and suppresses single-key shortcuts while the Observations filter is focused. Regression tests cover async routing, navigation cycles, and shortcut suppression. All build and test checks passed.

title: Fixed async message routing in TUI sub-models and redesigned tab navigation
content: A critical routing bug in the TUI caused async load results to be delivered to the wrong model. At startup, the Observations tab is active, so startup loads for Conflicts and Status tabs were silently dropped—those tabs never populated until manually reloaded while focused. The fix routes each async message to its owning sub-model regardless of which tab is currently active. Simultaneously, tab navigation was redesigned for better discoverability: tab/shift+tab now cycles between tabs and 1-4 jumps directly, with legacy single-letter shortcuts (O/C/S/?) kept as aliases. A hidden collision where the global key dispatcher stole characters from the Observations filter input was fixed by suppressing single-key shortcuts while the filter is focused. The UI and help documentation were updated to teach the new navigation scheme, and regression tests were added.

title: Feature branch created for TUI navigation and routing fixes
content: User created a dedicated feature branch to address TUI (Terminal User Interface) loading, routing, and navigation issues. All internal/tui/ modifications were staged in this branch, including updates to help display, model structure, and corresponding test golden files. This isolates TUI navigation fixes from main branch development.

title: TUI help display and model structure modifications ready for commit
content: User has modified the TUI help display and model components, including corresponding test fixtures. The golden test files were regenerated, indicating visual layout changes to the help display at 80x24 terminal dimensions. An untracked .glia/ directory was created, possibly for local tool configuration or state. Changes are ready to be committed before PR submission.

title: Complete project build, vet, and test suite passes
content: Build and validation suite passes cleanly. The TUI fixes compile without errors, pass static analysis (vet), and all TUI tests execute successfully in 1.540s. The full project test suite validates that no regressions were introduced — all existing functionality remains intact while the async message routing fix and navigation improvements are working correctly. The implementation is production-ready.

title: All TUI tests passing after golden file regeneration
content: All TUI tests now pass after regenerating golden files to capture the updated UI. The golden files have been updated with the new numeric tab shortcuts and modern status bar hints. The complete test suite validates that all existing functionality remains intact while the new async message routing and navigation improvements work correctly. The TUI is now fully tested and ready for use.

title: Update Help tab documentation for new navigation shortcuts
content: Updated the Help tab's Global keybindings section to document the new navigation patterns instead of the legacy single-letter shortcuts. The documentation now emphasizes tab/shift+tab for cycling (standard TUI pattern) and 1-4 for direct jumps, making the help text consistent with the actual implemented shortcuts and the updated status bar hint. This ensures users reading the Help tab see the correct navigation methods.

title: All new test cases passing for async routing and navigation fixes
content: All new regression tests pass, validating that both critical fixes are working correctly. The async message routing fix allows Status, Conflicts, and Observations data to reach their owning sub-models regardless of which tab is active at startup, solving the section-loading problem. The navigation improvements enable tab/shift+tab cycling and 1-4 numeric shortcuts while properly suppressing shortcuts during filter input. Legacy single-letter shortcuts (O/C/S/?) remain functional and tested. The fixes are production-ready.

title: Golden test files out of sync with updated tab bar labels and status hints
content: The golden test for Help tab layout is failing because the rendered output no longer matches the golden file. The UI improvements (numeric shortcuts in tab labels and updated status bar hint) have changed the rendered output. The test correctly detects this mismatch. To resolve, the golden files must be regenerated by running tests with the -update-golden flag to capture the new UI output as the new golden baseline.

title: Add comprehensive regression tests for async routing and navigation fixes
content: Added comprehensive regression tests covering both major fixes: the async data message routing bug (Fix 1) and the new navigation patterns (Fix 2). The async routing tests verify that conflictReloadMsg, statusDataMsg, and obsReloadMsg reach their owning sub-models regardless of which tab is currently active, preventing the silent data loss that caused sections to appear blank. The navigation tests verify tab/shift+tab cycling, numeric shortcuts, and critically, that single-key shortcuts are suppressed while the filter input is focused, preventing characters from being stolen from user input. Tests include detailed comments documenting the regression they prevent.

title: Add store import to model_test.go for testing async message types
content: Added store package import to the test file to enable testing of the async message routing fixes. This import provides access to store.ConflictEntry and other data types used in async reload messages (obsReloadMsg, conflictReloadMsg, statusDataMsg) so that tests can verify these messages route to their owning sub-models regardless of which tab is active.

title: Update status bar hint to reflect new navigation patterns
content: Updated the status bar hint text to match the new navigation scheme. The hint now emphasizes modern keyboard patterns: tab/shift+tab for cycling through tabs (standard in many TUIs), numeric shortcuts 1-4 for jumping directly to specific tabs, and q for quit. The old single-letter shortcuts (O/C/S/?) are no longer shown in the hint, making the interface cleaner and directing users toward the more discoverable navigation methods. The shift symbol (⇧) adds visual clarity for shift+tab.

title: Update tab bar labels to prominently display numeric shortcuts
content: Updated the tab bar display labels to prominently show the numeric shortcuts (1-4) instead of the letter shortcuts in brackets (O/C/S/?). This makes the keyboard shortcuts immediately visible to users without requiring them to read help text or documentation. The change improves discoverability of the new numeric navigation feature that was just added, making the TUI more intuitive by showing users how to quickly jump between sections.

title: Fix section loading failure by routing async data messages to owning sub-model
content: The reported TUI problem — sections not loading when selected — was caused by a message routing bug. The original Update method routed all messages through the active tab's sub-model. When Conflicts or Status data loaded asynchronously at startup while Observations was the active tab, those messages would be sent to the Observations model, which ignored them, leaving those tabs empty. The fix introduces explicit handling for obsReloadMsg, conflictReloadMsg, conflictResolvedMsg, and statusDataMsg before any tab dispatch, ensuring async data always reaches its owning sub-model regardless of which tab is currently visible. This allows sections to populate properly during startup and during background data refreshes.

title: Add tab navigation cycling and improved keyboard shortcuts
content: Added modern keyboard navigation patterns to improve TUI intuitiveness. Tab/shift+tab cycling is now the standard way to navigate between tabs using modulo arithmetic for wrap-around. Numeric shortcuts (1-4) provide direct access to specific tabs. The key dispatcher now checks whether the active tab is capturing text (presumably for filter input) before processing q and single-key shortcuts, preventing accidental key theft from filter fields — a common usability problem in TUIs. ctrl+c maintains its absolute priority for quitting.

title: Introduce activeCapturingText helper method for input-aware key routing
content: A new helper method activeCapturingText() is introduced to make key routing aware of whether the active sub-model is currently accepting text input (e.g., in a filter field). This method gates the processing of q quit and single-key tab-jump shortcuts, allowing text inputs to consume these keys as content rather than global commands. This is essential for filter inputs that might use these letters — without this guard, typing 'q' in a filter would quit the application.

title: Add numTabs constant for tab cycling infrastructure
content: Added a new numTabs = 4 constant to the tab definitions in model.go. This constant is intended to support cycling through tabs using tab and shift+tab keyboard shortcuts. The constant consolidates the total tab count in one place rather than hardcoding it in navigation logic, making the code more maintainable when tabs are added or removed.

title: TUI async load messages routed to active tab only, skipped for inactive tabs
content:
The glia TUI uses Bubble Tea's command/message pattern to load tab data asynchronously on startup. The root Model initializes all 4 sub-models (Observations, Conflicts, Status, Help) with Init() calls that return Cmds, which Bubble Tea runs concurrently. Each sub-model fires a typed async message (obsReloadMsg, conflictReloadMsg, statusDataMsg) when its load completes.

However, the root Model routes these messages through delegateMsg(), which uses a switch statement on the currently active tab. At startup, Observations is active. When Conflicts' data arrives asynchronously, delegateMsg routes it to the Observations sub-model (which doesn't handle conflictReloadMsg), causing it to drop. The Conflicts tab appears empty until the user navigates to it and presses 'r' (reload), at which point the message arrives while Conflicts is active and is correctly handled.

This explains the user's observation: tabs appear to load lazily only when manually accessed, giving the impression of a broken/unintuitive UI. The fix requires decoupling typed data messages from tab-active routing — either broadcast all typed messages to all sub-models (each ignores what it doesn't own), or add explicit type cases in root Update() to target the correct sub-model before delegating.

Secondary UX issues discovered: tab navigation uses Shift+O/C/S (requires Shift, unintuitive), collides with lowercase single-key commands within tabs (s=sync, c=copy), and lacks arrow/Tab key navigation between tabs.

title: Glia version confirmed in local environment
content: A version check was performed on the glia installation in the local development environment. The command `glia --version` successfully returned output indicating glia v1.3.0 with schema v1 support is installed. This confirms the tool is available and functional in the project context.

title: Implement sourceStatus NewestArtifact calculation to extract latest artifact timestamp
content: The sourceStatus() method is enhanced to calculate and populate the NewestArtifact field (latest artifact modification timestamp). The implementation iterates through each artifact returned by ListNative, reads the native record, converts to canonical form, and extracts the UpdatedAt timestamp. It tracks the most recent timestamp and formats it for display in SourceStatus. Error handling is best-effort: parse failures or missing artifacts are skipped silently. The field remains empty (rendered as "-" in status output) when no artifacts exist or all timestamps are invalid, providing accurate observability for source health and recency.

title: Add TDD tests for sourceStatus NewestArtifact field calculation
content: Two TDD test cases added to verify the NewestArtifact field behavior in sourceStatus(). The first test sets up a source with three artifacts having different modification timestamps and verifies that Status() correctly identifies and reports the most recent one. The second test verifies the edge case where no artifacts exist, confirming the field remains empty. These tests define the expected behavior before implementing the sourceStatus() logic to populate the NewestArtifact field from source adapter records.

title: Update sourceFakeAdapter.ToCanonical to use injected UpdatedAt timestamps
content: The sourceFakeAdapter.ToCanonical method is updated to use the updatedByID map to inject UpdatedAt timestamps on generated records. When updatedByID is set, each record gets its UpdatedAt field populated from the map based on its native ID. This enables tests to control artifact modification times and verify that sourceStatus() correctly identifies and reports the newest (most recently modified) artifact timestamp.

title: Add updatedByID field to sourceFakeAdapter test fixture to support newest-artifact testing
content: Work began on fixing the observability gap identified in the demo: sourceStatus() does not populate the NewestArtifact field (newest artifact mtime). The test fixture sourceFakeAdapter is being enhanced to support tests for this behavior by adding an updatedByID map that allows tests to inject UpdatedAt timestamps for individual artifacts. This enables testing the logic that extracts and reports the most recently modified artifact timestamp from a source.

title: Clarify resolveBaseURL priority comments with supervisor.json discovery status and actual worker port
content: The resolveBaseURL function comments are updated to make the supervisor.json discovery status and actual worker port explicit. Priority #2 (reading from supervisor.json) is now documented as "best-effort; a no-op on claude-mem v13.2.0" making clear it doesn't currently work. Priority #3 (hardcoded :37701) is now documented as "the worker's actual port today" making clear it's not an arbitrary fallback but the actual observed port. Together with the supervisorConfig and readSupervisorPort comments, this comprehensively documents why this code path exists, why it doesn't work currently, and why the fallback is correct.

title: Update readSupervisorPort function comments to document current behavior and forward-compatibility rationale
content: The readSupervisorPort function comments are updated to document realistic behavior and rationale. The new comment explains that with claude-mem v13.2.0, supervisor.json does not publish a port field, so the function always returns 0 and resolveBaseURL falls through to the hardcoded :37701 default. The code is retained not because it works currently, but as forward-compatible scaffolding: if a future claude-mem release publishes port information in supervisor.json, this discovery code begins working automatically with no code changes. This balances honesty about current behavior with preparation for future upstream changes.

title: Document supervisor.json port discovery in code comments with forward-compatibility rationale
content: The investigation into the supervisor.json port discovery dead code is documented in the source via updated comments. Rather than delete the dead code, the comments now explain why it exists: the Port field is kept as forward-compatible scaffolding for potential future claude-mem releases that might publish the port. Until that happens, readSupervisorPort returns 0 and resolveBaseURL falls back to the hardcoded :37701. The comments document the verified actual structure of supervisor.json (processes with no port field), making the rationale clear: this code path is not currently viable with claude-mem v13.2.0, but infrastructure is preserved for when/if the upstream daemon publishes this information. This balances pragmatism (code works correctly today) with forward compatibility and honest documentation.

title: Corrected understanding — supervisor.json has NO port field; readSupervisorPort is dead code
content: Earlier investigation claimed supervisor.json nested the port under "processes". The actual finding is more fundamental: supervisor.json has NO port field at all. The file only contains process metadata (worker, chroma-mcp, SDK entries with pid, type, startedAt, pgid, sessionId). The supervisorConfig struct expects a top-level "port" field that never appears in the actual file structure. When readSupervisorPort unmarshals supervisor.json against this struct, the Port field stays zero. This causes resolveBaseURL's priority #2 (read port from supervisor.json) to be a dead code path — the function always falls through to priority #3 (hardcoded :37701). The hardcoded fallback is correct by accident: the claude-mem worker IS listening on :37701. However, this is a latent vulnerability: if the worker port ever changes (e.g., upgrade, config change), glia will silently continue attempting :37701 with no configuration mechanism to update it. Port discovery via supervisor.json is not viable because claude-mem v13.2.0 does not publish the port to this file.

title: readSupervisorPort bug is benign but latent — fallback :37701 happens to match worker's actual port
content: The readSupervisorPort bug is a classic benign latent bug: the code has a broken schema assumption (expecting top-level "port" in supervisor.json), but because the actual claude-mem worker is configured to listen on the same hardcoded fallback port (:37701), the bug is invisible in current operation. The supervisor.json file contains no port information at all — only process metadata. When readSupervisorPort unmarshals this, it silently gets a Port of 0, triggering the hardcoded fallback. This works fine today because the worker IS on :37701, but creates a latent failure mode: if claude-mem ever changes its listening port or the worker runs on a different port, glia will silently continue trying :37701 with no error, causing silent connect failures. The code comment shows the developer verified the implementation live against :37701, suggesting this was intentional discovery-by-experiment rather than by specification. The bug will only surface when the port assumptions change.

title: supervisor.json schema mismatch confirmed — actual file has no port field, struct expects top-level "port"
content: The supervisor.json schema bug is now confirmed with concrete evidence. The supervisorConfig struct expects a top-level "port" field (json:"port,omitempty"), but the actual supervisor.json on this machine contains a completely different structure: a top-level "processes" object with nested process metadata (worker, chroma-mcp, SDK session). The port information is not present in the actual file at all. When json.Unmarshal runs against this structure, it finds no "port" key to match, so the Port field remains at zero. Because readSupervisorPort returns 0 on any unmarshal state (not just error), this zero-value triggers the hardcoded fallback to :37701. The code comment "PROVISIONAL: supervisor.json schema unverified" shows this mismatch was a known risk at implementation time but never resolved.

title: readSupervisorPort() supervisor.json schema mismatch causes silent fallback to :37701
content: The readSupervisorPort() function implements a three-tier fallback for discovering the claude-mem daemon port. Its first attempt reads ~/.claude-mem/supervisor.json and unmarshals it into a supervisorConfig struct. However, the struct definition appears to expect a top-level Port field, while supervisor.json may nest the port under another key (e.g., "processes"). When the JSON structure doesn't match, json.Unmarshal silently leaves Port at its zero value. All errors during reading/unmarshaling are caught and return 0 without logging, following ADR-3's principle that peripheral discovery must never fail construction. A Port value of 0 then triggers the hardcoded fallback to :37701 in resolveBaseURL, causing the supervisor.json to be effectively ignored. This is a schema mismatch bug: the code assumes a flat structure, but the actual supervisor.json has a nested structure.

title: PRD-11 archived with design-deviation correction integrated into canonical specs
content: The static-file-sources (PRD-11) change is formally archived. The archive executor integrated all seven SDD artifacts (explore, proposal, spec, design, tasks, apply-progress, verify-report) from engram, applied the mandatory design-deviation correction (original design falsely claimed zero engine changes; implementation required activeSources() helper in Push and Status), merged delta specs into the project's canonical specs with the correction applied, and wrote a comprehensive archive report documenting shipped scope, merged PRs (47104e5, 6258134), design correction, verification status (all tests green), and residual risks. PRD-11 is complete and closed. Two follow-ups remain documented but out-of-scope: a pre-existing supervisor.json schema mismatch in readSupervisorPort(), and PRD-10 (claude-mem file transport) revival blocked by upstream's missing export surface in v13.2.0.

title: Design assumption corrected — Push engine requires activeSources() for read-only source inclusion
content: PRD-11 (openspec read-only source for SDD artifacts) is complete, merged to main across PR #11 (foundation) and PR #12 (observability), and verified with full test coverage. However, the implementation revealed a critical design assumption was incorrect. Design artifact #806 claimed the sync engine required zero code changes because ErrUnsupported would gate writes at pull time. This overlooked an asymmetry: Push must construct a list of active sources to iterate, and the original construction coupled to cfg.Providers (a configuration-only concept), which excluded read-only sources by architectural design. The fix was minimal but significant: a new activeSources() helper method in Engine that iterates all registered adapters (including read-only sources) for Push and Status operations. Pull remains unchanged—the ErrUnsupported mechanism on FromCanonical correctly prevents write-back of spec_artifact records. All tests pass; the feature is production-ready with correct engine behavior.

title: Status sources block and TUI filters for openspec source merged to main
content: The glia project completed a two-part feature addressing PRD-11 requirements for openspec source handling. PR #11 established the foundation with a read-only openspec artifact source implementation. PR #12 builds on that foundation by adding status sources block capability, TUI filters for interactive filtering, end-to-end tests, and fixing engine ingestion behavior. The feature is now complete and merged into main.

title: PR-B verification complete: PASS verdict, all spec requirements covered, engine fix non-regressive
content: PR-B verification completed and PASSED with clean bill of health. All four tasks (6-9) are implemented and fully covered by tests: Status CLI sources block, TUI filter extension, integration + e2e tests, PRD-10 deferral doc. The engine deviation (activeSources + Status sources block) is correctly implemented and fully non-regressive: (1) activeSources() uses exact "read-only" comparison to distinguish sources from providers; (2) claude-mem's "read-only (write_enabled=false)" is NOT exact match, so it stays in provider pool (confirmed by TestStatusJSON_WriteCapabilityValue); (3) Pull never calls activeSources(), so sources are never exported (TestPull_DoesNotCallWriteNativeOnSource); (4) Providers not double-processed (TestPush_ProviderNotDoubleCountedByActiveSources). All 13 packages pass with race detection enabled. 14 spec requirements mapped to passing tests. Recommend next phase: sdd-archive.

title: Sources regression tests use explicit TDD cycle with critical guard assertions
content: The sources_test.go file implements comprehensive TDD regression testing for the engine fix with explicit guards against the five failure modes that could occur: (1) missing sources entirely (TestActiveSources_ReturnsReadOnlyNotInProviders), (2) mis-classifying providers as sources (TestActiveSources_ProviderThatIsReadOnlyStaysOut using name-exclusion guard and exact WriteCapability comparison), (3) ignoring ProviderFilter (TestActiveSources_ProviderFilterApplied), (4) missing edge case when all adapters are providers (TestActiveSources_NoSourcesWhenAllInProviders), (5) Pull exporting sources (TestPull_DoesNotCallWriteNativeOnSource with writeNativeCalled flag), and (6) double-processing providers (TestPush_ProviderNotDoubleCountedByActiveSources). Test fakes are designed to detect violations: readOnlyFakeAdapter tracks WriteNative calls; sourceFakeAdapter implements realistic round-trip. All guards and double-counting tests pass.

title: Comprehensive test coverage validates PR-B completeness: e2e, integration, and regression tests all pass
content: Test coverage for PR-B is comprehensive: (1) E2E tests verify the user-facing feature end-to-end — glia init/sync on a synthetic openspec fixture successfully ingests spec_artifact records with all types and archived tagging, and status --json properly surfaces sources. (2) Integration tests verify the adapter's round-trip behavior and crucially, TestIntegration_PullLeakageInvariant proves the read-only invariant via byte-identical snapshots: the fixture tree is never written to, confirming FromCanonical is never called on openspec during Pull simulation. (3) Seven dedicated regression tests in sources_test.go validate the engine fix: activeSources() correctly identifies sources, providers are not mis-classified or double-processed, and sources are only ingested in Push, never exported in Pull. All tests pass with race detection enabled.

title: Openspec and TUI tests pass: integration validated, SDD artifact filtering functional
content: Openspec adapter integration fully validated: 49 tests confirm fixture round-tripping, archive directory handling, artifact kind/type mapping, and archived tagging all work. Critical pull-leakage tests pass: TestIntegration_PullLeakageInvariant and TestPullLeakage_OpenspecDirUnchangedAfterFromCanonical confirm openspec never receives write calls, preserving the PR-A read-only invariant. TUI filter cycles extended: spec_artifact is now reachable via [k] cycling; SDD types (proposal, spec, design, tasks) are now reachable via [t] cycling. All new filter tests pass, enabling users to browse openspec planning artifacts in glia status.

title: Sync engine tests pass: activeSources() and source handling regression tests green
content: Engine regression tests confirm the activeSources() fix maintains all PR-A safety invariants: (1) TestActiveSources_ProviderThatIsReadOnlyStaysOut verifies adapters with read-only config return non-exact WriteCapability strings and stay in the provider pool, never mis-bucketed as sources — this protects against claude-mem double-processing; (2) TestPull_DoesNotCallWriteNativeOnSource confirms sources are never exported (Push ingests them, Pull ignores them); (3) TestPush_ProviderNotDoubleCountedByActiveSources confirms activeProviders and activeSources() are mutually exclusive in Push (no adapter processed twice). Status command tests verify the exact string comparison works: openspec returns "read-only" (exact), claude-mem returns "read-only (write_enabled=false)" (distinct), and both are correctly bucketed.

title: Status command tests pass: Sources block implementation verified in CLI and JSON
content: Status command implementation for Task 6 verified complete: Sources block is now rendered in both `glia status` (CLI table) and `glia status --json` (JSON field). New tests confirm the feature works: TestStatusJSON_SourcesKeyPresent and TestStatusJSON_SourcesEntryHasOpenspecFields verify JSON structure; TestStatus_TableHasSourcesBlock and TestStatus_TableNoSourcesBlock verify CLI behavior (visible when sources exist, hidden when none). The Sources block is properly separated from the providers table. All 21 Status tests pass including race detection.

title: PR-B verification: all tests pass, engine fix confirmed correct, no classification regressions
content: PR-B verification complete: all code compiles, all tests pass including race detection, and critical engine distinctions are confirmed correct. The engine fix adds activeSources() to both Push (for ingestion) and Status (for reporting), properly excluding adapters already in cfg.Providers and filtering by WriteCapability=="read-only" exactly. Claudemem read-only-by-config returns "read-only (write_enabled=false)" which is distinct from the source marker "read-only", confirming it stays in provider pool and never mis-classified as a source. E2E tests confirm openspec ingestion works end-to-end with spec_artifact records appearing in status. Status CLI now renders a separate sources block; TUI filter cycles extended for SDD artifact types.

title: Design deviation: sync engine required two fixes, not zero changes
content: The original PR-A design claimed the sync engine required zero changes to support read-only sources like openspec. This assumption was incorrect. The root issue: both Push and Status methods iterate only activeProviders(), which respects cfg.Providers (default [engram, claude-mem]). Since openspec is registered in the adapters map but not in cfg.Providers, it was silently excluded. This required two fixes: (1) Push.activeSources() to explicitly ingest read-only sources not in cfg.Providers, and (2) Status.activeSources() plus the sourceStatus() helper to report them. The design distinction between "provider" and "source" hinges on WriteCapability: adapters returning exact "read-only" are sources (openspec); those returning "read-only (write_enabled=false)" remain providers (claude-mem). The design artifact should note this correction.

title: PRD-10 deferred — claude-mem lacks static file export surface
content: During implementation of static-file-sources, PRD-10 (claude-mem file transport) was investigated and determined to be blocked. The design assumes claude-mem can export data as static files via a `server export` command or an archive directory. Investigation of claude-mem v13.2.0 confirmed neither surface exists: no exports/ or archive/ directories in the user's .claude-mem directory, and inspection of the worker service code found no export route handlers implemented. The file transport requires a source to read; without claude-mem's export surface, PRD-10 cannot proceed. Status marked Deferred with a note to revisit when claude-mem ships a stable export/archive surface. The full PRD design is preserved for future work.

title: Full CI test suite with race detector passes — no race conditions detected

The race detector is a critical tool for catching concurrent access bugs. The openspec-related packages (internal/source/openspec, internal/e2e, internal/sync, cmd tests) all pass cleanly with race detection active, confirming the Status method changes and sourceStatus helper do not introduce threading issues or data races.

title: Extracted sourceStatus helper method to prevent duplication
content: The duplicate logic for building SourceStatus was extracted into a dedicated helper method sourceStatus. Previously, the same adapter probing logic (Health check, ListNative count) was inline in the Status method. With the addition of the activeSources() loop, this logic is now needed in two places. The helper method encapsulates: construct base SourceStatus, probe Health, return early if unhealthy, probe ListNative for artifact count if healthy, return final status. This eliminates duplication and makes Status method more readable.

title: Status method now includes read-only sources not explicitly in cfg.Providers
content: The Status method had a critical gap: it only reported read-only adapters (sources) if they were explicitly listed in cfg.Providers. Adapters like openspec—registered in the adapters map with WriteCapability=="read-only" but NOT in cfg.Providers—were completely ignored. The e2e test failed with "status --json 'sources' must be non-empty array; got: []" because openspec wasn't included. The fix adds an explicit loop after the providers iteration that calls e.activeSources() to collect these implicit read-only sources and includes them in report.Sources. Also refactored the inline SourceStatus building logic into a reusable e.sourceStatus(ctx, a) helper method.

title: Status tests expect Sources field populated with read-only adapters like openspec
content: Code inspection reveals the architecture for handling read-only sources like openspec: activeSources() in engine.go identifies adapters with WriteCapability=="read-only" that aren't in cfg.Providers, returns them sorted and filtered. The cmd-layer status_test.go includes integration tests (TestStatusJSON_SourcesKeyPresent, TestStatusJSON_SourcesEntryHasOpenspecFields) that verify Sources appear in status JSON with openspec entries. However, there is zero test coverage for this Sources behavior in the engine/sync layer itself—no references to Sources, SourceStatus, or related fields in internal/sync/*_test.go. This test gap at the engine level combined with the failing e2e test (empty sources array) suggests the Sources field is either not being populated or passed through the engine's Report struct.

content: The user ran tests to verify an engine fix for openspec ingestion. Unit tests and openspec integration tests both passed, confirming the ingestion code itself works correctly. This indicates a gap in the end-to-end integration flow—sources are being ingested but not properly propagated to or reported by the status command output.

title: Design gap identified and documented: sync engine excludes openspec source from Push loop
content: Design gap discovered and escalated. PR-A implementation left the sync engine partially broken for sources: while buildAdapters() correctly registers openspec as an adapter, the Push() ingestion loop excludes it because activeProviders() only returns adapters in cfg.Providers. The fix requires: (1) new activeSources() method returning read-only adapters outside cfg.Providers, (2) Push() iterates activeProviders()+activeSources() to ingest from both providers and sources, (3) Pull() unchanged (read-only adapters have no WriteNative). Design artifact (sdd/static-file-sources/design) needs correction on the "zero engine changes" claim. This is the ROOT CAUSE of TestE2E_OpenspecSync failure.

title: CRITICAL: internal/sync/config.Config has no Sources field, only Providers
content: The ROOT CAUSE is now clear: the PR-A implementation stopped at the adapter layer. The internal/sync/config.Config type was never extended to include a Sources field. The e2e test writes config.yaml with sources.openspec section, but config.Load() in the sync package doesn't parse it. buildAdapters() receives loadedConfig but has no sources data to work with. The fix requires: (1) extending internal/config (not internal/sync/config) to include Sources field and config structure for openspec, (2) having config.Load() parse sources from YAML, (3) extending buildAdapters() to construct the openspec adapter when sources.openspec.enabled=true.

title: Engine.Status() correctly identifies sources, but Pull/Push iterate over activeProviders() which may not include openspec
content: Engine infrastructure is correct: it has Sources field in StatusReport, filters read-only adapters into Status output, and Pull() iterates all active adapters. The bottleneck is buildAdapters(): if it doesn't construct the openspec adapter from config.Sources, the adapter won't be in e.adapters map and won't be called during Pull(). Must examine: (1) config.Config type for Sources field, (2) config.Load() parsing of "sources:" section in YAML, (3) buildAdapters() wiring logic.

title: buildSyncEngine calls buildAdapters with loadedConfig but no sources wiring visible

Also need to verify config.Config type includes a Sources field and that config.Load() actually parses the "sources:" section from config.yaml.

title: Integrated printSourcesTable call into runStatus output flow
content: Final integration: printSourcesTable call wired into runStatus to automatically render the sources block in human-readable status output. Placed after the conflicts table so the output flows: PROVIDER table, optional conflicts block, optional sources block, then exit code check. Since printSourcesTable returns early for empty sources, the sources block appears only when read-only sources like openspec are configured. This completes the end-to-end wiring for Task 6 source observability.

title: Implemented buildStatusJSON Sources population and printSourcesTable rendering helper
content: Completed the sources observability implementation in status.go. buildStatusJSON now properly populates the Sources field in the statusJSON output, ensuring it's always a non-nil slice for consistent JSON structure (matching the Conflicts pattern). Added printSourcesTable helper function to render sources as a distinct table block in human-readable output. The table shows source name, health status (with error message if degraded), write capability, artifact count from ListNative, and newest artifact timestamp. The function returns early for empty sources list, making the sources block invisible when openspec is disabled. This design delivers the requirement from PRD-11 §10: sources render in a separate block from bidirectional providers, with no meaningless LAST_PUSHED/LAST_PULLED columns.

title: Added Sources field to statusJSON struct for JSON serialization of source data
content: Added Sources field to the statusJSON output struct used by the status --json flag. This field carries the array of SourceStatus entries populated by Engine.Status, allowing the JSON output to include read-only source health and freshness alongside provider health, write capability, and conflicts. The field is always present as an array in JSON (never null), matching the pattern of the Conflicts field which also ensures consistent JSON structure for programmatic consumers like the TUI.

title: Engine.Status method extended to handle source adapters with distinct observability
content: GREEN phase implementation of source observability in Engine.Status. The method now distinguishes read-only adapters (sources like openspec) from bidirectional providers by checking WriteCapability(). Sources bypass the ProviderHealth/ProviderWriteCapability maps and instead create SourceStatus entries carrying health state, artifact count, and (future) freshness data. Each source adapter triggers a Health() check; if healthy, a best-effort ListNative(ctx, "", zeroTime) call populates the ArtifactCount (no project filter, no since filter — list all artifacts to show the full ingest surface). This design allows the status CLI to render sources as a distinct block without attempting to show meaningless LAST_PUSHED timestamps (which only apply to bidirectional providers).

title: Added SourceStatus struct and Sources field to StatusReport for source observability
content: Added the data structure for source observability to internal/sync/report.go. New SourceStatus struct models per-source health and freshness with JSON tags for direct serialization in the --json output. StatusReport gains a Sources field to carry these entries alongside ProviderHealth and Conflicts, creating a distinct observability track for read-only ingest adapters (e.g., openspec). The structure distinguishes pure sources from bidirectional providers: pure sources are those whose WriteCapability is "read-only" and whose Name does not appear in ProviderHealth. This allows status output to render sources in a separate block from the provider health table, as specified in PRD-11 §10.

title: PR 1/2: OpenSpec static artifact source foundation implementation documented
content: PR 1 of a 2-PR stack implements PRD-11 (OpenSpec static artifact source), a read-only source adapter that ingests OpenSpec SDD artifacts into glia's canonical store. The implementation introduces a new `spec_artifact` canonical kind distinct from observations and session summaries, each mapped with provider_id (relative path), title extraction, topic_key resolution (sdd/change/artifact or spec/domain), and mtime timestamps. Archived artifacts are tagged while preserving their topic resolution. Configuration adds a new `sources.openspec` section with enabled flag and customizable path. The adapter is read-only by design: FromCanonical and WriteNative return ErrUnsupported, enforced by an engine-level byte-identity test (sha256 verification) ensuring the openspec tree cannot be modified via Pull(). The foundation includes comprehensive test coverage (880+ lines) across unit, config, wiring, and sync domains. All builds, vetting, and CI tests pass. A fresh review found no critical issues or warnings. PR 2 will add user-facing observability (status line, TUI filters) and e2e fixtures.

title: TUI loadRecords benchmark is hardware-dependent and CI-skipped
content: The TUI's loadRecords benchmark test is intentionally hardware-aware. It skips when running on CI hardware (shared, generic VMs) because the 500ms budget requirement is calibrated specifically for developer-class machines. The test preserves the benchmark locally to catch regressions on capable hardware but acknowledges it cannot be a meaningful signal on CI infrastructure. The failure on the local test run (3.7s vs 500ms) indicates the developer machine does not meet the baseline performance requirement, suggesting either a genuine performance regression in loadRecords() or a mismatch between the local hardware capability and the REQ-TUI-04 specification baseline.

title: TUI performance regression in loadRecords benchmark
content: The TUI package's test suite fails on a performance benchmark that validates loadRecords() can load 100k records within 500ms (REQ-TUI-04). Current performance is 3.7 seconds, representing a 7.4x slowdown against the specification. This is likely the data-loading bottleneck referenced in the earlier TUI directory contract fix, where loadRecords() silently treats missing files as valid empty state. The benchmark test executes under race detection enabled (matching CI), confirming the issue reproduces in the standard test environment. All other package tests pass successfully.

title: TUI module directory contract bug fix in glia repository
content: The glia TUI module had an inconsistent directory contract between path validation and sub-model initialization. The cmd/glia/cmd/tui.go validated that storePath(dir) (i.e., dir/.glia) exists, but then passed the raw project root to tui.New(dir). Sub-models then called loadRecords(m.dir) and loadIndexFile(m.dir), which attempted to read memory.jsonl and index.json directly from m.dir instead of m.dir/.glia. Since these files live in the .glia subdirectory, the reads silently failed—loadRecords treats missing files as valid empty state and returns nil—causing the Observations and Conflicts tabs to display no data despite files existing on disk. The Status tab avoided this bug by explicitly constructing filepath.Join(m.dir, ".glia") when reading and also leveraging subprocess --dir flag re-appending. The fix preserves m.dir as the project root (required for subprocess calls) and introduces a storeSubdir helper function to resolve the .glia subdirectory at all file-read call sites in sub-models, mirroring the Status tab pattern. A regression test confirms the fix works.

The test was added only to document and verify the existing correct behavior, closing a coverage gap. Finding 3 is now complete (test only, no code change needed).

The topic_key and type derivation must skip the archive/ segment, so changes/archive/auth/design.md maps to topic_key sdd/auth/design and type design (same as if the file were at changes/auth/design.md). Non-archived artifacts must keep empty tags. ListNative must include archived artifacts. These tests will fail until openspec.go:236 implements archive detection. This follows strict TDD: RED test, then GREEN implementation.
